question: A 19-year-old man with short stature, hypertelorism, and neck webbing has a loud systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border and a click that is heard better with expiration. Which genetic disorder is most likely responsible?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Down syndrome : More commonly associated with atrioventricular septal defects.
• Option B. Marfan syndrome : Associated with aortic root dilation and other skeletal abnormalities.
• Option D. Turner syndrome : Sex chromosome disorder seen in females, not males.
• Educational Objective:
• Noonan syndrome is commonly associated with congenital cardiac lesions, including pulmonary stenosis.

subject: Medicine
question: Falsely low BNP (B-type natriuretic peptide) values can be seen in which of the following conditions?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Advanced age: BNP levels tend to increase with age, so falsely low values are less likely in older individuals.
• Option C. Female gender: While there may be slight gender differences in BNP levels, it is not a major factor in causing falsely low values.
• Option D. Smoking: Smoking is not known to significantly affect BNP levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Obesity, constrictive pericarditis, cardiac tamponade and rarely flash pulmonary edema may be associated with lower than expected BNP levels.

subject: Medicine
question:
A patient presented with the following rhythm. His BP is 120/80 mm Hg and his pulse rate is 150/min. The following manoeuvre is performed. What is the manoeuvre?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/13/screenshot-2024-08-13-134324.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Standard Valsalva: The standard Valsalva manoeuvre alone is less effective than the REVERT manoeuvre in terminating SVTs.
• Option C. Castell’s manoeuvre: Used fir splenic percussion and not related to cardiology.
• Option D. Modified squat manoeuvre: There is no such manoeuvre.
• Educational Objective:
• REVERT manoeuvre is more effective in terminating paroxysmal SVTs compared to standard Valsalva manoeuvre.

subject: Medicine
question:
A patient with chronic heart failure presents with dyspnea on exertion and fatigue. You ask the patient to do Valsalva and you notice the blood pressure tracing as shown below. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this finding?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/13/screenshot-2024-08-13-134337.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Increased sympathetic nervous system activity: This would typically lead to an exaggerated overshoot response.
• Option C. Increased parasympathetic nervous system activity: This would result in a more pronounced decrease in blood pressure during the strain phase but not necessarily affect the overshoot.
• Option D. Impaired diastolic function: Usually results in a square wave response.
• Educational Objective:
• Understand the normal physiological response to the Valsalva maneuver and recognize the absence of the overshoot response as a potential sign of impaired systolic function in heart failure.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old patient presents with dyspnea, fatigue, and peripheral edema. On examination, you observe the JVP as shown in the video and a pansystolic murmur in the left sternal border. This finding is called as:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Bernheim effect: This refers to the displacement of the interventricular septum towards the left ventricle in patients with right ventricular hypertrophy.
• Option C. Muller's sign: This is the systolic pulsation of the uvula seen in severe aortic regurgitation.
• Option D. Brockenbrough effect: This refers to the paradoxical decrease in pulse pressure in the Post-VPC impulse, seen in HOCM.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize Lancisi's sign as a clinical manifestation of tricuspid regurgitation, characterized by a giant cv wave in the JVP coinciding with ventricular systole.

subject: Medicine
question: A 40-year-old man presents with palpitations. You examine his JVP and you see as what is shown in the video below. His pulse rate is 150/min. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Complete heart block: This would typically cause bradycardia (slow heart rate) and irregular cannon a waves.
• Option B. Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT): SVTs (like AVNRT) tend to produce regular cannon ‘a’ waves.
• Option D. WPW syndrome: WPW can cause SVTs (like AVRT) which sometimes can produce regular cannon ‘a’ waves.
• Educational Objective:
• Irregular cannon ‘a’ waves in the JVP is a hallmark of AV dissociation and it is commonly seen in complete heart block and VT.

subject: Medicine
question: A 60-year-old lady presents with ascites, elevated JVP, and pedal edema. Her JVP is shown in the video. All of the following can be the cause except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Constrictive pericarditis: The rigid pericardium restricts ventricular filling, leading to right heart failure and Kussmaul's sign. Classic cause of Kussmaul’s sign.
• Option B. Restrictive cardiomyopathy: The stiff ventricles in this condition also impair filling, causing right heart failure and Kussmaul's sign.
• Option C. Tricuspid stenosis: This directly obstructs blood flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle, leading to elevated right atrial pressure, JVP elevation, and Kussmaul's sign.
• Educational Objective
• Kussmaul's sign (increased JVP with inspiration) is a specific finding in conditions that impair right ventricular filling, such as constrictive pericarditis, restrictive cardiomyopathy, and tricuspid stenosis.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following can cause paradoxical splitting of the second heart sound except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. LBBB (Left Bundle Branch Block): Delays left ventricular activation and thus aortic valve closure.
• Option C. RV pacing: Delays right ventricular activation and thus pulmonic valve closure, leading to A2 preceding P2 on expiration (similar to paradoxical splitting).
• Option D. HOCM (Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy): Can cause delayed left ventricular emptying and aortic valve closure.
• Educational Objective:
• Classic causes of paradoxical splitting are LBBB, severe AS/HOCM and RV pacing.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old man presents with pounding sensation in the chest for the last 1 hour. On examination you notice the JVP to be elevated and it shown in the video below. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ventricular ectopics: Ventricular ectopics can cause irregular cannon a waves but not regular ones.
• Option C. Tricuspid regurgitation: While tricuspid regurgitation can cause prominent v waves in the JVP, it does not typically cause cannon a waves.
• Option D. Complete heart block: Complete heart block usually causes irregular cannon a waves due to AV dissociation.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize the clinical significance of regular cannon a waves in the JVP and their association with AVNRT.

subject: Medicine
question:
Which of the following is the diagnosis based on the pressure tracing shown in the image.

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/13/screenshot-2024-08-13-134415.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Aortic stenosis: Gradient decreases with Valsalva and post VPC.
• Option C. Mitral regurgitation: Does not exhibit bronckenbrough effect.
• Option D. Aortic regurgitation: Does not exhibit bronckenbrough effect.
• Educational Objective:
• Brockenbrough effect is a hallmark of HOCM.

subject: Medicine
question:
Which of the following is the likely diagnosis based on the pulse waveform shown below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/13/screenshot-2024-08-13-134431.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Ventricular Bigeminy: Associated with pulsus bigeminy.
• Option B. Aortic Regurgitation: Usually associated with bounding and collapsing (water hammer) pulse. Sometimes can produce pulsus bisferians (in severe cases) but does not have the spike and dome appearance like that of HOCM.
• Option D. Aortic Stenosis: Aortic stenosis typically presents with a slow-rising and delayed pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus).
• Educational Objective:
• Pulsus bisferians can be seen in HOCM, AR and sometimes AS + AR but the spike and dome appearance is typical of HOCM.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old woman with a history of recently diagnosed lymphoma presents with dyspnea and diaphoresis. Blood pressure is 70/60 mm Hg, heart rate is 110 beats/min, and her radial pulse becomes difficult to palpate at times, especially during inspiration. You see the monitor and notice the rhythm as shown in the video. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute anterior myocardial infarction: While MI can cause hypotension and tachycardia, it is less likely to cause pulsus paradoxus or electrical alternans.
• Option B. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease exacerbation: COPD exacerbation can cause dyspnea and tachycardia, but not the other findings in this case.
• Option C. Myocardial amyloidosis: Amyloidosis can cause restrictive cardiomyopathy, but it is a less likely cause of acute tamponade in this patient with a recent lymphoma diagnosis.
• Educational Objective
• Classic clinical presentation of cardiac tamponade is hypotension, tachycardia, pulsus paradoxus, and electrical alternans in ECG.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 65-year-old man presents with severe shortness of breath. You are attending him and notice the pulse and ECG tracing as shown in the image below. What is the diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/13/screenshot-2024-08-13-134518.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Cardiac Tamponade: Cardiac tamponade can cause pulsus paradoxus and electrical alternans (not pulsus alternans).
• Option C. Septic shock: Septic shock usually results in a bounding pulse. Sometimes, it is associated with a. dicrotic pulse.
• Option D. Digitalis toxicity: Digitalis toxicity causes pulsus bigeminy and not alternans.
• Educational Objective:
• Recognize pulsus alternans as a clinical sign of severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction and understand its underlying pathophysiology.

subject: Medicine
question: A 62-year-old woman with a history of hypertension, now with a new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus, presents with elevated blood pressure (140/90 mmHg), total cholesterol (250 mg/dL), and hemoglobin A1c (6.6%). Which risk factor confers the highest risk for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) in this patient?
options: Diastolic blood pressure of 90 mm Hg

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B, C, D : A diastolic blood pressure of 90 mm Hg, HDL cholesterol level of 30 mg/dL (0.78 mmol/L) and total cholesterol level of 250 mg/dL (6.47 mmol/L) have a relatively small effect on 10-year ASCVD risk compared with the presence of diabetes.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with diabetes mellitus have a 2-4x increased risk for CV disease, with more than two thirds of patients with diabetes eventually dying of heart disease.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following can produce the murmur depicted in the video except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA): This is a persistent connection between the aorta and pulmonary artery, leading to a continuous left-to-right shunt and a continuous "machinery murmur."
• Option B. Lutemacher syndrome: This is a combination of an atrial septal defect (ASD) and mitral stenosis creating a continuous increase in LA → RA pressure gradient resulting in a continuous murmur.
• Option D. Ruptured sinus of Valsalva aneurysm: This can create a fistula between the aorta and right heart chambers, leading to a continuous shunt and murmur.
• Educational Objective:
• Aortic regurgitation typically results in an early diastolic murmur.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following are causes of a soft first heart sound except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Calcified valves: Calcification of the mitral valve leaflets can make them rigid and less mobile, leading to a softer S1.
• Option B. Mitral regurgitation: In some cases of mitral regurgitation, the mitral valve may not close completely, resulting in a softer S1.
• Option C. Heart failure: Reduced left ventricular contractility in heart failure can lead to less forceful closure of the mitral valve and a softer S1.
• Educational Objective:
• Hyperdynamic states are associated with loud S1.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following can result in mid-diastolic murmur except?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Complete heart block: Can result in a mid-diastolic murmur (called Rytand’s murmur)
• Option B. Aortic regurgitation: Severe aortic regurgitation can cause a mid-diastolic murmur known as the Austin Flint murmur. This occurs due to the regurgitant jet striking the anterior leaflet of the mitral valve, causing it to vibrate and create turbulence.
• Option C. Acute rheumatic fever: Can result in mitral valvulitis resulting in turbulent blood flow and a mid-diastolic murmur (Carey Coomb’s murmur).
• Educational Objective:
• Pulmonary regurgitation is associated with an early diastolic murmur.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old asymptomatic man is found to have a grade II diastolic murmur on routine physical examination. His ECG and chest X-ray are normal. What is the next best step in evaluation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cardiac MRI : Offers excellent visualization but is typically reserved for more complex cases or when echocardiography is inconclusive.
• Option B. Carotid ultrasound : Not relevant for the evaluation of a cardiac murmur.
• Option C. No further workup : A diastolic murmur always requires further investigation.
• Educational Objective:
• A diastolic murmur is always pathological and should be evaluated with echocardiography to assess for underlying structural heart disease.

subject: Medicine
question: Mr. Tamilvanan, a 67-year-old man, presents with symptoms of heart failure. You are assessing his volume status using JVP examination. Which statement is true?
options: The vertical distance between the top of the jugular venous pulsation and the sternal angle accurately measures central venous pressure (CVP).
The angle of inclination is irrelevant when measuring JVP.
Normally, JVP rises with inspiration due to increased right heart filling.
Venous pulsations above the clavicle in the sitting position are abnormal.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . The vertical distance between the top of the JVP and the sternal angle underestimates CVP, and the measurement is affected by body size and patient position.
• Option B. The angle of inclination is important, as it affects the hydrostatic pressure in the jugular veins.
• Option C. Normally, the JVP falls with inspiration due to decreased intrathoracic pressure. A rise in JVP with inspiration (Kussmaul's sign) is seen in constrictive pericarditis.
• Educational Objective:
• Jugular venous pulsations above the clavicle in the sitting position are abnormal and indicative of elevated central venous pressure.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is true regarding blood pressure measurements?
options: SBP increases and DBP decreases when measured in more distal arteries.
SBP in the legs are usually as much as 20 mmHg lower than arm blood pressures.
The difference in blood pressure measured in both arms should be less than 20 mmHg.
Using a BP cuff that is too small will result in a marked underestimation of the true BP.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B . Systolic leg blood pressures are actually higher than arm pressures, not lower.
• Option C . The difference in blood pressure between arms should be less than 10 mmHg, not 20 mmHg.
• Option D . Using a too-small cuff leads to overestimation of blood pressure, not underestimation.
• Educational Objective:
• So many things have to be considered while taking BP recordings!

subject: Medicine
question:
What is the cardiac axis marked by ‘?’ in the image below?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/13/screenshot-2024-08-13-134607.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Right Axis: From +90° to 180 °
• Option B. Northwest Axis: -90 ° to 180 °
• Option C. Normal Axis: -30 ° to 90 ° (or 100 ° )
• Educational Objective
• Axis determination is important in exams!

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following diseases are associated with the development of cardiac conduction disease EXCEPT:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Lyme disease: Cardiac involvement in Lyme disease can lead to AV block.
• Option C. Syphilis: This can lead to aortitis and subsequent damage to the conducting system.
• Option D. Systemic lupus erythematosus: This autoimmune disease can cause inflammation in the heart, including the conduction system.
• Educational Objective:
• Hypothyroidism (not hyperthyroidism) causes conduction system abnormalities. Hyperthyroidism is associated with sinus tachycardia and AF.

subject: Medicine
question: Ms. Anitha, a 24-year-old woman, presents with postural light-headedness and palpitations after a recent upper respiratory illness. Upon standing for 2 minutes, her heart rate increases to 135 beats/min with no change in blood pressure. Which of the following is a reasonable therapeutic option for her?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fluid restriction: This would worsen the hypovolemia and exacerbate the symptoms of POTS.
• Option B. Upright Yoga: While exercise can be beneficial in the long term, it may initially worsen symptoms in POTS patients due to increased demands on the cardiovascular system.
• Option C. Furosemide: This is a diuretic that would further decrease blood volume and worsen symptoms.
• Educational Objective:
• POTS is characterized by symptomatic sinus tachycardia that occurs with postural change from a supine position to standing. The sinus rate increases by 30 beats/min or to >120 beats/min within 10 minutes of standing and in the absence of hypotension.

subject: Medicine
question: After a common heart transplant operation, a portion of which donor heart chamber will remain in the donor?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The most common contemporary cardiac transplant operation is referred to as a “bicaval” orthotopic cardiac transplant that mimics the natural anatomic position. In this operation, the donor and recipient SVC and IVC are connected as are the aortic and pulmonary great vessels. The left atrium of the recipient retains its roof, including the draining pulmonary veins, and the donor left atrium is then sutured to the retained atrial tissue. This technique maintains function of the donor right atrium, which is important for governing early postoperative right heart output, and may prevent atrial arrhythmias.
• Educational Objective:
• In a standard heart transplantation procedure, the left atrium of the recipient retains its roof, including the draining pulmonary veins, and the donor left atrium is then sutured to the retained atrial tissue.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT an early diastolic sound?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. S3: Early diastolic sound.
• Option C. OS: Early diastolic sound.
• Option D. Pericardial knock: Early diastolic sound.
• Educational Objective:
• S3, OS, pericardial knock and tumour plop (seen in atrial myxoma) are early diastolic sounds, while S4 is a late diastolic sound.

subject: Medicine
question: Mr. Farooq, a 45-year-old man, presents with progressive dyspnea. PFT and CT chest are normal. Echo shows features of pulmonary hypertension and his LVEF is 65%. His cardiac catheterization reports are shown. RA pressure- 10 mmHg PA pressure - 55/35 (mean = 42) mmHg PCWP - 34 mmHg LVEDP - 34 mmHg Cardiac output - 4.5 L/min PVR - 1.8 Wood unit What is the most likely cause of his pulmonary hypertension?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . Chronic lung disease : Normal PFT and CT chest make chronic lung disease unlikely.
• Option B. Mitral stenosis : Elevated LVEDP is unlikely in mitral stenosis. Also, mitral stenosis patients have a gradient between LVEDP and PCWP. Identical LVEDP and PCWP rules out mitral stenosis.
• Option D. Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension: The elevated PCWP rules out idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension.
• Educational Objective
• In the presence of elevated PCWP equal to LVEDP and normal LV systolic function, consider HFpEF as the cause of pulmonary hypertension.

subject: Medicine
question: You are assessing a patient with an aortic aneurysm. You observe hypertelorism, a bifid uvula, and "corkscrew" carotids on imaging. You suspect Loeys-Dietz syndrome. Which protein's gene mutation is responsible for the syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator : Mutations in this gene cause cystic fibrosis.
• Option B. Fibrillin-1 : Mutations in this gene cause Marfan syndrome.
• Option C. STK11 gene : Mutations in this gene cause Peutz-Jeghers syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Loeys-Dietz syndrome is caused by mutations in the genes encoding TGF-βr.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following rheumatologic diseases is associated with a risk for aortic aneurysms?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Various rheumatologic diseases have increased risk of aortic aneurysms.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following are appropriate therapeutic steps for varicose veins EXCEPT:
options: Frequent elevation of the lower extremities.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Avoid prolonged standing: Prolonged standing increases pressure in the veins of the legs, worsening varicose veins.
• Option C. Frequent elevation of the lower extremities: Elevating the legs helps reduce pressure in the veins and improves blood flow back to the heart.
• Option D. Graduated compression stockings: These stockings apply pressure to the legs, helping to improve blood flow and reduce swelling.
• Educational Objective
• Aspirin is not a standard treatment for varicose veins.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following are reasonable therapeutic options for chronic lymphedema EXCEPT:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Frequent leg elevation: Elevating the affected limb helps reduce swelling by promoting lymphatic drainage.
• Option C. Intermittent pneumatic compression devices: These devices use air pressure to massage the limb and promote lymphatic flow.
• Option D. Liposuction: In some cases, liposuction can be used to remove excess fat and reduce the size of the affected limb. Liposuction may be particularly useful in post-mastectomy lymphedema.
• Educational Objective:
• Diuretic therapy is ineffective and contraindicated in lymphedema.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the statements regarding chronic venous disease is true?
options: More men than women suffer from varicose veins.
More men than women suffer from chronic venous insufficiency with edema.
More than 50% of patients over 70 years old have chronic venous insufficiency.
Most patients with chronic venous insufficiency will develop venous ulcers.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. More men than women suffer from varicose veins: This is incorrect; varicose veins are more common in women.
• Option C. More than 50% of patients over 70 years old have chronic venous insufficiency: The prevalence in this age group is closer to 10%.
• Option D. Most patients with chronic venous insufficiency will develop venous ulcers: Only about 20% of patients with chronic venous insufficiency develop venous ulcers.
• Educational Objective
• Varicose veins are more common in women than in men.

subject: Medicine
question: In a patient with bacterial endocarditis, which of the following echocardiographic lesions is most likely to lead to embolization?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Although any valvular vegetation can embolize, vegetations located on the mitral valve and vegetations >10 mm are at greatest risk of embolizing. Of the choices, options B, C, and D are large enough to increase the risk of embolization.
• Hematogenously seeded infection from an embolized vegetation may involve any organ, but particularly affects those organs with the highest blood flow. They are seen in up to 50% of patients with endocarditis. Tricuspid lesions will lead to pulmonary septic emboli, which is common in injection drug users. Mitral and aortic lesions can lead to embolic infections in the skin, spleen, kidneys, meninges, and skeletal system. A dreaded neurologic complication is mycotic aneurysm, focal dilations of arteries at points in the arterial wall that have been weakened by infection in the vasa vasorum or septic emboli, leading to hemorrhage.
• Mitral vegetations >10 mm are at greatest risk of embolization

subject: Medicine
question: A 75-year-old man with a history of bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement presents with intermittent fever and dyspnea 3 years after the procedure. TTE is normal. What is the most appropriate diagnostic test?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cardiac CT : Less sensitive than TEE for detecting vegetations.
• Option B. Cardiac MRI : Not the first-line imaging modality for PVE.
• Option D. No further testing : Inappropriate given the suspicion for PVE.
• Educational Objective
• In patients with suspected infective endocarditis, transthoracic echocardiography is recommended as the initial imaging choice in most clinical situations.
• TEE is the preferred imaging modality to evaluate for PVE when TTE is inconclusive or if complications have developed or are clinically suspected or if intracardiac device leads are present, transesophageal echocardiography is recommended.

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old man is evaluated for recurrent pericarditis. He has had two prior episodes, both successfully treated with ibuprofen and colchicine. His current symptoms include fever, pulsus paradoxus, a pericardial friction rub, and widespread ST-segment elevation on ECG. Echocardiogram shows a small pericardial effusion without tamponade. What is the most appropriate treatment?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anakinra : This is an interleukin-1 receptor antagonist used for refractory cases of recurrent pericarditis that have not responded to other treatments.
• Option B. Colchicine and IVIG : This combination is also used for refractory cases, but glucocorticoids should be added first.
• Option C. Ibuprofen alone : Unlikely to be effective for recurrent pericarditis, especially since it has been used previously.
• Educational Objective
• In patients with recurrent pericarditis initially treated with both colchicine and an NSAID, the addition of a glucocorticoid should be considered.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old man had an atrial septal defect (ASD) closure one year ago. He is now scheduled for tooth extraction and periodontal cleaning. The dentist recommended a nil per oral (NPO) 8 hours prior to the procedure. What is the choice for IE prophylaxis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• A), B), C): These are antibiotics used for endocarditis prophylaxis but are not indicated in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Endocarditis prophylaxis is not routinely recommended for dental procedures in patients with repaired ASDs unless there are specific high-risk features.

subject: Medicine
question: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, weight loss, and pulsus paradoxus. Physical exam reveals jugular venous distention, distant heart sounds, and crackles. Chest X-ray shows a large right upper lobe mass and an enlarged cardiac silhouette. What is the next test?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. CT-guided needle biopsy : May be needed later to assess the lung mass, but not the immediate priority.
• Option B. Fiberoptic bronchoscopy : May be useful for evaluating the lung mass, but not the priority in this unstable patient.
• Option C. PET/CT : May be useful for staging malignancy, but not the immediate priority.
• Educational Objective
• Findings suggesting cardiac tamponade include pulsus paradoxus, tachycardia, distant heart sounds, and occasionally an enlarged silhouette on CXR.
• Echocardiography is an essential tool in the diagnosis of cardiac tamponade because it defines the presence, distribution, and relative volume of pericardial fluid.

subject: Medicine
question:
A patient evaluated for cough is found to have an enlarged cardiac silhouette on chest radiography. He is suspected of having pericardial effusion. Ultrasonography is performed with the probe placed in the subxiphoid area. The results are shown. Which of the following is the most likely interpretation of this study?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/WhatsApp%20Image%202024-12-14%20at%2009.53.39.jpeg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. No pericardial effusion: This option is incorrect because the ultrasound clearly demonstrates the presence of a significant anechoic space around the heart, indicating a pericardial effusion.
• Option C. LV dilatation: Left ventricular (LV) dilatation is not indicated by the findings described in this scenario. The primary abnormality is the pericardial effusion, not an intrinsic abnormality of the left ventricle.
• Option D. Indeterminate study : This option is incorrect because the ultrasound findings are definitive for a large pericardial effusion. There is no indication that the study is inconclusive or indeterminate.
• Educational Objective:
• A large pericardial effusion is characterized by the accumulation of fluid around the heart, visible as an anechoic space on ultrasound. It can lead to cardiac tamponade, a life-threatening condition that requires prompt recognition and management.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 35-year-old woman with SLE presents with progressive dyspnea, fatigue, chest fullness, and bilateral peripheral edema. She is febrile and her JVP is elevated. ECG is shown. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-12-124716.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Constrictive pericarditis : Although it can present with similar symptoms, it usually has a more insidious onset and does not typically cause electrical alternans on ECG.
• Option C. Pulmonary embolism : Can cause dyspnea and elevated JVP, but it would not typically present with electrical alternans.
• Option D. Pulmonary hypertension : Less likely in the absence of lung disease or right heart failure.
• Educational Objective:
• Electrical alternans is a hallmark of cardiac tamponade.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 30-year-old man is evaluated for a 2-week history of fever and malaise. Nail findings show splinter hemorrhages. Which of the following is the next appropriate step?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-150655.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Urine cultures: While urine cultures may be helpful in identifying a urinary tract infection, they are not the primary diagnostic test for suspected IE.
• Option C. CT scan of the chest: A CT scan might be helpful in later stages to assess for complications of IE, such as embolic events, but blood cultures are the first step.
• Option D. CSF analysis: CSF analysis is used to diagnose infections of the central nervous system, not IE.
• Educational Objective
• Splinter hemorrhages in the setting of fever and malaise raise the suspicion of infective endocarditis.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 50-year-old man is evaluated for a 2-day history of fever, malaise, and heart murmur. Today, crops of nonpainful cutaneous lesions have appeared on his feet as shown. Given the diagnosis, the patient is at risk of all of the following except?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184301.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Intracranial hemorrhage: Septic emboli from IE can lodge in the brain, causing intracranial hemorrhage.
• Option B. Stroke: Septic emboli can also cause ischemic stroke.
• Option C. Heart failure: Valve damage from IE can lead to heart failure.
• Educational Objective
• Aortic complications are uncommon in endocarditis.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 60-year-old man is evaluated for chronic right-sided heart failure. Chest radiographs are shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184320.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension: This condition involves pulmonary artery obstruction due to organized clots, leading to increased pulmonary artery pressure and right-sided heart failure. However, it would not typically present with pericardial calcification on chest radiographs.
• Option C. Primary hyperparathyroidism: While primary hyperparathyroidism can cause hypercalcemia and possibly lead to calcifications in various tissues, it is not typically associated with pericardial calcification or the symptoms of chronic right-sided heart failure.
• Option D. Restrictive cardiomyopathy : Restrictive cardiomyopathy causes diastolic dysfunction due to myocardial stiffness but does not involve pericardial calcification. The imaging findings and clinical presentation are more consistent with constrictive pericarditis.
• Educational Objective:
• Constrictive pericarditis is characterized by pericardial thickening and calcification that leads to impaired cardiac filling and chronic right-sided heart failure. Radiographic evidence of pericardial calcification is a key diagnostic clue.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 45-year-old man is evaluated for a 2-week history of subjective fever and malaise. A grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur that increases with inspiration is present at the left mid-sternal border. A new, painful skin lesion is shown. Which of the following is FALSE?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184332.jpg]
options: They are associated with infective endocarditis.
They are caused by immune complex deposition.
They can be found on the fingertips and toes.

explanation.short: Ans. B) They are typically painless.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. They are associated with infective endocarditis : This is true. Osler's nodes are a cutaneous manifestation of infective endocarditis.
• Option C. They are caused by immune complex deposition: This is true. The pathogenesis of Osler's nodes involves the deposition of immune complexes in the skin, leading to inflammation and the formation of the nodules.
• Option D. They can be found on the fingertips and toes: This is true. Osler's nodes are most commonly found on the pads of the fingers and toes, but they can also occur on other parts of the hands and feet.
• Educational Objective
• Osler's nodes as a painful, cutaneous manifestation of infective endocarditis caused by immune complex deposition.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 77-year-old man is evaluated for a 10-day history of fever and fatigue. Two years ago, he underwent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation. He reports tenderness at the site of his pacemaker, as shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184347.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma typically presents as a persistent, non-healing ulcer or nodule with a rough or scaly surface. It would not be associated with the systemic symptoms (fever, fatigue) described here, nor with tenderness at the site of a previously implanted device.
• Option C. Pyoderma gangrenosum : Pyoderma gangrenosum is a rare inflammatory condition that typically presents with painful, rapidly progressing ulcers with a purulent base and violaceous borders. It is associated with systemic diseases like inflammatory bowel disease and rheumatoid arthritis, not with pacemaker implantation.
• Option D. Pyogenic granuloma : Pyogenic granuloma is a benign vascular lesion that typically appears as a small, red, rapidly growing nodule or mass, often after minor trauma. It would not typically present with systemic symptoms like fever or tenderness at a pacemaker site.
• Educational Objective:
• A device-associated infection can occur after implantation of devices like pacemakers and may present with systemic symptoms such as fever, along with local signs like tenderness, erythema, and even erosion of the device through the skin.

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old man presents with edema and ascites. On examination, he has elevated JVP with no respiratory variation. His BP is 110/70 mmHg and decreases to 80/60 mmHg with inspiration. 2D echo revealed a large pericardial effusion. Pericardiocentesis was performed, but intracardiac pressures remain elevated. What is the diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Constrictive pericarditis: This would present with elevated intracardiac pressures, but there wouldn't be a significant pericardial effusion.
• Option B. Cardiac tamponade: This would also present with elevated intracardiac pressures, but these pressures would normalize after pericardiocentesis.
• Option D. Dilated cardiomyopathy: This presents with a dilated left ventricle and reduced ejection fraction, and typically does not have elevated intracardiac pressures or pericardial effusion.
• Educational Objective
• Effusive-constrictive pericarditis is a combination of pericardial effusion and constrictive pericarditis, characterized by persistently elevated intracardiac pressures even after drainage of the effusion.

subject: Medicine
question: A 58-year-old man with chronic kidney disease shortness of breath and diaphoresis. BP is 90/60 mm Hg and it decreases to 70/50 during inspiration. On examination, his JVP is elevated, has pitting pedal edema and heart sounds were faint. What is the next management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hemodialysis : May worsen the situation by reducing the right sided filling pressures without affecting the increased intra-pericardiac pressure in tamponade.
• Option B. Intravenous nitroglycerin : May reduce right heart filling and may worsen hypotension.
• Option D. Right heart catheterization : May be useful for diagnosis but does not treat tamponade.
• Educational Objective
• Definitive treatment of cardiac tamponade is pericardiocentesis or surgical pericardial drainage.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 43-year-old patient presents with edema and elevated jugular venous pressure (JVP). The JVP pressure tracing is shown below. What is the diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184409.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cardiac tamponade: In cardiac tamponade, the x descent is typically absent or minimal, and the y descent is blunted or slow.
• Option C. Tricuspid regurgitation: This would cause a prominent v wave in the JVP tracing, not a prominent x descent.
• Option D. Tricuspid stenosis: Tricuspid stenosis would cause a slow y descent, but the x descent would be normal or blunted.
• Educational Objective
• Prominent x and y descent is characteristic of constrictive pericarditis. The prominent ‘y’ descent of constrictive pericarditis is also called as ‘friedrich sign’.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 42-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with acute chest pain, elevated CRP, and normal troponin levels. ECG is shown. Echocardiogram reveals a small pericardial effusion. What is the most appropriate management?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184426.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Echocardiographic-guided pericardiocentesis : Indicated for large effusions or tamponade, neither of which is present in this patient.
• Option B. Emergent cardiac catheterization : Not necessary as the ECG findings are not consistent with ST-elevation myocardial infarction, and troponin levels are normal.
• Option C. Exercise treadmill stress testing : Contraindicated in acute pericarditis due to the risk of exacerbating inflammation.
• Educational Objective
• In patients with acute pericarditis, first-line treatment is high-dose aspirin or NSAIDs and adjuvant colchicine therapy.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old man presents with pleuritic chest pain, fever, and malaise two weeks after a myocardial infarction (MI). On auscultation, you hear a multiphasic rub. His CRP levels are elevated, and troponin levels are slightly elevated but unchanged compared to previous values. ECG shows new ST elevations. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute pericarditis: Less likely given the recent MI.
• Option C. Pulmonary embolism: Unlikely with unchanged troponin and ECG findings.
• Option D. Recurrent MI: Unlikely with unchanged troponin and ECG findings suggestive of pericarditis.
• Educational Objective
• Dressler's syndrome is a post-MI pericarditis that occurs after 1-2 weeks.

subject: Medicine
question: A 38-year-old homeless man presents with TIA, fever, chills, tender splenomegaly, new painful nodules on fingers, and nits on clothes. Blood cultures are negative, but echo reveals aortic valve vegetation. What test should be sent to confirm the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Epstein-Barr virus heterophile antibody: EBV does not typically cause endocarditis.
• Option C. HIV polymerase chain reaction: HIV does not cause endocarditis.
• Option D. Q fever serology: While Q fever can cause culture-negative endocarditis, the patient's history and presentation are more consistent with Bartonella infection.
• Educational Objective
• Two most common pathogens responsible for culture negative endocarditis re

subject: Medicine
question:
A 19-year-old man with substance use disorder is evaluated for 3 weeks of fever and malaise and a new heart murmur. Funduscopic findings are shown. Which of the following about the finding is FALSE?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/12/screenshot-2024-08-10-184442.jpg]
options: They are retinal hemorrhages with pale centers.
They are NOT pathognomonic for infective endocarditis.
They are due to septic emboli to the retina.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. They are retinal hemorrhages with pale centers: This is the classic description of Roth spots. The pale center is thought to represent fibrin or inflammatory cells.
• Option B. They are not pathognomonic for infective endocarditis: While Roth spots are strongly associated with infective endocarditis, they can also occur in other conditions like leukemia, HIV, anemia, diabetic/hypertensive retinopathy and vasculitis.
• Option C. They are usually asymptomatic: Roth spots themselves do not usually cause any sympotms.
• Educational Objective
• Roth spots are examples of immunological manifestations of endocarditis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old African American male presents with nephrotic syndrome and progressive renal failure. Renal biopsy reveals focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). Which of the following genetic polymorphisms is most likely associated with his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. & B. NPHS1 and NPHS2: Mutations are associated with congenital nephrotic syndrome, not FSGS in adults.
• Option C. TRPC6 mutations: Can cause FSGS, but they are less common than APOL1 variants in African Americans.
• Educational Objective:
• APOL1 gene variants are a significant risk factor for focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) and other forms of non-diabetic kidney disease in individuals of African descent.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Minimal change disease: This is a common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children, but less frequent in adults.
• Option B. Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS): FSGS can cause nephrotic syndrome, but it's less common than membranous nephropathy in adults.
• Option D. Diabetic nephropathy: This is a leading cause of chronic kidney disease, but it typically presents with proteinuria in the absence of the full-blown nephrotic syndrome (edema, hyperlipidemia, hypoalbuminemia).
• Educational Objective:
• Membranous nephropathy is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults, especially in Caucasian populations.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 50-year-old obese woman with controlled hypertension has nephrotic-range proteinuria and mild edema. Renal biopsy reveals segmental scarring in 60% of glomeruli, mainly at the corticomedullary junction. Which diagnosis is most likely?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/17/screenshot-2024-08-17-163558.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Hypertensive nephrosclerosis: While the patient has hypertension, the biopsy findings do not fit the typical pattern of nephrosclerosis (arteriolosclerosis and global glomerulosclerosis).
• Option C. Minimal change disease: This would usually present with less severe proteinuria and no glomerular scarring on biopsy.
• Option D. Crescentic glomerulonephritis: This is a rapidly progressive form of glomerulonephritis with crescents (proliferations of cells) on biopsy, not present in this case.
• Educational Objective:
• Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is characterized by segmental scarring in a subset of glomeruli, often associated with obesity.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT a typical feature of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options A, B & C : These are classic features of PSGN.
• Educational Objective:
• Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) typically presents with hematuria, proteinuria and hypertension, but not hypokalemia. The usual timeframe for PSGN development after impetigo is 2-6 weeks.

subject: Medicine
question: A 5-year-old boy presents with hematuria and edema, three weeks after a confirmed case of impetigo. Which of the following is the most appropriate time frame for the development of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) following impetigo?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 1-3 weeks: This time frame is too short for PSGN to develop after a streptococcal infection like impetigo.
• Option C. 3-8 weeks: While PSGN can occasionally present slightly later, the most typical window is within the first 2-6 weeks.
• Option D. 2-6 days: This time frame is far too short. PSGN takes weeks, not days, to manifest.
• Educational Objective:
• Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) is an immune-mediated complication that can occur after a streptococcal infection. The classic presentation includes hematuria (blood in the urine), edema (swelling) and hypertension. The most common time frame for the development of PSGN following a streptococcal infection is 2-6 weeks . Recognizing this typical time frame is crucial for timely diagnosis and management of PSGN.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) presents with proteinuria, edema and a normal serum creatinine. Renal biopsy reveals Thickened basement membranes with diffuse sub-epithelial immune deposit . Which of the following complications is she at increased risk of developing?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Acute kidney injury: While possible in SLE, it's less common with class V lupus nephritis, which typically has a more insidious onset.
• Option C. Nephritic syndrome: This is characterized by hematuria and hypertension, which are not prominent features of class V lupus nephritis.
• Option D. Hypocomplementemia: While common in other classes of lupus nephritis, it's not always present in class V, which is often associated with normal complement levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Lupus nephritis class V (membranous lupus nephritis) carries an increased risk of renal vein thrombosis.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 28-year-old man presents with hemoptysis (coughing up blood), shortness of breath and hematuria. His serum creatinine is elevated. A renal biopsy is taken and immunofluorescence is shown below. Given the likely diagnosis, what would be the immunofluorescence findings?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/17/screenshot-2024-08-17-164723_IdSR2Ar.jpg]
options: Linear IgG deposits along the glomerular basement membrane

explanation.short: Ans. B) Linear IgG deposits along the glomerular basement membrane
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Mesangial cell proliferation: Is seen in IgA nephropathy and other conditions.
• Option C. Subepithelial immune complex deposits: Are characteristic of membranous nephropathy.
• Option D. Mesangial IgA deposits: Are seen in IgA nephropathy.
• Educational Objective:
• Linear IgG deposits along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence are characteristic of Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease).

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following demographic groups is MOST likely to be affected by IgA nephropathy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Elderly women: IgA nephropathy is less common in elderly individuals and slightly more common in men than women.
• Option C. Children under 5 years old: While IgA nephropathy can occur in children, it is more typically diagnosed in adolescents and young adults.
• Option D. African American individuals: IgA nephropathy is less prevalent in individuals of African descent compared to those of Caucasian or Asian descent.
• Educational Objective:
• IgA nephropathy, also known as Berger's disease, primarily affects young men, particularly those in their teens to late 30s. While it can affect individuals of any age, gender or race, certain demographic factors increase the risk of developing this condition.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT a typical feature of ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Palpable purpura: Is a common skin manifestation of AAV.
• Option B. Mononeuritis multiplex: (asymmetric peripheral neuropathy) is also a classic finding.
• Option C: Hematuria and proteinuria: Are common due to glomerulonephritis
• Educational Objective:
• ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV) does not typically present with hypocomplementemia.

subject: Medicine
question: What percentage of Alport syndrome cases are inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. 5%: This is the approximate percentage for the less common autosomal dominant form.
• Option C. 50%: This is the probability of inheritance for each child if one parent has the X-linked dominant form.
• Option D. 85%: This is the approximate percentage for the most common X-linked dominant form.
• Educational Objective:
• Alport syndrome is a genetic disorder primarily affecting the kidneys, hearing, and vision. While most cases (around 80-85%) are inherited in an X-linked pattern, approximately 15% of cases are inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern. This means that both parents must carry a mutated gene for their child to be affected

subject: Medicine
question: A 10-year-old boy presents with recurrent hematuria and a family history of kidney disease, on further questioning he has difficulty in hearing since childhood. A renal biopsy reveals thin glomerular basement membranes (GBMs) on electron microscopy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . IgA nephropathy: Presents with mesangial IgA deposits.
• Option B. Thin basement membrane nephropathy ( TBMN) : This is a benign condition with persistent thin GBM, but no progression to multilamellation or other Alport features.
• Option D. Minimal change disease ( MCD): Does not cause thinning of the GBM.
• Educational objective:
• Alport syndrome is primarily inherited in an X-linked dominant pattern (approximately 85%), but 15% of cases follow an autosomal recessive pattern.
• Thin glomerular basement membranes (GBMs) on electron microscopy, combined with recurrent hematuria and a family history of kidney disease and hearing loss, are suggestive of Alport syndrome.

subject: Medicine
question: A 12-year-old boy presents with asymptomatic microscopic hematuria and a family history of similar findings. Renal function is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alport syndrome: Usually has proteinuria and progressive kidney disease.
• Option B. IgA nephropathy: Presents with episodic gross hematuria, often after infections.
• Option D. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN): Occurs after streptococcal infection, with edema and hypertension
• Educational Objective:
• Benign familial hematuria (thin basement membrane nephropathy) is the most likely diagnosis in a child with asymptomatic microscopic hematuria and a positive family history. It is a benign condition with normal renal function.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with nephrotic syndrome is at increased risk of developing which of the following complications?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Thromboembolism: Nephrotic syndrome leads to the loss of proteins involved in blood clotting, increasing the risk of blood clots.
• Option B. Infection: Loss of immunoglobulins (antibodies) in the urine makes individuals more susceptible to infections.
• Option C. Acute kidney injury: The underlying kidney disease causing nephrotic syndrome can worsen, leading to acute kidney injury
• Educational Objective:
• Nephrotic syndrome increases the risk of blood clots, infections, and kidney failure.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT a typical feature of nephrotic syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Proteinuria > 3.5 g/day: This is a hallmark of nephrotic syndrome. The excessive loss of protein in the urine is a defining characteristic.
• Option B. Hypoalbuminemia: This occurs due to the significant loss of albumin (a protein) in the urine, leading to low albumin levels in the blood.
• Option C. Hyperlipidemia: The liver tries to compensate for the protein loss by increasing the production of lipids (fats), leading to high levels of cholesterol and triglycerides in the blood.
• Educational Objective:
• Nephrotic syndrome is characterized by a constellation of findings, including heavy proteinuria, low albumin levels, and elevated lipids. Hyperkalemia (high potassium) is not typically associated with nephrotic syndrome; in fact, hypokalemia (low potassium) is more common due to urinary losses.

subject: Medicine
question: A 6-year-old boy presents with generalized edema, massive proteinuria, and hypoalbuminemia. Given the likely diagnosis, what would be electron microscopic findings would be present in this patient?
options: Thinning of the glomerular basement membrane

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sub-endothelial immune complex deposits: This is typically seen in lupus nephritis and other immune-complex mediated glomerulonephritis.
• Option B. Mesangial electron-dense deposits: This is characteristic of IgA nephropathy.
• Option C. Thinning of the glomerular basement membrane: This is seen in thin basement membrane nephropathy, a benign condition with minimal proteinuria.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical presentation strongly suggests minimal change disease (MCD) , which is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. The hallmark electron microscopic finding in MCD is the effacement (flattening or fusion) of podocyte foot processes . These foot processes are crucial for the filtration barrier in the glomerulus and their damage leads to the massive proteinuria seen in nephrotic syndrome.

subject: Medicine
question: In children diagnosed with minimal change disease (MCD), what percentage is expected to achieve spontaneous remission without specific treatment?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. 30%, Option C. 50% & Option D. 75%: These percentages are too high. While spontaneous remission can happen in MCD, it's not the norm. Most children require treatment with corticosteroids to achieve remission.
• Educational Objective:
• Minimal change disease (MCD) is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. The hallmark of MCD on electron microscopy is the effacement of podocyte foot processes. Spontaneous remission occurs in a minority of children with MCD (approximately 10%), while the majority respond well to corticosteroid therapy.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old obese man with a history of hypertension presents with proteinuria, edema and hypoalbuminemia. Renal biopsy reveals focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). Which of the following would be the LEAST appropriate initial treatment?
options: Weight loss and blood pressure control

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Options A, B & D: These are all appropriate initial interventions for secondary FSGS.
• Educational Objective:
• Secondary FSGS, often due to obesity and hypertension, usually doesn't respond to corticosteroids. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying cause and using renoprotective agents (ACEi/ARBs).

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with obesity-related FSGS is started on a weight loss program and an ACE inhibitor. After 6 months, their proteinuria has significantly decreased. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue current therapy and monitor closely.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Add a corticosteroid: Corticosteroids are typically used for other forms of FSGS, not primarily obesity-related. Adding them unnecessarily could expose the patient to potential side effects without a clear benefit.
• Option C. Consider renal biopsy: A renal biopsy is typically performed at the initial diagnosis to confirm FSGS and exclude other causes. If the patient is responding well to therapy, a repeat biopsy is usually not indicated.
• Option D. Initiate dialysis: Dialysis is indicated in cases of end-stage renal disease (ESRD) when the kidneys are no longer functioning adequately. This patient is responding to therapy and does not show signs of ESRD.
• Educational Objective:
• If a patient with obesity-related FSGS responds well to initial therapy (weight loss and ACEi/ARB), continued monitoring is usually sufficient. Further intervention isn't immediately necessary.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is associated with “tram-track” appearance of renal biopsy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. MPGN type II: Shows dense deposits within the GBM, not the tram-track appearance.
• Option C. MPGN type III: Is similar to type I but with less prominent mesangial interposition.
• Option D. Alport syndrome: Shows splitting and lamellation of the GBM.
• Educational Objective:
• The "tram-track" appearance on renal biopsy is a hallmark of MPGN type I due to the characteristic mesangial interposition. This distinctive finding helps differentiate MPGN type I from other glomerular diseases.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old patient is newly diagnosed with idiopathic membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis (MPGN) type I. Which of the following statements regarding their prognosis is MOST accurate?
options: The majority of patients will experience spontaneous remission within 5 years.
Renal function typically remains stable for at least 20 years after diagnosis.
Approximately 50% of patients will develop end-stage renal disease (ESRD) within 10 years.
Early treatment with corticosteroids can prevent progression to ESRD in most cases.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The majority of patients will experience spontaneous remission within 5 years :  Spontaneous remission is rare in MPGN type I.
• Option B. Renal function typically remains stable for at least 20 years after diagnosis: Renal function typically declines over time, with 90% of patients developing renal insufficiency within 20 years.
• Option D. Early treatment with corticosteroids can prevent progression to ESRD in most cases: While corticosteroids may be beneficial in some cases, they do not guarantee prevention of ESRD.
• Educational Objective:
• MPGN type I is a progressive disease with a poor prognosis. Approximately half of the patients progress to ESRD within 10 years, emphasizing the need for early diagnosis and management to potentially slow disease progression. While treatments like corticosteroids may be used, they do not guarantee prevention of ESRD.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements about the risk of urothelial malignancy in patients with analgesic nephropathy-related ESRD is TRUE?
options: The risk is lower than in patients with other causes of ESRD.
The risk is the same as in patients with other causes of ESRD.
The risk is higher than in patients with other causes of ESRD.
There is no association between analgesic nephropathy and urothelial malignancy.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. The risk is lower than in patients with other causes of ESRD: This is incorrect. The association between analgesic nephropathy and urothelial malignancy increases the risk compared to other ESRD causes.
• Option B. The risk is the same as in patients with other causes of ESRD: While patients with ESRD generally have a higher risk of certain malignancies, the risk of urothelial malignancy is specifically elevated in those with analgesic nephropathy.
• Option D. There is no association between analgesic nephropathy and urothelial malignancy: Extensive research has established a clear link between prolonged analgesic use, analgesic nephropathy, and the increased risk of developing urothelial cancers.
• Educational Objective:
• Patients with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) caused by analgesic nephropathy are at an increased risk of developing urothelial malignancies (cancers of the urinary tract) compared to individuals with ESRD from other causes.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, and palpitations. Her TSH is normal, and free T4 is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely cause?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Graves' disease: TSH would be suppressed as it is a primary hyperthyroidism. RAIU will show diffuse increase in uptake of Iodine.
• Option B. Toxic multinodular goiter: TSH would be suppressed as it is a primary hyperthyroidism. RAIU will show patchy multi-focal increased uptake of Iodine.
• Option C. Subacute thyroiditis (de Quervain's thyroiditis): TSH would be suppressed as it is a primary hyperthyroidism. RAIU will show decreased uptake.
• Educational Objective:
• A TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma is indicated by the combination of normal TSH and elevated free T4, consistent with central hyperthyroidism.

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old male presents with congestive heart failure and sepsis. Thyroid function tests (TFT) reveal a low total T3, normal free T4 and normal TSH. What is the next step?
options: Give Levothyroxine 200µg and 100mg Hydrocortisone IV STAT
Observation and repeat TFT after discharge

explanation.short: Ans. D) Observation and repeat TFT after discharge
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Give Levothyroxine 200µg IV STAT: Levothyroxine replacement is not indicated in SES and may even be harmful.
• Option B. Give Hydrocortisone 100mg IV STAT: This could be appropriate for suspected adrenal insufficiency.
• Option C. Give Levothyroxine 200µg and 100mg Hydrocortisone IV STAT: This could be an appropriate treatment for myxedema coma.
• Educational Objective:
• In patients with sick euthyroid syndrome, characterized by low total T3, normal free T4, and normal TSH in the context of critical illness, observation and repeat TFTs after recovery is the appropriate management.

subject: Medicine
question: A 4-year-old boy presents with growth retardation, delayed bone age, and normal intellect. His thyroid function tests reveal elevated T3, T4, and normal TSH levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma: This would result in elevated TSH and T4 levels, but the patient would likely exhibit symptoms of hyperthyroidism.
• Option C. McCune-Albright syndrome: This syndrome is associated with precocious puberty, bone lesions, and café-au-lait spots.
• Option D. Graves' disease: This autoimmune disorder causes clinical hyperthyroidism with elevated T4 and suppressed TSH levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Refetoff syndrome, or resistance to thyroid hormone (RTH), presents with elevated T3 and T4, normal TSH levels, growth retardation, and delayed bone age, with the patient usually being clinically euthyroid or hypothyroid.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 40-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of palpitations and excessive sweating. Physical examination reveals a non-tender thyroid gland. Thyroid function tests show an elevated free T4 and suppressed TSH. Radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) scan is shown. Thyroglobulin levels are low. What is the likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/17/screenshot-2024-08-17-133042_3j62nY0.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Graves' disease: Graves' disease is an autoimmune condition causing hyperthyroidism. It typically presents with a diffuse goiter, increased RAIU, and high thyroglobulin levels.
• Option C. Toxic Multinodular Goiter: This condition also has an increased RAIU and increased thyroglobulin levels.
• Option D. Thyroiditis: RAIU can be suppressed but thyroglobulin levels are usually elevated in thyroiditis.
• Educational Objective:
• Thyrotoxicosis factitia is characterized by low radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) and low thyroglobulin levels, indicating exogenous thyroid hormone use.

subject: Medicine
question: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of fatigue and constipation. Thyroid function tests show reduced free T4 and increased TSH levels. Which of the following symptoms or signs is LEAST likely to be associated with her condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Alopecia: Hypothyroidism can cause hair loss due to slowed metabolism and decreased hair growth.
• Option B. Amenorrhea: Hypothyroidism can disrupt menstrual cycles (Menorrhagia initially and oligo/amenorrhea in long standing disease).
• Option C. Carpal tunnel syndrome: Hypothyroidism can cause fluid retention and swelling, which can compress the median nerve in the wrist, leading to carpal tunnel syndrome
• Educational Objective:
• Cardiac tamponade is the least likely symptom associated with primary hypothyroidism, despite pericardial effusion being relatively common.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is false regarding congenital hypothyroidism?
options: Thyroid dyshormonogenesis is the most common cause.
Most infants appear normal at birth.
The recommended initial dose of levothyroxine is 10-15 µg/kg/day.
Early treatment can significantly improve neurodevelopmental outcomes.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Thyroid dyshormonogenesis is the most common cause.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Most infants appear normal at birth: The majority of infants appear normal at birth (but classic features are prolonged jaundice, poor feeding, hypotonia, macroglossia, delayed bone maturation, and umbilical hernia).
• Option C. The recommended initial dose of levothyroxine is 10-15 µg/kg/day: When the diagnosis is confirmed, T4 is instituted at a dose of 10–15 μg/kg/day and the dose is adjusted by close monitoring of TSH levels.
• Option D. Early treatment can significantly improve neurodevelopmental outcomes: Early treatment with T4 results in normal IQ levels, but subtle neurodevelopmental abnormalities may occur in those with the most severe hypothyroidism at diagnosis or when treatment is delayed or suboptimal.
• Educational Objective:
• Thyroid gland dysgenesis, not thyroid dyshormonogenesis, is the most common cause of congenital hypothyroidism.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old hypothyroid patient calls your OPD and informs that she has missed her thyroxine tablet the previous day and asks what to do about it. What will be your advice?
options: Ask her to review immediately to the hospital
Ask her to take 2 tablets today
Skip the missed dose and resume from today
Ask her to take TFT and review

explanation.short: Ans. B) Ask her to take 2 tablets today
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• All others are incorrect by default.
• Educational Objective:
• Advise hypothyroid patients who miss a dose of thyroxine to take two doses the next day due to the long half-life of T4.

subject: Medicine
question:
Match the following:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/17/whatsapp-image-2024-08-17-at-121458-pm.jpeg]

explanation.short: Ans. A) A-iv, B-iii, C-ii, D-i
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• If there is no residual thyroid function, the daily replacement dose of LT4 is usually 1.6 μg/kg body weight (typically 100–150 μg/d) Adult patients under 60 years old without evidence of heart disease may be started on 50–100 μg of LT4 daily. For adults with indications to treat in a subclinical hypothyroidism treatment is administered by starting with a low dose of LT4 (25–50 μg/d). In the elderly, especially patients with known coronary artery disease, the starting dose of LT4 is 12.5–25 μg/d.
• Educational Objective:
• Standard Adult: 50-100 µg/day Post thyroidectomy: 100-150 µg/day Coronary Artery Disease: 12.5-25 µg/day Subclinical Hypothyroidism: 25-50 µg/day

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old woman with a history of Hashimoto's thyroiditis is brought to the emergency department with altered mental status, hypothermia (35°C), bradycardia (45 bpm), and hypotension (80/50 mmHg). Her skin is dry and doughy, and she has non-pitting edema in her extremities. Which of the following is the NOT indicated?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Levothyroxine 200 μg IV bolus: LT4 single IV bolus of 200–400 μg (loading dose) is the preferred initial treatment in myxedema coma (alternate if no IV avaialble: NG tube LT4).
• Option B. IV Hydrocortisone 50mg 6 th hourly: Parenteral hydrocortisone (50 mg every 6 h) should be administered because there is impaired adrenal reserve in profound hypothyroidism
• Option C. IV broad spectrum Antibiotics: Sepsis is an important precipitating cause of myxedema coma. Early use of broad-spectrum antibiotics is warranted until infection is excluded.
• Educational Objective:
• External (active) rewarming is not indicated for myxedema coma unless the temperature is below 30°C, as it can result in cardiovascular collapse.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman with a history of Graves' disease presents to the emergency department with fever, tachycardia, agitation, and vomiting. She recently underwent a dental procedure. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for her suspected condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Propranolol (A): Can be used for cardio-protection but not immediately required.
• Option C. Radioactive iodine (C) : Not suitable for acute management of thyrotoxic crisis.
• Option D. Hydrocortisone (D) : Adjunctive therapy in thyroid storm, can be added later.
• Educational Objective:
• Propylthiouracil (PTU) is the most appropriate initial treatment for thyrotoxic crisis (thyroid storm) due to its rapid onset of action and ability to inhibit peripheral conversion of T4 to T3.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following are features of thyrotoxicosis except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Sinus Tachycardia: The most common CV manifestation in thyrotoxicosis is sinus tachycardia.  Atrial fibrillation is more common in patients >50 years of age.
• Option B. Polyuria : Probably due to increased renal blood flow and hypercalciuria.
• Option C. Hypercalcemia: This is due to the effect of thyroid hormones causing bone resorption.
• Educational Objective:
• Hypercalciuria, not hypocalciuria, is a common feature of thyrotoxicosis due to the effect of thyroid hormones on bone resorption.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with thyrotoxicosis develops atrial fibrillation. Which of the following statements regarding their management is CORRECT?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Long-term anticoagulation is unnecessary
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Age < 50 is a risk factor: Elderly patients (Age >50) are at increased risk.
• Option C. Digoxin sensitivity is increased: Digoxin sensitivity is decreased because of increased renal clearance and increased number of Na-K ATPse.
• Option D. Warfarin sensitivity is decreased: Warfarin sensitivity is increased as production of vitamin K dependent clotting factors are reduced.
• Educational Objective:
• Long-term anticoagulation is generally unnecessary in patients with atrial fibrillation and thyrotoxicosis, as spontaneous reversion to sinus rhythm often occurs with control of hyperthyroidism.

subject: Medicine
question: A 54-year-old male smoker with a history of Graves' disease presents with weight loss and loss of appetite. He is on carbimazole 5mg per day, and his recent free T4 levels (done a week ago) were normal. He has mild orbitopathy but no dermopathy. On examination, you notice clubbing; otherwise, he is vitally stable. What is the next step?

explanation.short: Ans. C) CT of the chest
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. MRI of the orbit: It not the primary concern given the new symptoms and clubbing.
• Option B. X-ray of hands with wrist: May be useful in thyroid acropachy (It refers to a form of clubbing found in < 1% of patients with Graves’ disease. It is so strongly associated with thyroid dermopathy that an alternative cause of clubbing should be sought in a Graves’ patient without coincident skin and orbital involvement).
• Option D. Repeat TFT: Repeating thyroid function tests is unlikely to yield new information.
• Educational Objective:
• In a smoker with Graves' disease presenting with clubbing, weight loss, and loss of appetite, a CT of the chest is indicated to rule out an underlying lung malignancy.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following should be avoided in children with Graves disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Methimazole: Preferred treatment in children with Graves disease.
• Option C. Radioactive Iodine Ablation: Usually reserved for refractory/severe Graves disease in children.
• Option D. Thyroidectomy: Usually reserved for refractory/severe Graves disease in children.
• Educational Objective:
• Propylthiouracil should be avoided in children with Graves' disease due to the risk of hepatitis.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is unlikely in a case of neonatal thyrotoxicosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Poor intrauterine growth: Common feature due to increased basal metabolic rate in the fetus with thyrotoxicosis.
• Option C. Fetal tachycardia: HR > 160/min is commin in the fetus with thyrotoxicosis.
• Option D. Fetal goiter: Common due to trophic effects of antibodies on the thyroid gland.
• Educational Objective:
• Neonatal thyrotoxicosis is associated with advanced bone age, not reduced bone age.

subject: Medicine
question: A 25-year-old woman with Graves' disease undergoes RAI therapy. Two months later, she complains of persistent palpitations and heat intolerance. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her persistent symptoms?
options: RAI therapy is ineffective in young patients.
The patient is experiencing a relapse of Graves' disease.
The full effect of RAI therapy takes 2-3 months to manifest.

explanation.short: Ans. D) The full effect of RAI therapy takes 2-3 months to manifest.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A . RAI therapy is ineffective in young patients: RAI is effective in all age groups.
• Option B . The patient is experiencing a relapse of Graves' disease: Relapse is possible, but less likely within two months.
• Option C . The patient is developing hypothyroidism: Hypothyroidism is a later complication of RAI.
• Summary of RAIA in Graves:
• Indication : Initial treatment or for relapses after antithyroid drugs. Tracer used: I131. Emits β & γ. t1/2 ∼ 8 days. Elderly/Cardiac Patients: Consider pretreatment with antithyroid drugs and beta-blockers (because of small, increased risk of Thyrotoxic Crisis) Carbimazole/Methimazole: Stop 2-3 days before and restart 3-7 days after RAI. PTU: Prolonged radioprotective effect, requires longer discontinuation or higher RAI dose. Persistence of Hyperthyroidism: Can persist for 2-3 months, managed with beta-blockers or antithyroid drugs. Safety Precautions: Avoid close contact with children and pregnant women for 5-7 days. Complications: Radiation Thyroiditis: Mild pain may occur 1-2 weeks after treatment. Flare of Orbitopathy: Especially in smokers. Consider prednisone pre-treatment. Permanent Hypothyroidism: Risk is 10-20% in the first year, 5% per year thereafter, requiring lifelong LT4 replacement. Possibly a small increased lifetime risk of solid cancers. Contraindications: Pregnancy, breastfeeding and suspected thyroid cancer.
• Educational Objective:
• The full effect of RAI therapy for Graves' disease takes 2-3 months to manifest, which can explain persistent symptoms shortly after treatment.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old woman presents with progressive confusion, memory loss, and seizures. She also has a history of hypothyroidism and she is on Levothyroxine 75 µg/d. Her records suggest a high anti TPO antibodies and MRI of the brain reveals diffuse white matter hyperintensities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Multiple sclerosis (MS): MS follows dissemination in space and time and it is not associated with anti TPO antibodies.
• Option B. Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML): It is caused by JC virus and is usually seen in immunocompromised individuals.
• Option D. Neurosarcoidosis: The clinical features are NOT compatible with the diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
