• Option D. Lung transplantation : Considered for patients with advanced disease who are not candidates for other treatments, but pulmonary endarterectomy is preferred if feasible.
• Educational Objective :
• Pulmonary endarterectomy is the best treatment for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) as it removes organized thrombi from the pulmonary arteries, improving hemodynamics and symptoms.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old male presents to the OPD with exertional dyspnea, fatigue, syncope, and peripheral edema. On examination, JVP is raised and a loud P2 is noted. ECHO shows right atrial and right ventricular enlargement with a mean pulmonary artery pressure of 30 mmHg. Which of the following statements is false regarding the condition?
options: Diuretics are given to reduce peripheral edema
Oxygen supplementation is given if PO2 is reduced
For patients with persistent right heart failure, lung transplantation may be considered
Pulmonary arterial hypertension is more common in males

explanation.short: Ans. D) Pulmonary arterial hypertension is more common in males
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Diuretics are given to reduce peripheral edema : True. Diuretics are commonly used to manage symptoms of right heart failure, including peripheral edema.
• Option B. Oxygen supplementation is given if PO2 is reduced : True. Oxygen therapy is used to maintain adequate oxygenation and prevent hypoxemia in PAH patients.
• Option C. For patients with persistent right heart failure, lung transplantation may be considered : True. Lung transplantation is a treatment option for patients with advanced PAH and right heart failure that is refractory to medical therapy.
• Educational Objective :
• Idiopathic Pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH) is more common in females than in males, with a female-to-male ratio of approximately 3:1.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old male presents with chronic progressive breathlessness. On examination, his JVP is elevated, there is a loud 2nd heart sound, and bilateral pedal edema. Right heart catheterization reveals pulmonary hypertension. Pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension is defined as:
options: mPAP >20 mm Hg, PCWP > 15 mm Hg
mPAP >25 mm Hg, PCWP > 15 mm Hg
mPAP >20 mm Hg, PCWP < 15 mm Hg
mPAP >25 mm Hg, PCWP < 15 mm Hg

explanation.short: Ans. C) mPAP >20 mm Hg, PCWP < 15 mm Hg
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. mPAP >20 mm Hg, PCWP > 15 mm Hg : This describes post-capillary pulmonary hypertension, indicating elevated pressures due to left heart disease.
• Option B. mPAP >25 mm Hg, PCWP > 15 mm Hg : This was the previous threshold for post-capillary pulmonary hypertension but has been updated to mPAP >20 mm Hg.
• Option D. mPAP >25 mm Hg, PCWP < 15 mm Hg : This was the previous threshold for pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension but has been updated to mPAP >20 mm Hg.
• Educational Objective :
• Pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension is defined by a mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) greater than 20 mm Hg and a pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) less than 15 mm Hg.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old male from an endemic region presents with signs of pulmonary hypertension (PH). He has a history of hepatosplenic disease and portal hypertension. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his pulmonary hypertension?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) : Typically associated with a history of smoking and different clinical features.
• Option B. Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (IPAH) : A diagnosis of exclusion when other causes of PH are ruled out.
• Option D. Left heart disease : Causes post-capillary pulmonary hypertension, typically with elevated PCWP, which is not the scenario here.
• Educational Objective :
• Pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension is defined by a mean pulmonary artery pressure (mPAP) greater than 20 mm Hg and a pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) less than 15 mm Hg.

subject: Medicine
question: The Gold's criteria for very severe COPD is: (NEET PG 2018)

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Currently Class C+D have been combined and called Class E Classification of types have been updated.
• ↳ Reference: GOLD 2023 guidelines

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old man with a history of asthma presents to the clinic with worsening wheezing, cough, and shortness of breath. Which of the following is the LEAST likely trigger for his current exacerbation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Options A. Exposure to cat dander: Animal dander is a common allergen and a well-known trigger for asthma exacerbations.
• Options B. Recent URI with rhinovirus: Viral respiratory infections are among the most common triggers of asthma exacerbations.
• Options C. Using a beta-blocker for hypertension: Beta-blockers can worsen asthma symptoms due to their bronchoconstrictive effects by blocking beta-2 receptors in the airways.
• Educational Objective :
• Alcohol consumption is not a direct trigger for bronchoconstriction and airway inflammation in asthma.

subject: Medicine
question: A 5-year-old boy with a family history of asthma and allergies presents with recurrent wheezing, coughing, and shortness of breath. Genetic testing is being considered to assess his risk of developing persistent asthma. Which of the following genes is MOST consistently associated with asthma susceptibility?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Options A. CFTR: Associated with cystic fibrosis.
• Options B. BRCA1: Associated with breast and ovarian cancer.
• Options D. KCNH2: Associated with long QT syndrome.
• Educational Objective :
• ORMDL3 is the most consistently associated gene with asthma susceptibility.

subject: Medicine
question: A 36-year-old woman experiences recurrent episodes of wheezing and nasal congestion after taking aspirin for a headache. Which of the following is most likely associated with her condition?
options: Positive skin test for inhaled allergens

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Raised serum IgE levels: Associated with allergic asthma, not AERD.
• Option B. Positive skin test for inhaled allergens: Also associated with allergic asthma.
• Option D. A type of extrinsic asthma: AERD is a type of intrinsic (non-allergic) asthma.
• Educational Objective :
• Nasal polyps are characteristic of aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) in patients with asthma and aspirin sensitivity.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient experiences recurrent episodes of wheezing, cough, and shortness of breath. Which test is most reliable in confirming the diagnosis of bronchial asthma?
options: Demonstration of reversible obstruction on PFT

explanation.short: Ans. D) Demonstration of reversible obstruction on PFT
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Chest X-ray: Typically normal in asthma.
• Option B. Skin prick test: Identifies allergic triggers, but not diagnostic of asthma.
• Option C. FEV1 measurement: May show obstruction, but reversibility is key for diagnosis.
• Educational Objective :
• Demonstration of reversible obstruction on PFTs is the most reliable test for confirming the diagnosis of bronchial asthma.

subject: Medicine
question: After administering a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) inhaler like salbutamol for suspected asthma, how long should you wait before repeating spirometry to assess for reversibility?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. 5 minutes: Too soon; the full effect of the bronchodilator may not yet be evident.
• Option C. 30 minutes and, D. 60 minutes : Waiting longer may lead to the bronchodilator effect wearing off, giving a false negative result.
• Educational Objective :
• Wait 15 minutes after administering a SABA inhaler like salbutamol before repeating spirometry to assess for reversibility.

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old man with stable COPD and resting oxygen saturation of 88% on room air is started on long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT). Which of the following is NOT a known benefit of LTOT in COPD?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) in COPD patients with chronic hypoxemia (resting SpO2 ≤ 88%) has several established benefits:
• Improved survival: LTOT has been shown to increase survival in patients with severe resting hypoxemia (PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg). Reduced pulmonary vascular resistance: LTOT helps reduce pulmonary hypertension by improving oxygenation and decreasing hypoxic vasoconstriction. Improved V/Q mismatch: By increasing alveolar oxygen tension, LTOT can help correct the ventilation-perfusion mismatch often seen in COPD. Decreased hematocrit (polycythemia): Chronic hypoxemia can trigger an increase in red blood cell production (polycythemia). LTOT can reverse this effect
• Educational Objective :
• Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) in COPD improves survival, reduces pulmonary vascular resistance, and improves V/Q mismatch, but it does not increase hematocrit.

subject: Medicine
question: A 72-year-old man with severe emphysema secondary to alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is being evaluated for long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT). Which of the following scenarios would NOT meet the criteria for LTOT in this patient?
options: Resting PaO2 of 54 mmHg and evidence of cor pulmonale
Resting SpO2 of 88% on room air and a history of two COPD exacerbations in the past year
Resting PaO2 of 58 mmHg and a hematocrit of 55%
Resting SpO2 of 89% and a normal 6-minute walk distance

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Resting PaO2 of 54 mmHg and evidence of cor pulmonale: Meets the criteria for LTOT.
• Option B. Resting SpO2 of 88% and a history of two COPD exacerbations: Meets the criteria due to low SpO2.
• Option C. Resting PaO2 of 58 mmHg and a hematocrit of 55%: Meets the criteria due to low PaO2 and high hematocrit
• Educational Objective :
• Resting SpO2 of 89% and a normal 6-minute walk distance do not meet the criteria for long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) in COPD.

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old man with severe COPD and chronic bronchitis presents with a history of frequent exacerbations despite optimal bronchodilator therapy. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke 1 pack per day. Which of the following medications is most likely to reduce the frequency of his exacerbations?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Tiotropium: While a LAMA improves lung function and symptoms, it doesn't have the same impact on exacerbation frequency as roflumilast.
• Option B. Salmeterol: LABAs improve lung function but don't specifically target exacerbations like roflumilast.
• Option D. Prednisolone: Oral steroids are used for acute exacerbations, not long-term prevention.
• Educational Objective :
• Roflumilast (PDE4 inhibitor) is indicated for severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations, reducing inflammation and mucus production.

subject: Medicine
question: A 60-year-old smoker with a history of COPD presents to the clinic with a COPD Assessment Test (CAT) score of 15 and two hospitalizations for COPD exacerbations in the past year. Which GOLD category does he belong to based on the 2023 GOLD guidelines?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. GOLD A: Low risk, low symptoms
• Option B. GOLD B: Low risk, high symptoms
• Option C. GOLD C: No longer used in the 2023 guidelines
• Educational Objective :
• Patients with a high symptom burden (CAT score ≥10) or a history of exacerbations belong to GOLD category E according to the 2023 GOLD guidelines.

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old man with a long history of smoking and progressive dyspnea presents with a barrel chest and expiratory wheezing. Which of the following pulmonary function test (PFT) findings is NOT typically seen in emphysema?
options: Increased diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO)

explanation.short: Ans. C) Increased diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Increased total lung capacity (TLC) : Common in emphysema due to air trapping and hyperinflation.
• Option B. Decreased FEV1/FVC ratio: Hallmark of obstructive lung disease like emphysema.
• Option D. Decreased vital capacity (VC): Often seen in emphysema due to severe air trapping - also called as ‘pseudo-restriction’
• Educational Objective :
• Increased DLCO is not typically seen in emphysema, as this condition reduces gas exchange surface area, leading to decreased DLCO.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old patient with severe COPD undergoes spirometry after bronchodilator administration. His FEV1 is 28% of the predicted value. Which GOLD stage does he belong to?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. GOLD 1 (Mild) : FEV1 ≥ 80% predicted
• Option B. GOLD 2 ( Moderate) : 50% ≤ FEV1 < 80% predicted
• Option C. GOLD 3 (Severe) : 30% ≤ FEV1 < 50% predicted
• Educational Objective :
• Very severe COPD (GOLD 4) is defined by a post-bronchodilator FEV1 less than 30% of the predicted value.

subject: Medicine
question: A 62-year-old woman with COPD reports that she becomes short of breath when walking at her own pace on level ground, and sometimes has to stop for breath. Based on the modified Medical Research Council (mMRC) dyspnea scale, what is her dyspnea grade?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. mMRC 0: No breathlessness except with strenuous exercise.
• Option B. mMRC 1: Breathlessness when hurrying or walking up a slight hill.
• Option D. mMRC 3: Walks slower than others of same age due to breathlessness or stops for breath at own pace.
• Educational Objective :
• The patient's description matches the mMRC grade 2 criterion: "Short of breath when hurrying on the level or walking up a slight hill."

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the BODE index, a prognostic tool in COPD that incorporates the mMRC dyspnea scale?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. BMI: Lower BMI is associated with worse prognosis in COPD.
• Option B. Airflow obstruction (FEV1): Lower FEV1 indicates worse lung function and prognosis.
• Option C. Dyspnea (mMRC grade): Higer mMRC grade indicates more severe dyspnea and worse prognosis.
• Educational Objective :
• Exacerbation frequency, while important, is not included in the BODE index, which incorporates BMI, FEV1, mMRC dyspnea grade, and 6-minute walk distance.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old man with a 20-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive shortness of breath and chronic cough. Spirometry reveals an FEV1 of 62% predicted and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 39%. After administration of a bronchodilator (BD), the FEV1 improves to 75% predicted, and the FEV1/FVC ratio improves to 73%. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease) : Although COPD can also cause airflow obstruction, it is typically less reversible with bronchodilators.
• Option C. Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA) : While ABPA can cause asthma-like symptoms, it is usually associated with elevated IgE levels and positive Aspergillus serology.
• Option D. Pulmonary Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis: Primarily seen in smokers and associated with cystic lung lesions on imaging.
• Educational Objective :
• Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC > 70% rules out COPD and establishes the diagnosis of asthma, with significant improvement in FEV1 indicating reversibility.

subject: Medicine
question: A 60-year-old smoker with a long-standing history of dyspnea and a scanty cough experiences relief with bronchodilator therapy. Pulmonary function tests reveal an FEV1/FVC 54% and FEV1 50% predicted. Post Bronchodilator neb his FEV1/FVC improved to 65% and FEV1 improved to 57%. Which of the following is NOT a typical feature of this patient's likely diagnosis?
options: Can occur due to protease-antiprotease imbalance

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Irreversible airflow limitation: This is the hallmark of COPD.
• Option C. Affects small airways: Small airway inflammation and narrowing contribute to COPD.
• Option D. Can occurs due to protease-antiprotease imbalance: This imbalance contributes to the destruction of lung tissue in emphysema, which is a major cause of COPD
• Educational Objective :
• COPD is characterized by air trapping, leading to an increased residual volume (RV), not decreased.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is found to have emphysema predominantly affecting the lower lobes of the lungs. Which type of emphysema is this most consistent with?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Centrilobular emphysema: Predominantly affects the respiratory bronchioles and is more common in smokers.
• Option B. Paraseptal emphysema: Occurs adjacent to pleural surfaces and interlobular septa.
• Option D. Bullous emphysema: Refers to large bullae formation, not a distinct type.
• Educational Objective :
• Panacinar emphysema involves uniform destruction of the acinus and is typically seen in the lower lobes of patients with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old man with moderate to severe COPD is being considered for treatment with inhaled corticosteroids (ICS). Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the use of ICS in COPD?
options: ICS reduce the frequency of COPD exacerbations.
ICS reduce the rate of decline of FEV1 in COPD
ICS reduce the incidence of pneumonia in COPD.

explanation.short: Ans. B) ICS reduce the frequency of COPD exacerbations.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. ICS reduce mortality in COPD : Not true. But recently, triple combinations (LABA + LAMA + ICS) in selected patients (Group E) has shown to reduce mortality.
• Option C. ICS reduce the rate of decline of FEV1 in COPD : Not true.
• Option D. ICS reduce the incidence of pneumonia in COPD : Actually, ICS may slightly increase the risk of pneumonia.
• Educational Objective :
• Inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) reduce the frequency of COPD exacerbations.

subject: Medicine
question: A 69-year-old smoker with COPD has had three hospitalizations for exacerbations in the past year. He is already on LABA(long acting beta 2 agonist), LAMA long acting muscarinic antagonist) and ICS (inhaled corticosteroids). Which of the following treatments is most likely to decrease the frequency of his exacerbations?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Continuous oxygen at 2 L/min: While oxygen therapy is crucial for patients with chronic hypoxemia, it doesn't directly reduce the frequency of exacerbations.
• Option C. Nocturnal BiPAP (bilevel positive airway pressure) : This is indicated for COPD patients with hypercapnic respiratory failure or nocturnal desaturation, not as a primary prevention for exacerbations.
• Option D. Inhaled corticosteroids: The patient is already on ICS, and further increasing the dose is unlikely to provide additional benefit in preventing exacerbations.
• Educational Objective :
• Daily azithromycin 500mg can significantly reduce the frequency of exacerbations in severe COPD patients already on LABA, LAMA, and ICS therapy.

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old man with an acute exacerbation of COPD presents with respiratory distress, tachypnea, and altered mental status. Which of the following would be an absolute contraindication to initiating non-invasive ventilation (NIV)?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Inability to protect airway
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hypoxemia (PaO2 < 60 mmHg on room air): NIV can be used to improve oxygenation in COPD exacerbations.
• Option B. Hypercapnia (PaCO2 > 50 mmHg): NIV can improve ventilation and reduce CO2 levels.
• Option C. Respiratory acidosis (pH < 7.35): NIV can help correct acidosis caused by hypercapnia.
• Educational Objective :
• Inability to protect the airway is an absolute contraindication to initiating non-invasive ventilation (NIV) in patients with acute exacerbation of COPD.

subject: Medicine
question: A 30-year-old occasional smoker presents with a chronic cough and dyspnea on exertion. Physical examination is unremarkable, and chest X-ray is normal. Spirometry reveals an FEV1 of 60% predicted and an FEV1/FVC ratio of 80%. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Preserved Ratio Impaired Spirometry (PRISm)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Asthma: Usually shows a reduced FEV1/FVC ratio with reversibility.
• Option B. COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease) : Requires an FEV1/FVC < 0.70.
• Option D. Normal lung function: Not possible with reduced FEV1.
• Educational Objective:
• PRISm (Preserved Ratio Impaired Spirometry) is characterized by a reduced FEV1 with a normal FEV1/FVC ratio, often seen as a potential precursor to COPD.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman with a history of asthma presents with worsening wheezing, cough, and shortness of breath. There is no associated angioedema or urticaria. Her vital signs are stable, and her oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. Would intramuscular epinephrine be indicated in this scenario?
options: Yes, epinephrine is indicated for all asthma exacerbations.
Yes, but only if she fails to respond to inhaled bronchodilators.
No, epinephrine is only indicated for asthma associated with anaphylaxis or angioedema.
No, epinephrine should only be used in patients with severe asthma exacerbations requiring hospitalization.

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Yes, epinephrine is indicated for all asthma exacerbations: Incorrect. Epinephrine is a potent drug reserved for life-threatening situations like anaphylaxis.
• Option B. Yes, but only if she fails to respond to inhaled bronchodilators: Incorrect. While epinephrine may be used in refractory cases, it's not the initial therapy for most asthma exacerbations.
• Option D. No, epinephrine should only be used in patients with severe asthma exacerbations requiring hospitalization: Incorrect. While epinephrine is often used in severe cases, it's indicated for any asthma exacerbation with anaphylaxis or angioedema, regardless of severity.
• Educational Objective :
• Intramuscular epinephrine is only indicated for asthma exacerbations associated with anaphylaxis or angioedema.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old woman with mild persistent asthma is well-controlled on a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) as maintenance therapy. She experiences occasional wheezing and shortness of breath, especially after exercise. According to current asthma guidelines, which of the following is the preferred reliever medication for her symptoms?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Low-dose ICS + LABA (long-acting beta-agonist) combination
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) alone: Although SABAs provide quick relief, they don't address underlying inflammation.
• Option B. Short-acting muscarinic antagonist (SAMA) alone: Less effective than SABAs in most cases.
• Option C. Low-dose ICS + SABA combination : Not preferred as the LABA component is short-acting and doesn't provide sustained bronchodilation.
• Educational Objective :
• The preferred reliever medication for mild persistent asthma is a combination inhaler containing both low-dose ICS and a LABA.

subject: Medicine
question: A 20-year-old man with severe asthma remains symptomatic despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids (ICS), long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMA), and long-acting beta-agonists (LABA). He is also on oral steroids and montelukast. Further evaluation reveals elevated IgE levels and FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) of 55 ppb. Which therapy is most likely to be effective for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Inhaled short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) as needed: Used for quick relief, not long-term control.
• Option B. Bronchial thermoplasty: Considered for severe refractory asthma but not the first-line option in this case.
• Option D. Oral corticosteroids at higher doses: Already on high doses, increasing further may lead to adverse effects.
• Educational Objective :
• Anti-IgE therapy (e.g., omalizumab) is indicated for severe allergic asthma not controlled with standard therapy, especially in the presence of elevated IgE and FeNO levels.

subject: Medicine
question: A 10-year-old boy with a history of mild persistent asthma is brought to the ER with an acute exacerbation. His symptoms include wheezing, shortness of breath, and cough. He is using accessory muscles to breathe, and his peak flow is 50% of his personal best. Which of the following is NOT recommended as part of his initial management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Oxygen therapy to maintain SpO2 ≥ 94%: Essential to correct hypoxemia.
• Option B. Repeated doses of nebulized salbutamol: Mainstay of acute bronchodilator therapy.
• Option C. Oral prednisolone: Oral corticosteroids reduce airway inflammation and are important in exacerbation management.
• Educational Objective :
• LABAs like formoterol are not used in the acute management of asthma exacerbations due to their delayed onset of action.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following are complications of CPAP therapy in OSA except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Nasal congestion: Common due to the air pressure and can be mitigated with humidification or steroid/saline nasal sprays.
• Option C. Claustrophobia: A common issue due to the mask, which can make some patients feel confined. Can mitigate by changing mask interface (e.g., nasal prongs)
• Option D. Aerophagia: Swallowing air, which can lead to bloating and discomfort, is a known side effect of CPAP therapy. Can mitigate to some extent by administering antacids
• Educational Objective:
• Difficulty inhaling is not a typical complication of CPAP therapy; common issues include nasal congestion, claustrophobia, and aerophagia.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old man with a history of severe COPD presents to the emergency department with an acute exacerbation. He is tachypneic, using accessory muscles of respiration, and his oxygen saturation is 88% on room air. Arterial blood gas analysis shows a pH of 7.28 and PaCO2 of 65 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Proceed with endotracheal intubation (ETT) and mechanical ventilation

explanation.short: Ans. A) Initiate noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Start IV methylprednisolone: Corticosteroids are indicated in COPD exacerbations, but NIPPV should be prioritized in patients with respiratory failure.
• Option C. Administer a nebulized bronchodilator: Bronchodilators are important for symptom relief, but they won't address the primary issue of hypercapnia and acidosis.
• Option D. Proceed with endotracheal intubation (ETT) and mechanical ventilation: While intubation may be necessary if NIPPV fails or the patient deteriorates, it is not the first-line treatment due to its associated risks and complications.
• Educational Objective :
• Initiate noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) for patients with acute COPD exacerbation and respiratory failure to improve ventilation and avoid invasive mechanical ventilation.

subject: Medicine
question:
A young patient with asthma is given a device to monitor his asthma. What is the device?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/picture5.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Spirometer: Measures lung volumes and flow rates during forced exhalation and inhalation.
• Option C. Body plethysmograph: Measures total lung capacity and airway resistance.
• Option D. Incentive spirometer: Helps improve lung function by encouraging deep breaths.
• Educational Objective :
• The dveice shown in the image is a peak flow meter used by asthmatics to measure how fast they can forcefully exhale, providing a quick assessment of airway obstruction.

subject: Medicine
question: A 62-year-old patient with known bronchiectasis is hospitalized with acute worsening of cough, sputum production, and fever. Which antibiotic regimen is most appropriate for this exacerbation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Amoxicillin: Not effective against Pseudomonas .
• Option B. Azithromycin: May be used in chronic management but not first-line for acute exacerbations with Pseudomonas.
• Option D. Doxycycline: Not effective against Pseudomonas.
• Educational Objective :
• Piperacillin-tazobactam is the preferred antibiotic for acute exacerbations of bronchiectasis due to its broad-spectrum activity against Pseudomonas aeruginosa and other gram-negative organisms.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old man with chronic cough and sputum production undergoes bronchoscopy, which reveals dilated airways and nodular lesions. Bronchial washings culture positive for Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC). Which type of bronchiectasis is most likely associated?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Cylindrical bronchiectasis: This type of bronchiectasis is characterized by uniformly dilated airways, typically seen in post-infectious bronchiectasis or bronchiectasis associated with bronchial obstruction.
• Option C. Cystic bronchiectasis: This is the most severe form of bronchiectasis, with large, balloon-like dilations of the airways. While it can be seen in NTM infections, it's less common than varicose bronchiectasis.
• Option D. Traction bronchiectasis: This type is caused by fibrosis pulling on the airways, often seen in conditions like tuberculosis or sarcoidosis. It presents with irregular dilations and distortions of the airways but is less common in NTM infections.
• Key Point: Varicose bronchiectasis is the most common type seen in NTM infections, particularly Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC). The irregular dilations and constrictions of the airways resemble varicose veins.
• Educational Objective :
• Varicose bronchiectasis, with irregular airway dilation and constriction, is commonly seen in NTM infections like Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC).

subject: Medicine
question: A 66-year-old man presented after coughing up copious amounts of blood-tinged sputum at his nursing home Physical examination shows digital clubbing and cyanosis of the lips. Coarse crackles are heard throughout the lung fields and his SpO2 is 95%. A chest X-ray reveals increased translucency and tram-track opacities in the right lower lung field. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Chronic bronchitis: Typically seen in patients with a history of smoking and characterized by a chronic, productive cough. However, copious amounts of blood-tinged sputum and signs of chronic hypoxia (e.g., digital clubbing, lip cyanosis) are less typical.
• Option C. Aspiration pneumonia: Common in patients with a history of stroke, which may result in dysphagia. Patients with aspiration pneumonia may present with dyspnea, cough, and hypoxemia, but would not typically show the tram-track opacities seen in this case.
• Option D. Emphysema: Usually presents with a history of a chronic cough that is characteristically worse in the mornings, production of small amounts of sputum, and dyspnea. Chest X-rays typically show hyperinflation rather than tram-track opacities.
• Educational Objective :
• Bronchiectasis presents with chronic cough, copious sputum, hemoptysis, coarse crackles, and tram-track opacities on X-ray, along with digital clubbing and cyanosis due to chronic hypoxia.

subject: Medicine
question: A 4-year-old girl presents with productive cough, high fever, poor weight gain, and frequent respiratory infections. Exam reveals crackles in the left lower lung. Chest X-ray shows dilated bronchi and infiltrates. Sputum culture: pink rods on Gram stain, non-lactose fermenting, oxidase positive. She is at increased risk for which condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Cirrhosis: Although CF can lead to liver disease, pancreatitis is a more immediate and common complication.
• Option B. Hypothyroidism: Not typically associated with CF.
• Option C. Panacinar emphysema: More commonly related to alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, not CF.
• Educational Objective :
• Cystic fibrosis, characterized by chronic respiratory infections and bronchiectasis, increases the risk of pancreatitis due to thick mucus secretions affecting the pancreas.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 47-year-old man complains of cough, shortness of breath, and daily copious sputum production. He has a history of frequent lung infections. His SpO2 is 95%. Diffuse crackles and wheezing are heard on auscultation throughout the lung fields. A CT scan is shown. The patient is at greatest risk for which of the following complications?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/picture9.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Rhizopus infections: Causes Mucormycosis. Uncommon in bronchiectasis. More common in patients with DKA or COVID19
• Option C. Pneumothorax: While possible, it is less common in bronchiectasis. More common in Emphysema
• Option D. Lung cancer: Bronchiectasis is not a risk factor for lung cancer
• Educational Objective :
• Bronchiectasis, characterized by chronic productive cough and recurrent lung infections, significantly increases the risk of hemoptysis due to damage and dilation of the bronchial arteries.

subject: Medicine
question: A 25-year-old man with a history of upper respiratory tract infections presents with four other respiratory infections this year and frequent bouts of otitis media. He was diagnosed with bronchiectasis as a child. Which structure is most likely defective in this patient?
options: Cells with multilobular nuclei and enzyme-filled granules
Heavy and light protein chains linked by disulfide bonds
Tubular arrangements of linked heavy chains that slide against one another

explanation.short: Ans. D) Tubular arrangements of linked heavy chains that slide against one another
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Cells with multilobular nuclei and enzyme-filled granules: Refers to neutrophils, not related to this condition.
• Option B. Collagen α-chains in triplets: Related to collagen structure, not relevant here.
• Option C. Heavy and light protein chains linked by disulfide bonds: Refers to immunoglobulins, not relevant here.
• Educational Objective :
• The patient's symptoms suggest primary ciliary dyskinesia (Kartagener syndrome), where the defect is in the cilia's dynein arms, which are tubular arrangements of linked heavy chains that slide against one another to facilitate movement.

subject: Medicine
question: A 5-year-old child with cystic fibrosis presents with a persistent cough and frequent respiratory infections over the past year. Sputum cultures have consistently grown a specific pathogen. Given the patient's age and clinical history, which pathogen is most likely responsible for these early respiratory infections?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Pseudomonas aeruginosa: This pathogen typically becomes more prevalent in the respiratory tracts of cystic fibrosis patients as they get older and is associated with chronic lung infections and a more rapid decline in lung function.
• Option C. Haemophilus influenzae: This bacterium can cause respiratory infections but is less common and less significant than Staphylococcus aureus in the early stages of cystic fibrosis.
• Option D. Streptococcus pneumoniae: Although it can cause respiratory infections, it is not the primary pathogen in early cystic fibrosis respiratory infections.
• Educational Objective :
• Staphylococcus aureus is the primary pathogen responsible for early respiratory infections in young children with cystic fibrosis, frequently causing persistent and recurrent infections.

subject: Medicine
question: A 16-year-old patient with cystic fibrosis, who has been colonized with Burkholderia cepacia complex (BCC), is scheduled for a lung transplant evaluation. What is a major concern related to BCC colonization in this context?
options: Increased risk of liver dysfunction post-transplant
Reduced success rates of lung transplantation
Higher likelihood of developing diabetes post-transplant

explanation.short: Ans. B) Reduced success rates of lung transplantation
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Increased risk of liver dysfunction post-transplant: This is not specifically related to BCC colonization.
• Option C. Higher likelihood of developing diabetes post-transplant: Diabetes can occur post-transplant but is not specifically linked to BCC.
• Option D. Increased risk of gastrointestinal complications: Gastrointestinal issues are common in cystic fibrosis but not specifically related to BCC colonization.
• Educational Objective :
• Burkholderia cepacia complex (BCC) colonization in cystic fibrosis patients is a significant concern due to the reduced success rates and increased risk of severe infections post-lung transplantation.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old patient with a history of non-CF bronchiectasis presents for a routine follow-up. The patient has experienced a noticeable decline in lung function over the past few years. What is the typical annual decline in FEV1 for patients with non-CF bronchiectasis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A . 10-15 mL per year: This rate is too low and more typical of normal aging in healthy individuals.
• Option B. 20-30 mL per year: This is the decline seen in healthy controls, not in bronchiectasis patients.
• Option D. 70-80 mL per year: This rate is higher than what is typically observed in non-CF bronchiectasis.
• Educational Objective :
• Patients with non-CF bronchiectasis typically experience an annual decline in FEV1 of about 50-55 mL, highlighting the progressive nature of the disease.

subject: Medicine
question: A male patient with cystic fibrosis is found to be infertile despite having normal spermatogenesis. What is the most likely cause of this infertility?
options: Complete involution of the vas deferens

explanation.short: Ans. B) Complete involution of the vas deferens
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Obstruction of the epididymis: Not the primary cause.
• Option C. Defective spermatogenesis: Spermatogenesis is typically normal in CF.
• Option D. Hormonal imbalances: Infertility in CF is primarily due to anatomical defects.
• Educational Objective :
• In cystic fibrosis, nearly 99% of males are infertile due to the complete involution of the vas deferens, despite having normal spermatogenesis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old with cystic fibrosis presents with right lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and obstipation. Abdominal X-ray reveals a mass in the ileocecal region with dilated bowel loops. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome (DIOS)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Appendicitis: While appendicitis can present with similar symptoms, the presence of a mass in the ileocecal region and the patient's history of cystic fibrosis make DIOS a more likely diagnosis
• Option B. Crohn's disease: Crohn's disease is an inflammatory bowel disease that can cause abdominal pain and bowel obstruction. However, it is less common in cystic fibrosis patients and typically presents with a different clinical picture and radiological findings
• Option D. Intussusception: Intussusception is the telescoping of one segment of the intestine into another. While it can cause bowel obstruction, it is more common in children and usually presents with a sausage-shaped mass on palpation, which is not seen in this case.
• Educational Objective :
• Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome (DIOS) is a common complication in cystic fibrosis, characterized by the buildup of thick, sticky stools in the distal ileum and cecum, leading to abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and obstipation.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old female patient with cystic fibrosis is found to have the most common mutation seen in these patients. What is the primary defect caused by this mutation?
options: Impaired synthesis of the CFTR protein
Defective chloride channel function at the cell surface
Increased degradation of the CFTR protein in the endoplasmic reticulum
Abnormal post-translational modification of the CFTR protein

explanation.short: Ans. C) Increased degradation of the CFTR protein in the endoplasmic reticulum
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Impaired synthesis of the CFTR protein: The delta F508 mutation affects protein folding, not synthesis.
• Option B. Defective chloride channel function at the cell surface : This is a consequence, but the primary issue is the degradation before reaching the cell surface.
• Option D. Abnormal post-translational modification of the CFTR protein : This is not the main issue with the delta F508 mutation
• Educational Objective :
• The most common mutation in cystic fibrosis, delta F508, leads to increased degradation of the CFTR protein in the endoplasmic reticulum, preventing it from reaching the cell surface and causing impaired chloride and water transport.

subject: Medicine
question: A 24-year-old patient with cystic fibrosis has a homozygous F508del mutation in the CFTR gene. What is the most appropriate treatment regimen?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ivacaftor monotherapy: Effective for certain gating mutations but not sufficient for the F508del mutation.
• Option B. Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor combination: Provides benefit for F508del mutation but is less effective than the triple therapy.
• Option C. Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor combination: An improvement over Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor but still not as effective as the triple therapy for the F508del mutation
• Educational Objective :
• The Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor combination (Trikafta) is the most advanced and effective treatment for patients with the F508del mutation in the CFTR gene.

subject: Medicine
question: A 22-year-old cystic fibrosis patient is found to have a CFTR mutation that results in defective protein processing and impaired channel function. Which type of CFTR defect is most effectively treated with a combination of potentiators and correctors?
options: Class I mutation leading to no CFTR protein production
Class II mutation causing defective protein processing
Class III mutation resulting in defective channel regulation
Class IV mutation causing decreased conductance

explanation.short: Ans. B) Class II mutation causing defective protein processing
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Class I mutation leading to no CFTR protein production: These mutations produce little to no CFTR protein, making correctors and potentiators ineffective.
• Option C. Class III mutation resulting in defective channel regulation : Typically treated effectively with potentiators alone, as the CFTR protein is present but dysfunctional.
• Option D. Class IV mutation causing decreased conductance: Potentiators may help, but the primary defect involves reduced channel function rather than misprocessing.
• Educational Objective :
• Class II CFTR mutations causing defective protein processing are most effectively treated with a combination of correctors and potentiators.

subject: Medicine
question: A 40-year-old man presents with massive hemoptysis and is desaturating. Which of the following is incorrect according to AAFP guidelines?
options: Selective intubation of the bleeding lung to remove blood clots
Position the patient with the bleeding side down

explanation.short: Ans. B) Selective intubation of the bleeding lung to remove blood clots
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Avoid Endotracheal intubation: Recommended unless absolutely necessary, to use the cough reflex for blood removal.
• Option C. Insert a single-lumen endotracheal tube: Incorrect as it does not allow for selective ventilation and protection of the nonbleeding lung.
• Option D. Position the patient with the bleeding side down : Correct as it uses gravitational advantage to prevent blood from entering the nonbleeding lung.
• Educational Objective :
• According to AAFP guidelines, selective intubation should be performed on the nonbleeding lung, not the bleeding lung, to protect the nonbleeding lung from contamination and ensure effective ventilation.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old lady presents with non-massive hemoptysis. What is the most common cause?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Tuberculosis: It is one of the most common causes in developing countries like India.
• Option C. Lung cancer: A significant cause but not the most common.
• Option D. Pulmonary embolism: Can cause hemoptysis, but it is not the most common cause of non-massive hemoptysis.
• Educational Objective :
• Viral bronchitis is the most common cause of non-massive hemoptysis, typically resulting from inflammation and irritation of the airways.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old male presents with massive hemoptysis. What is the procedure of choice for controlling the bleeding?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Bronchoscopy: Useful for diagnosis and initial management but not primarily for controlling severe bleeding.
• Option C. Surgical resection: Considered when embolization fails or is not feasible, but it is more invasive.
• Option D. Intravenous antibiotic therapy: Used to treat underlying infections but not effective for immediate control of severe bleeding.
• Educational Objective:
• Bronchial artery embolization is the procedure of choice for controlling bleeding in life-threatening cases of massive hemoptysis.

subject: Medicine
question: All of the following are complications of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Options A. Hypertension: Common in OSA due to repeated hypoxic episodes and increased sympathetic activity.
• Option B. Stroke: Increased risk due to the association of OSA with hypertension and other cardiovascular risk factors.
• Option D. Cardiovascular disease: Strongly linked with OSA, including conditions such as heart failure and arrhythmias.
• Educational Objective:
• Pulmonary fibrosis is not a typical complication of obstructive sleep apnea (OSA); common complications include hypertension, stroke, and cardiovascular disease.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old man presents with dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. A chest X-ray reveals a pleural effusion. Diagnostic thoracentesis is performed, and pleural fluid analysis shows the following: pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio of 0.7. How would this pleural effusion be classified?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Transudative : Transudative effusions do not meet Light's criteria for exudative effusions and are typically due to systemic factors such as heart failure or cirrhosis.
• Option C. Malignant : While malignancy can cause exudative effusions, this classification is based on Light's criteria, not etiology.
• Option D. Hemorrhagic : Refers to the presence of blood in the pleural fluid, which is a descriptive term rather than a classification based on Light's criteria.
• Educational Objective:
• A pleural effusion is classified as exudative if one or more of Light's criteria are met, such as a pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio > 0.5. In this case, the pleural fluid protein/serum protein ratio is 0.7, classifying it as exudative.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old man presents with dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Thoracentesis reveals loculated pleural fluid, pH of 7.15, glucose of 2.8 mmol/L (50 mg/dL), and gross pus in the pleural space. Which of the following is not an indication for chest drain placement?
options: Presence of gross pus in the pleural space

explanation.short: Ans. C) Pleural fluid glucose <60 mg/dL)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Loculated pleural fluid : Indicates complicated effusion that often requires drainage
• Option B. Pleural fluid pH <7.2 : Suggests a more severe infection or inflammation needing drainage
• Option D. Presence of gross pus in the pleural space: Clearly indicates empyema, which requires drainage.
• Educational Objective:
• While low pleural fluid glucose (<3.3 mmol/L or <60 mg/dL) suggests a complicated effusion, it is not an absolute indication for chest drain placement. However, loculated pleural fluid, pleural fluid pH <7.2, and the presence of gross pus in the pleural space are definite indications for chest drain placement to prevent or treat empyema or complicated parapneumonic effusions.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old patient with loculated pleural effusion has been treated with antibiotics and tube thoracostomy. Despite treatment, the patient continues to have fever, leukocytosis, and anorexia. Which of the following treatments should be considered next to improve clinical outcomes ?
options: Intrapleural tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and deoxyribonuclease (DNase)

explanation.short: Ans. B) Intrapleural tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and deoxyribonuclease (DNase)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Intrapleural fibrinolytic agents alone : Controlled trials have not shown improvement in drainage with fibrinolytic agents alone.
• Option C. Repeat thoracostomy : Not typically the next step when combination therapy of tPA and DNase can be more effective.
• Option D. High-dose corticosteroids : Not indicated for improving drainage or treating persistent symptoms in pleural effusion
• Educational Objective:
• Intrapleural tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and deoxyribonuclease (DNase) should be considered when fever, leukocytosis, or anorexia persist despite antibiotics and tube thoracostomy, as this combination has been shown to improve clinical outcomes, including increased drainage, decreased length of stay, and decreased surgical referral.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old male with a history of alcoholism presents with acute onset of left-sided chest pain and shortness of breath. Chest X-ray reveals a left-sided pleural effusion and pneumothorax. Thoracentesis yields turbid, red-brown fluid with a pH of 5.5. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Uncomplicated parapneumonic effusions typically have clear to turbid fluid with a near normal pH
• Option B. Malignant effusions are rarely associated with pneumothorax or a low pH
• Option D. Tuberculosis effusions are usually serous to serosanguineous  and have a near normal pH
• Educational Objective:
• The combination of pleural effusion, pneumothorax, red-brown fluid, and low pH strongly suggests esophageal rupture.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old male presents with sudden onset of pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. On examination he had an irregularly irregular pulse. X ray showed a wedge shaped opacity with effusion in the left lung. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the effusion ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Esophageal rupture: This would present with severe chest pain, often radiating to the back, and may be associated with subcutaneous emphysema or mediastinal air.
• Option C. Malignancy: While malignancy can cause pleural effusions, they are typically more indolent in onset and not associated with the acute presentation or wedge-shaped opacity seen in this case.
• Option D. Parapneumonic effusion: This is an effusion associated with pneumonia and would likely present with fever, cough, and a more diffuse consolidation on chest X-ray.
• Educational Objective:
• The combination of sudden-onset pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, irregularly irregular pulse (suggesting atrial fibrillation), and a wedge-shaped opacity on chest X-ray is highly suggestive of pulmonary embolism with subsequent pulmonary infarction, which can cause pleural effusion.

subject: Medicine
question: A 40-year-old male with a history of lymphoma presents with dyspnea and a large pleural effusion. Pleural fluid analysis reveals a milky white appearance and an elevated triglyceride level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Empyema presents with purulent fluid and a low glucose level.
• Option C. Hemothorax presents with bloody fluid.
• Option D. Pseudochylothorax has a milky white appearance but normal triglyceride levels
• Educational Objective:
• A milky white appearance and elevated triglyceride level in pleural fluid are characteristic of chylothorax, which results from the disruption of the thoracic duct.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old male presents with a gradual onset of dyspnea and a non-productive cough. Ultrasound confirms the presence of pleural effusion. Which of the following minimum fluid pocket depths on ultrasound would be considered adequate for a safe diagnostic thoracentesis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. 5 mm: This is too shallow and increases the risk of puncturing the lung.
• Option C. 15 mm and D. 20 mm: While these depths are safe, they are not the minimum required. A 10 mm pocket is sufficient for a diagnostic thoracentesis.
• Educational Objective:
• A minimum pleural fluid pocket depth of 10 mm on ultrasound is considered adequate for a safe diagnostic thoracentesis, providing sufficient space to insert the needle and aspirate fluid without risking lung injury.

subject: Medicine
question: A 24-year-old tall, thin male presents with acute-onset left-sided chest pain and shortness of breath. He reports a 10-pack-year smoking history. Chest X-ray reveals a small apical pneumothorax. Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding his condition?
options: This is most likely a secondary spontaneous pneumothorax due to underlying lung disease
The presence of apical blebs is highly suggestive of Marfan syndrome
Smoking is an important risk factor
Recurrence of pneumothorax is unlikely in this patient population.

explanation.short: Ans. C) smoking is an important risk factor
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. This is most likely a secondary spontaneous pneumothorax due to underlying lung disease : This is likely a primary, not secondary, spontaneous pneumothorax given the absence of underlying lung disease.
• Option B. The presence of apical blebs is highly suggestive of Marfan syndrome : Apical blebs are not specific to Marfan syndrome; they are common in Primary spontaneous pneumothorax
• Option D. Recurrence of pneumothorax is unlikely in this patient population : Recurrence of PSP is common, especially in smokers.
• Educational Objective:
• Smoking is an important risk factor for primary spontaneous pneumothorax, which is often associated with the rupture of subpleural apical blebs in young, tall, thin individuals. Recurrence is common, especially in smokers.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 25-year-old male with no significant past medical history presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of sharp, right-sided chest pain and shortness of breath. His JVP is normal and BP is 110/70 mm Hg. He denies any recent trauma or illness. X-ray is shown. What is the likely diagnosis ?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/screenshot-2024-06-20-160110.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Pneumonia: would typically present with consolidation or infiltrates on chest x-ray.
• Option B. Pulmonary embolism: could present with chest pain and shortness of breath, but x-ray findings are usually normal or show subtle abnormalities.
• Option D. Tension pneumothorax: this is a life-threatening emergency where the patients would be having high JVP and hypotension and X rays usually show mediastinal shift and tracheal deviation, which are not seen in this image.
• Educational Objective:
• The chest X-ray shows a visible visceral pleural line with no lung markings peripheral to it, indicating a pneumothorax. Given the patient's young age, lack of underlying lung disease, and absence of trauma, the most likely diagnosis is a primary spontaneous pneumothorax, typically caused by the rupture of apical blebs in the lungs.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of shortness of breath, chest pain, and tachycardia. Upon examination, he has decreased breath sounds on the left side, jugular venous distention, and tracheal deviation to the right. The most appropriate immediate management of this patient is:
options: Chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
Needle decompression in the second intercostal space, midclavicular line
Placement of a chest tube in the fourth intercostal space, anterior axillary line

explanation.short: Ans. B) Needle decompression in the second intercostal space, midclavicular line
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis: While a chest X-ray can confirm the diagnosis, it should not delay the immediate treatment of needle decompression in a suspected tension pneumothorax.
• Option C. Placement of a chest tube in the fourth intercostal space, anterior axillary line: This is the definitive treatment for pneumothorax, but needle decompression is the immediate life-saving intervention in a tension pneumothorax.
• Option D. Intubation and mechanical ventilation: While intubation and mechanical ventilation may be necessary later in the management, needle decompression is the priority in stabilizing the patient's condition.
• Educational Objective:
• The immediate management of a tension pneumothorax is needle decompression in the second intercostal space, midclavicular line, to relieve the pressure in the pleural space and stabilize the patient's condition. This is a life-saving intervention that should be performed promptly based on clinical suspicion without waiting for imaging confirmation.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old man is diagnosed with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) after presenting with progressive dyspnea and a dry cough and he has a strong family history. Which gene mutation is most commonly associated with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. CFTR: Associated with cystic fibrosis, not IPF.
• Option C. BRCA1: Associated with breast and ovarian cancer, not IPF.
• Option D. KRAS: Commonly associated with various cancers, including lung cancer, but not specifically IPF.
• Educational Objective :
• The MUC5B gene mutation is the most commonly associated genetic variant with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF).

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old woman presents with progressive shortness of breath and a dry cough. High-resolution CT of the chest reveals honeycombing and reticular opacities. Based on epidemiological data, which of the following is the most common interstitial lung disease?
options: Respiratory bronchiolitis associated interstitial lung disease

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Non-specific interstitial pneumonia: Less common than IPF and has different radiographic and clinical features.
• Option B. Desquamative interstitial pneumonia: Also less common and typically associated with smoking.
• Option D. Respiratory bronchiolitis associated interstitial lung disease: Primarily seen in smokers but less common than IPF.
• Educational objective:
• The most common interstitial lung disease is idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old retired coal miner presents with progressive shortness of breath and chronic cough. He has a long history of working in coal mines. Which of the following conditions is least likely to be associated with his occupational history?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD): CWP can lead to the development of COPD due to long-term exposure to coal dust.
• Option B. Interstitial lung disease (ILD): CWP is a form of ILD caused by coal dust inhalation.
• Option C. Chronic bronchitis: Chronic bronchitis is common in coal workers due to continuous exposure to coal dust.
• Educational Objective:
• Coal workers' pneumoconiosis (CWP), or black lung disease, is associated with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), interstitial lung disease (ILD), and chronic bronchitis due to long-term inhalation of coal dust. However, CWP is not directly associated with an increased risk of lung cancer.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old man with a history of working in the mining industry presents with chronic cough and joint pain. Chest X-ray shows multiple, rounded nodules in the lungs. He also has a known diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis. Which of the following diagnoses best explains his clinical presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Sarcoidosis: Characterized by bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas, not typically associated with rheumatoid arthritis and mining exposure.
• Option B. Tuberculosis: Typically presents with cavitary lesions or consolidations, not rounded nodules, and is not associated with rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option D. Interstitial lung disease (ILD): Refers to a group of disorders causing fibrosis and scarring of the lung tissue, which can include conditions like idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, but does not typically present with the specific combination of rheumatoid arthritis and mining exposure seen in Caplan syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Caplan syndrome, or rheumatoid pneumoconiosis, occurs in patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are exposed to dust such as silica or coal dust. It is characterized by multiple, rounded nodules on chest X-ray in the setting of pneumoconiosis and rheumatoid arthritis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old man who works in an aerospace manufacturing plant presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, and fatigue. Chest X-ray showed bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, and a transbronchial lung biopsy revealed non-caseating granulomas. Which diagnostic test is most appropriate for confirming the diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) with CD4/CD8 ratio : Useful in sarcoidosis but not specific for berylliosis.
• Option C. Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) level: Elevated in sarcoidosis but not specific for berylliosis.
• Option D. High-resolution CT scan of the chest: Helps in identifying interstitial lung disease but does not confirm beryllium exposure or CBD.
• Educational Objective:
• The Beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT) is the most appropriate diagnostic test for confirming chronic beryllium disease (CBD) in a patient with a history of beryllium exposure and findings of non-caseating granulomas on biopsy. The BeLPT measures the proliferation of lymphocytes in response to beryllium exposure and is specific for diagnosing beryllium sensitization and chronic beryllium disease.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 45-year-old woman presents with chronic cough, dyspnea, and fatigue. She keeps birds as pets, and high-resolution CT of the chest is done and shown. All are useful for diagnosis except:

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/screenshot-2024-06-20-124749.png]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) level
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) with lymphocyte count : An increased lymphocyte count and CD4/CD8 ration of < 1 in BAL fluid is indicative of HP and helps in the diagnosis.
• Option C. Serum antigen-specific IgG testing : Identifies antibodies against specific antigens (such as bird proteins) that can cause HP, making it useful for diagnosis.
• Option D. Non-caseating granulomas in lung biopsy : The presence of non-caseating granulomas is a key histopathological feature of HP, supporting the diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) level is typically elevated in sarcoidosis but is not specific for hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP). Therefore, it is not useful for diagnosing HP.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old agricultural worker presents with symptoms of chronic cough, dyspnea, and fatigue. He reports working with moldy hay and grain regularly. Based on the table, which pathogen is most likely responsible for his condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Penicillium casei: Associated with Cheese washer’s lung, not typically found in moldy hay or grain.
• Option B. Coffee bean dust : Associated with Coffee worker’s lung, not relevant to this patient's agricultural exposure.
• Option D. Botrytis cinerea: Associated with Wine maker’s lung, linked to grape exposure, not moldy hay or grain.
• Educational Objective:
• The patient's occupational exposure to moldy hay and grain is consistent with Farmer's lung, caused by inhaling thermophilic actinomycetes such as Saccharopolyspora rectivirgula.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of sarcoidosis presents with new-onset facial weakness and decreased tear production. She also reports episodes of dry mouth and swelling near her jawline. Which specific manifestation of sarcoidosis is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Mikulicz syndrome : Characterized by chronic, painless enlargement of the parotid and lacrimal glands, often associated with Sjögren's syndrome.
• Option C. Löfgren syndrome : Characterized by bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and arthritis.
• Option D. Blau syndrome : A genetic disorder presenting with granulomatous arthritis, uveitis, and dermatitis, seen in children, not related to Heerfordt syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Heerfordt syndrome, also known as uveoparotid fever, is a rare manifestation of sarcoidosis characterized by parotid gland enlargement, uveitis, fever, and facial nerve palsy.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old African American woman presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and arthritis. She also reports occasional fever and malaise. What is the most likely clinical syndrome associated with her presentation in the context of sarcoidosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Heerfordt syndrome : Associated with uveoparotid fever, characterized by parotid gland enlargement, uveitis, fever, and facial nerve palsy.
• Option C. Mikulicz syndrome: Characterized by chronic, painless enlargement of the parotid and lacrimal glands, not specific to sarcoidosis.
• Option D. Blau syndrome: A genetic disorder presenting with granulomatous arthritis, uveitis, and dermatitis, seen in children, not related to Löfgren syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Löfgren syndrome is a clinical syndrome associated with sarcoidosis characterized by the triad of bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and arthritis, often accompanied by fever and malaise.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 30-year-old woman presents with multiple facial lesions as shown in the image. She also reports chronic fatigue and dyspnea. Biopsy of the skin lesions shows non-caseating granulomas. What is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/screenshot-2024-06-20-131733.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Lupus vulgaris: A form of cutaneous tuberculosis that presents with reddish-brown plaques, not typically associated with non-caseating granulomas.
• Option B. Leprosy: Presents with hypopigmented or reddish skin lesions with sensory loss and is diagnosed by the presence of Mycobacterium leprae in skin biopsy.
• Option D. Discoid lupus : Presents with chronic discoid lesions, erythematous plaques with scaling, and can be associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, but does not typically show non-caseating granulomas.
• Educational Objective:
• Lupus pernio is a specific form of cutaneous sarcoidosis characterized by chronic, violaceous, indurated lesions on the face, especially around the nose, cheeks, and ears, and is associated with systemic sarcoidosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 40-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of sarcoidosis presents with new-onset vision problems and facial droop on one side. Which of the following neurological manifestations is least likely to be associated with her condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Optic neuritis : Sarcoidosis can cause inflammation of the optic nerve, leading to vision problems.
• Option B. 7th nerve palsy : Facial nerve palsy is a well-known manifestation of neurosarcoidosis, presenting as facial droop.
• Option D. Basilar meningitis : Sarcoidosis can lead to chronic basilar meningitis, presenting with symptoms such as headache and cranial nerve deficits.
• Educational Objective:
• Sarcoidosis can involve the nervous system, leading to various neurological manifestations such as CN VII palsy, optic neuritis, and basilar meningitis. Posterior hypothalamic involvement is least likely in this context.

subject: Medicine
question: A 40-year-old woman with biopsy-proven pulmonary sarcoidosis presents with worsening dyspnea and cough. Imaging shows progressive pulmonary involvement which is progressing despite initial management by his clinician. Which of the following is the treatment for refractory disease?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Methotrexate : Used as a second-line treatment or steroid-sparing agent, but not typically first-line for refractory disease.
• Option C. Prednisone : The first-line treatment for sarcoidosis, but the patient has refractory disease, indicating that prednisone alone is not sufficient.
• Option D. Azathioprine: An immunosuppressive agent used as a second-line treatment or steroid-sparing agent, but not the first choice for refractory disease.
• Educational Objective:
• Infliximab, a TNF-alpha inhibitor, is used for treating severe or refractory sarcoidosis when initial treatments such as corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) and second-line agents (e.g., methotrexate, azathioprine) are not effective.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with progressive dyspnea, recurrent pneumothoraces, and chylous pleural effusions. High-resolution CT of the chest shows diffuse thin-walled cysts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Sarcoidosis: Typically presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas, not diffuse thin-walled cysts.
• Option C. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF): Characterized by progressive fibrosis and honeycombing on CT, not thin-walled cysts.
• Option D. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD): Characterized by airflow obstruction and emphysematous changes, not diffuse thin-walled cysts.
• Educational Objective:
• Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM) is a rare lung disease affecting women of childbearing age, characterized by diffuse thin-walled cysts on high-resolution CT, recurrent pneumothoraces, and chylous pleural effusions. It involves the proliferation of abnormal smooth muscle-like cells, distinguishing it from other conditions like sarcoidosis, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

subject: Medicine
question: A 42-year-old man presents with progressive dyspnea and a chronic cough productive of white, frothy sputum. Chest X-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates, and high-resolution CT of the chest reveals a "crazy paving" pattern. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) yields milky fluid with periodic acid-Schiff (PAS)-positive material. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF): Typically presents with progressive fibrosis and honeycombing on CT, not a "crazy paving" pattern.
• Option C. Sarcoidosis: Characterized by bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas, not PAS-positive material in BAL.
• Option D. Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP): Can present with diffuse bilateral infiltrates, but the BAL fluid would typically show Pneumocystis organisms on special staining, not PAS-positive material.
• Educational Objective:
• Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is diagnosed by the accumulation of surfactant-like material in the alveoli, presenting with progressive dyspnea, a chronic cough with white frothy sputum, bilateral alveolar infiltrates on chest X-ray, and a "crazy paving" pattern on high-resolution CT. The presence of PAS-positive material in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid confirms the diagnosis. This condition is distinct from idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, each having unique diagnostic features and findings.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old man presents with a three-month history of non-productive cough, low-grade fever, and progressive dyspnea. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral patchy infiltrates, and high-resolution CT shows peripheral consolidation and ground-glass opacities. Lung biopsy reveals organizing pneumonia with granulation tissue within the alveolar ducts and bronchioles. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF): Typically presents with progressive fibrosis and honeycombing on CT, not peripheral consolidation and ground-glass opacities.
• Option C. Sarcoidosis : Characterized by bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas, not organizing pneumonia.
• Option D. Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia (CEP): Characterized by peripheral infiltrates on imaging and an increased number of eosinophils in the lung and blood, not organizing pneumonia with granulation tissue.
• Educational Objective:
• Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP) is identified by granulation tissue within the alveolar ducts and bronchioles, presenting with a non-productive cough, low-grade fever, and progressive dyspnea. Imaging shows bilateral patchy infiltrates and peripheral consolidation with ground-glass opacities. It is differentiated from idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, and chronic eosinophilic pneumonia, each having unique histopathological and radiological features.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old woman presents with a history of asthma, progressive dyspnea, and weight loss over the past few months. She also reports night sweats and cough. Chest X-ray shows peripheral infiltrates, and blood tests reveal marked eosinophilia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Acute eosinophilic pneumonia: Typically presents with acute onset of respiratory failure, diffuse pulmonary infiltrates, and eosinophilia in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid but not always in blood.
• Option C. Hypereosinophilic syndrome : Characterized by persistent eosinophilia and multi-organ involvement, not specifically peripheral infiltrates on chest X-ray.
• Option D. Loeffler's syndrome : Characterized by transient pulmonary infiltrates and blood eosinophilia, often due to parasitic infections, and usually resolves within a few weeks without leading to chronic symptoms.
• Educational Objective:
• Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia (CEP) is identified by peripheral infiltrates on chest imaging and marked eosinophilia in the blood. It presents with a history of asthma, progressive dyspnea, weight loss, night sweats, and cough. This condition is distinguished from acute eosinophilic pneumonia, hypereosinophilic syndrome, and Loeffler's syndrome based on the specific clinical features, imaging findings, and chronicity of symptoms.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old man presents with cough, dyspnea, fatigue, weight loss, and splenomegaly. Blood tests reveal marked eosinophilia and elevated white blood cell count. Chest X-ray shows diffuse infiltrates. Bone marrow biopsy shows increased cellularity with eosinophil precursors and myeloid hyperplasia. What is the next step ?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) chest: While it provides detailed imaging of lung pathology, it does not identify specific genetic mutations needed for diagnosing MPN-HES.
• Option C. Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL): Useful for diagnosing infections and certain interstitial lung diseases but not for identifying genetic mutations associated with MPN-HES.
• Option D. Lung biopsy: While it can provide histopathological information, it is invasive and does not identify specific genetic mutations crucial for diagnosing MPN-HES.
• Educational Objective:
• For patients with marked eosinophilia and bone marrow findings suggestive of hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES), FISH testing for specific genetic mutations is essential to confirm the diagnosis and guide treatment

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old man presents with a chronic cough, wheezing, and night sweats. Blood tests reveal marked eosinophilia. Chest X-ray shows diffuse miliary nodules. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia: Typically presents with peripheral infiltrates on chest imaging, not miliary nodules.
• Option B. Tuberculosis : Miliary tuberculosis can present with diffuse miliary nodules, but it is not typically associated with marked eosinophilia.
• Option D. Loeffler's syndrome: Characterized by transient pulmonary infiltrates and blood eosinophilia, often due to parasitic infections, but typically resolves within a few weeks and does not present with chronic symptoms or miliary nodules.
• Educational Objective:
• Tropical pulmonary eosinophilia (TPE) is characterized by chronic cough, wheezing, night sweats, and marked eosinophilia. It is caused by an immunological reaction to filarial parasites and commonly presents with persistent infiltrates and diffuse miliary nodules on chest X-ray. This condition is distinguished from chronic eosinophilic pneumonia, tuberculosis, and Loeffler's syndrome based on the combination of eosinophilia, respiratory symptoms, and characteristic radiographic findings.

subject: Medicine
question: A 30-year-old male smoker presents with chronic cough and dyspnea. He reports unintentional weight loss and episodes of spontaneous pneumothorax. High-resolution CT of the chest shows numerous thin-walled cysts and nodules predominantly in the upper lobes. Lung biopsy reveals characteristic tennis racquet like granules on electron microscopy. What is the likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis (PLCH)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM): Typically affects women of childbearing age and is characterized by diffuse thin-walled cysts but not associated with Birbeck granules.
• Option C. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF ): Presents with progressive fibrosis and honeycombing on imaging, not thin-walled cysts and nodules.
• Option D. Sarcoidosis: Characterized by bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and non-caseating granulomas, not thin-walled cysts and Birbeck granules.
• Educational Objective:
