• Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis (PLCH) is a smoking-related interstitial lung disease characterized by numerous thin-walled cysts and nodules predominantly in the upper lobes and presence of Birbeck granules on electron microscopy.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 70-year-old non-smoker male with progressive dry cough, SOB, and NYHA Class III presents with fine bibasilar crackles and no clubbing. HRCT is shown. Which investigation is required for further evaluation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/screenshot-2024-06-20-133026.png]
options: Laboratory testing for connective tissue disease

explanation.short: Ans. B) Laboratory testing for connective tissue disease
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Measurement of serum precipitating antibodies: Typically used to diagnose hypersensitivity pneumonitis, which is less likely given the lack of exposure history.
• Option C. Transbronchial biopsy: Can be helpful in certain interstitial lung diseases but may not provide sufficient tissue for a definitive diagnosis of NSIP or an underlying CTD.
• Option D. Surgical lung biopsy : Provides more tissue for diagnosis but is more invasive. It may be considered if initial non-invasive tests do not yield a diagnosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Laboratory testing for connective tissue diseases is essential when HRCT shows features suggestive of Non Specific Interstitial Pneumonia (NSIP) to identify any underlying autoimmune conditions.

subject: Medicine
question: A 60-year-old female is referred for chronic cough and dyspnea with exertion. Over the past year, her symptoms have steadily worsened. Pulmonary function testing shows FVC: 72%, FEV1: 80%, DLCO: 38%. HRCT of the chest is shown. Lung biopsy shows foci of scarring dispersed and surrounded by healthy lung parenchyma and fibroblasts. Which of the following is the appropriate therapy for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Bosentan: Primarily used to treat pulmonary arterial hypertension, not idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
• Option C. Acetylcysteine : An antioxidant used in the management of various conditions, but not first-line for IPF.
• Option D. Prednisolone/azathioprine: Immunosuppressive therapy that has not shown benefit and may even be harmful in patients with IPF.
• Educational Objective:
• Pirfenidone is the appropriate therapy for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), as it helps to slow disease progression.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old woman with suspected sarcoidosis presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and distal renal tubular acidosis (dRTA). Which of the following statements about the disease is false?
options: ACE is elevated in 60% of cases
Hypercalcemia occurs in about 10% of patients
BAL CD4/CD8 ratio > 3.5 is a characteristic finding
Lung biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. ACE is elevated in 60% of cases : Elevated angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) levels are seen in about 60% of patients with sarcoidosis.
• Option B. Hypercalcemia occurs in about 10% of patients : Hypercalcemia occurs in approximately 10% of patients with sarcoidosis due to increased production of vitamin D by granulomas.
• Option C. BAL CD4/CD8 ratio > 3.5 is a characteristic finding : A high CD4/CD8 ratio in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid is a characteristic finding in sarcoidosis.
• Educational Objective:
• While lung biopsy showing non-caseating granulomas is highly suggestive of sarcoidosis and can be critical in diagnosis, it is not considered the gold standard as the diagnosis often relies on a combination of clinical, radiographic, and histopathological findings. Other conditions can also present with non-caseating granulomas, necessitating clinical correlation and exclusion of other diseases for accurate diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old male with a history of atrial fibrillation presents with progressive dyspnea and a dry cough over the past three months. He has been on amiodarone therapy for the past year. High-resolution CT of the chest reveals findings consistent with interstitial lung disease (ILD). All of the following medications are known to cause ILD except:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Amiodarone: Known to cause ILD, particularly with long-term use.
• Option B. Methotrexate: Associated with ILD in patients, particularly those with rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option C. Bleomycin: A chemotherapeutic agent well known for its potential to cause ILD
• Educational Objective :
• Amiodarone, methotrexate, and bleomycin are known to cause drug-induced interstitial lung disease (ILD), while cisplatin is not typically associated with ILD; its primary toxicities are nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old male with a 30-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnea and chronic cough. High-resolution CT of the chest shows diffuse interstitial changes. Which of the following interstitial lung diseases is least likely to be associated with smoking?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Respiratory bronchiolitis associated ILD: Commonly seen in smokers.
• Option B. Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia: Strongly associated with smoking.
• Option C. Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis: Typically seen in smokers and characterized by cystic lung disease.
• Educational objective :
• Usual Interstitial Pneumonia is least likely to be associated with smoking.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old man with a history of working in a construction agency involved in rock tunnelling presents with acute onset of dyspnea, cough, and fatigue for the last 3 weeks. High-resolution CT shows diffuse ground-glass opacities and a crazy paving pattern. Bronchoalveolar lavage reveals large amounts of periodic acid-Schiff-positive material. Which of the following conditions is most likely ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP): Although PAP can present with similar imaging findings, the patient's occupational history and acute onset suggest acute silicosis.
• Option C. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF): Typically presents with chronic progressive dyspnea and honeycombing on imaging, not acute symptoms and crazy paving pattern.
• Option D. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis: Related to environmental exposures, presenting with cough, dyspnea, and fatigue, but typically involves centrilobular nodules rather than crazy paving pattern
• Educational Objective :
• Acute silicosis can present with diffuse ground-glass opacities and a crazy paving pattern on high-resolution CT, and bronchoalveolar lavage may reveal large amounts of periodic acid-Schiff-positive material, reflecting occupational exposure to silica dust.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old man with a long history of working in construction presents with chronic cough and shortness of breath. He reports joint pain and stiffness, and a recent chest X-ray shows interstitial lung disease (ILD). Which of the following conditions is least likely to be associated with his diagnosis of silicosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Interstitial lung disease (ILD): Silicosis is a form of ILD caused by inhalation of silica dust.
• Option B. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD): Long-term exposure to silica can lead to COPD.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis: Silicosis can be associated with rheumatoid arthritis in the form of Caplan's syndrome.
• Educational Objective:
• Silicosis is primarily associated with interstitial lung disease (ILD) and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) due to inhalation of silica dust and can also be associated with autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, but not with lymphoma.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old man with a history of working in shipyards presents with chest pain and shortness of breath. Imaging reveals a pleural mass, and biopsy confirms malignant mesothelioma. Which of the following is true regarding the association of mesotheliomas with asbestos exposure?
options: Mesotheliomas are strongly associated with smoking
Short-term asbestos exposures of ≤1–2 years can lead to mesotheliomas
Mesotheliomas are not associated with asbestos exposure
Mesotheliomas develop within a few years of exposure to asbestos

explanation.short: Ans. B) Short-term asbestos exposures of ≤1–2 years can lead to mesotheliomas
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Mesotheliomas are strongly associated with smoking: Mesotheliomas are not associated with smoking.
• Option C. Mesotheliomas are not associated with asbestos exposure: Mesotheliomas are indeed associated with asbestos exposure.
• Option D. Mesotheliomas develop within a few years of exposure to asbestos: Mesotheliomas can develop decades after exposure to asbestos.
• Educational Objective:
• Mesotheliomas are strongly associated with asbestos exposure and can develop even after short-term exposures of ≤1–2 years, occurring up to 40 years in the past. They are not associated with smoking.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 65-year-old retired construction worker presents for a routine check-up. His chest radiograph is shown. What does it indicate?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/06/20/screenshot-2024-06-20-124125.png]

explanation.short: Ans. B) Past exposure to asbestos
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Significant pulmonary impairment: Pleural plaques indicate exposure but do not imply pulmonary impairment without additional manifestations.
• Option C. Pleural effusion : Benign pleural effusions can occur with asbestos exposure but are not seen in this CXR
• Option D. Active tuberculosis infection: Pleural plaques are not associated with tuberculosis (but silicosis has association)
• Educational Objective:
• Chest X-ray showing bilateral calcified pleural plaques indicates past exposure to asbestos. Pleural plaques are a hallmark of asbestos exposure but do not imply significant pulmonary impairment without additional findings.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old woman presents with acute onset of severe dyspnea and fever. She was previously healthy but has rapidly deteriorated over the past week. Chest X-ray shows diffuse bilateral alveolar infiltrates, and HRCT of the chest reveals widespread ground-glass opacities and consolidation. Routine work up did not reveal any identifiable cause. Lung biopsy demonstrates diffuse alveolar damage with hyaline membrane formation. What is the likely diagnosis ?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS): While ARDS also presents with diffuse alveolar damage and similar imaging findings, it is typically secondary to an identifiable cause such as sepsis, trauma, or pneumonia, whereas AIP occurs without a known trigger.
• Option C. Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP) : Usually presents with subacute symptoms and patchy lung infiltrates, not rapid onset and diffuse alveolar damage.
• Option D. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF): Characterized by chronic progressive dyspnea and fibrosis with honeycombing, not acute onset or diffuse alveolar damage.
• Educational Objective:
• Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) is characterized by rapid onset of severe respiratory symptoms, diffuse bilateral alveolar infiltrates, and lung biopsy showing diffuse alveolar damage with hyaline membrane formation.

subject: Medicine
question: During a temporal artery biopsy for suspected giant cell arteritis, which of the following factors is MOST important for improving diagnostic yield?
options: Obtaining a biopsy sample from the most tender area of the artery
Using a large bore needle for biopsy
Obtaining a biopsy segment of at least 3-5 cm in length
Performing the biopsy under general anesthesia

explanation.short: Ans. C) Obtaining a biopsy segment of at least 3-5 cm in length
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Obtaining a biopsy sample from the most tender area of the artery: While tenderness might indicate inflammation, a longer segment is more important for sampling.
• Option B. Using a large bore needle for biopsy: The needle size is less relevant than the length of the biopsy specimen.
• Option D. Performing the biopsy under general anesthesia: General anesthesia is not typically required.
• Educational Objective
• Adequate biopsy length is essential for accurate diagnosis of GCA. While other factors like tenderness or imaging findings can guide the biopsy site, the length of the specimen is the most critical determinant of diagnostic yield.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of headaches, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness. She has been taking low-dose prednisone for polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) for the past 5 days. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discontinue prednisone and observe for symptom resolution

explanation.short: Ans. C) Perform a temporal artery biopsy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Discontinue prednisone and observe for symptom resolution: While low-dose prednisone may be used to manage polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR), it's not sufficient to treat giant cell arteritis (GCA). Discontinuing it without a definitive diagnosis could lead to worsening symptoms and potentially irreversible vision loss.
• Option B. Order a temporal artery ultrasound: While ultrasound can be a helpful tool in the assessment of GCA, it is not as definitive as a biopsy. A negative ultrasound doesn't rule out GCA, and a positive result still requires biopsy confirmation.
• Option D. Increase the dose of prednisone: Increasing the dose of prednisone without a biopsy-confirmed diagnosis of GCA is not recommended. Higher doses of prednisone carry a significant risk of side effects, and it's important to establish the diagnosis first to determine the appropriate treatment course.
• Educational Objective
• Early diagnosis and treatment of GCA are crucial to prevent complications like vision loss. Even if a patient is already on glucocorticoids, a biopsy should not be delayed, as it is the gold standard for confirming the diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old woman with newly diagnosed temporal arteritis complains of blurred vision in one eye. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Continue with oral prednisone 40 mg daily
Increase the dose of oral prednisone to 60 mg daily
Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 3 days
Refer urgently to ophthalmology for assessment

explanation.short: Ans. C) Administer intravenous methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 3 days
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Continue with oral prednisone 40 mg daily: Oral prednisone is the standard treatment for temporal arteritis, but higher doses or IV steroids are needed for ocular involvement.
• Option D. Refer urgently to ophthalmology for assessment: Ophthalmology referral is essential, but not the immediate first step
• Educational Objective
• Prompt recognition and treatment of visual symptoms in temporal arteritis are critical to preserve vision. High-dose intravenous glucocorticoids are the mainstay of initial therapy for ocular involvement.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old woman presents with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following medications is FDA approved for the treatment of giant cell arteritis (GCA)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Methotrexate and Option B. Rituximab are not FDA-approved for GCA.
• Option D. Infliximab is a TNF inhibitor and is not used in GCA.
• Educational Objective
• While glucocorticoids remain the first-line treatment for GCA, tocilizumab is an important alternative for patients who cannot tolerate glucocorticoids or who experience relapses despite glucocorticoid therapy. It is essential to be aware of the available treatment options for GCA to provide optimal care for patients.

subject: Medicine
question: A 30-year-old woman presents with fatigue, weight loss, and claudication in her arms and legs. Physical exam reveals diminished pulses in the upper extremities and a carotid bruit. Which of the following imaging modalities is the GOLD STANDARD for confirming the diagnosis of Takayasu arteritis?
options: Doppler ultrasound of the carotid arteries

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chest X-ray: Chest X-ray is not sensitive enough to detect early vascular changes.
• Option B. Doppler ultrasound of the carotid arteries: Doppler ultrasound can assess blood flow but is limited in visualizing the arterial wall in detail.
• Option D. Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA): MRA is a good alternative to CT angiography, but CT is often more readily available.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the gold standard imaging modality for TAK is essential for timely diagnosis and appropriate management. While other imaging modalities can provide supportive evidence, CT angiography remains the most definitive diagnostic tool.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old Asian woman presents with fatigue, weight loss, and arm claudication. Physical exam reveals a blood pressure discrepancy between arms and diminished brachial pulses. Which of the following diagnoses is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Giant cell arteritis: Typically affects older individuals and involves cranial arteries.
• Option B. Polyarteritis nodosa: Affects medium-sized arteries and presents with multisystem involvement.
• Option C. Kawasaki disease: Kawasaki disease is a childhood vasculitis.
• Educational Objective
• Takayasu arteritis is a large vessel vasculitis that predominantly affects young women, particularly those of Asian descent. It is important to consider this diagnosis in patients presenting with characteristic clinical features and to initiate prompt evaluation and treatment to prevent complications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old man with a history of hepatitis B presents with fever, weight loss, and abdominal pain. He also complains of new-onset muscle weakness and skin nodules. Which of the following diagnostic tests would have the HIGHEST diagnostic yield for polyarteritis nodosa (PAN)?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Biopsy of a skin nodule
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) testing: ANCA is usually negative in PAN.
• Option C. Abdominal ultrasound: Ultrasound might show organ ischemia, but not the underlying vasculitis.
• Option D. Electromyography (EMG): EMG can assess muscle involvement but is not diagnostic
• Educational Objective
• When suspecting PAN, a tissue biopsy of an affected organ (such as skin, nerve, or muscle) is the most reliable diagnostic test. While other tests can provide supportive evidence, biopsy provides definitive proof of vasculitis

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old man with a recent diagnosis of polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) is most likely to present with which of the following renal manifestations?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Renal failure and hypertension
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Nephrotic syndrome : This is characterized by heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia. It's not the typical presentation of renal involvement in PAN.
• Option B. Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN): While RPGN can cause renal failure, it's less common in PAN and usually associated with other vasculitides like microscopic polyangiitis or granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
• Option D. Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stones): Kidney stones are not directly related to the vasculitic process of PAN
• Educational Objective
• PAN commonly affects the kidneys, leading to hypertension and renal failure. Recognizing this association is crucial for early diagnosis and appropriate management to prevent irreversible kidney damage.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 3-year-old child presents with a 5-day history of high fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, erythema of the oral mucosa and lips, and cervical lymphadenopathy. The child’s tongue is shown below. Which of the following diagnoses is most likely?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/07/29/screenshot-2024-07-29-174133.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Scarlet fever typically presents with a sandpaper-like rash and sore throat.
• Option B. Measles has a characteristic maculopapular rash and Koplik spots in the mouth.
• Option D. Infectious mononucleosis is associated with fatigue, lymphadenopathy, and atypical lymphocytes.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the characteristic clinical features of Kawasaki disease is crucial for timely diagnosis and treatment to prevent complications, such as coronary artery aneurysms.

subject: Medicine
question: A 4-year-old child with Kawasaki disease is found to have coronary artery aneurysms on echocardiogram. Which of the following histopathological findings would most likely be present in the affected coronary arteries?
options: Intimal proliferation and infiltration of mononuclear cells

explanation.short: Ans. C) Intimal proliferation and infiltration of mononuclear cells
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Granulomatous inflammation with giant cells: Granulomatous inflammation is seen in conditions like giant cell arteritis.
• Option B. Transmural inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis: Transmural inflammation with fibrinoid necrosis is seen in polyarteritis nodosa.
• Option D. Medial calcification and fibrosis: Medial calcification and fibrosis are more typical of atherosclerosis.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the pathological changes in coronary arteries caused by Kawasaki disease is important for recognizing the potential for long-term cardiovascular complications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 7-year-old boy with a history of Kawasaki disease presents with chest pain and signs of myocardial ischemia. Echocardiogram reveals giant coronary artery aneurysms with evidence of thrombosis. Which of the following surgical interventions is MOST likely to be considered for this patient?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Thromboendarterectomy and aneurysmal reconstruction
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Coronary artery stenting: Stenting is less suitable for giant aneurysms.
• Option B. Pericardiectomy: Pericardiectomy is the removal of the pericardium and is not relevant to coronary artery disease.
• Option D. Cardiac transplantation: Cardiac transplantation is a last resort for severe, refractory cases.
• Educational Objective
• Timely surgical intervention is crucial for managing giant coronary artery aneurysms in Kawasaki disease to prevent life-threatening complications like myocardial infarction and sudden cardiac death.

subject: Medicine
question: A 3-year-old boy presents with fever, rash, conjunctival injection, and swollen glands, raising suspicion for Kawasaki disease. What is the most likely initial dose of aspirin that would be prescribed by the doctor?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. 10 mg/kg/day: This is a low dose of aspirin and would not be sufficient for the anti-inflammatory effect needed in acute Kawasaki disease.
• Option B. 20 mg/kg/day: This dose is also too low for the initial treatment of Kawasaki disease.
• Option C. 50 mg/kg/day: This dose may be appropriate for some children, but the standard initial dose is generally higher.
• Educational objective :
• High-dose aspirin (80-100 mg/kg/day) is the standard initial treatment for Kawasaki disease due to its anti-inflammatory and antipyretic (fever-reducing) effects. This higher dose is crucial during the acute phase of the illness to control inflammation and potentially reduce the risk of coronary artery complications. Once the fever subsides, the aspirin dose is typically tapered down to a lower dose (3-5 mg/kg/day) for its antiplatelet effect.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with a history of recurrent sinusitis and chronic cough now presents with bloody nasal discharge and a new saddle nose deformity. Which additional finding would MOST strongly support a diagnosis of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Positive cytoplasmic antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (c-ANCA)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Positive rheumatoid factor and Option B. Elevated antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer are not specific for GPA.
• Option D. Eosinophilia on complete blood count (CBC): Eosinophilia is more characteristic of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA).
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the significance of c-ANCA positivity in the context of suspected GPA is crucial for early diagnosis and prompt initiation of treatment to prevent irreversible organ damage.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements about renal involvement in GPA is FALSE?
options: It is the most common cause of mortality in untreated GPA
It typically presents with rapidly progressive renal failure
It is often associated with pulmonary hemorrhage
It can be effectively treated with immunosuppressive therapy

explanation.short: Ans. C) It is often associated with pulmonary hemorrhage.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The other statements are true regarding renal involvement in GPA.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the patterns of organ involvement in GPA is important for accurate diagnosis and management. While renal involvement is a serious complication, it is not typically associated with pulmonary hemorrhage.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with GPA presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 3 days

explanation.short: Ans. D) Intravenous methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily for 3 days
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day: Oral prednisone is too slow and less potent for this scenario.
• Option B. Methotrexate 25 mg/week: Methotrexate is a DMARD and not appropriate for acute life-threatening disease.
• Option C. Rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 weeks: Rituximab is an option, but high-dose glucocorticoids are typically used first.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the need for rapid intervention with high-dose glucocorticoids in patients with life-threatening manifestations of GPA is essential for optimal management and improved outcomes. Early diagnosis and aggressive treatment can significantly reduce morbidity and mortality associated with this disease.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient presents with asthma, eosinophilia, and a skin biopsy revealing a necrotizing vasculitis with extravascular granulomas. Which of the following histopathological findings is most characteristic of EGPA?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Granulomas with eosinophilic infiltration
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Leukocytoclastic vasculitis: This type of vasculitis is characterized by the fragmentation of neutrophils (leukocytoclasis) in small blood vessel walls. While it can be seen in EGPA, it is not specific and can occur in other vasculitides as well.
• Option B. Fibrinoid necrosis without granulomas: Fibrinoid necrosis, a type of tissue damage, is often present in various vasculitides, including EGPA. However, the absence of granulomas makes this finding less specific for EGPA.
• Option D. Lymphocytic vasculitis: This refers to vasculitis with predominantly lymphocytic infiltration. While lymphocytes may be present in EGPA lesions, the predominance of eosinophils and the presence of granulomas are more characteristic features.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the unique histological pattern of granulomas with eosinophils is crucial for diagnosing EGPA. This finding, combined with clinical and serological evidence, can confirm the diagnosis and guide treatment decisions.

subject: Medicine
question: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) can affect multiple organ systems. What is the approximate percentage of EGPA patients who experience mononeuritis multiplex (MM), the second most common manifestation of this disease?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Understanding the prevalence of MM in EGPA is essential for recognizing potential neurological complications and initiating appropriate management. Early diagnosis and treatment can prevent irreversible nerve damage.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old man with a history of asthma and eosinophilia presents with new-onset shortness of breath, chest pain, and fatigue. He has recently been diagnosed with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA). An echocardiogram reveals significant LV dysfunction and evidence of myocarditis. Which of the following treatment regimens is most appropriate for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Oral prednisone alone: Oral prednisone alone is insufficient for severe disease.
• Option B. Methotrexate and prednisone: Methotrexate is less potent than cyclophosphamide.
• Option D. Rituximab and prednisone: Rituximab is an option for refractory disease or in those who cannot tolerate cyclophosphamide.
• Educational Objective
• Severe EGPA, especially with cardiac involvement, requires aggressive treatment to achieve remission and prevent complications. Cyclophosphamide and prednisone are the mainstay of initial therapy, but other immunosuppressive agents like rituximab may be considered in refractory cases or when cyclophosphamide is contraindicated.

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old woman presents with a purpuric rash, hematuria, and hemoptysis. Renal biopsy reveals a pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis. Which of the following serological markers is MOST likely to be positive in this patient?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Perinuclear antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (p-ANCA)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Anti- Glomerular Basement Membrane (GBM) antibodies: These are characteristic of Goodpasture syndrome, which usually presents with pulmonary hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.
• Option B. Antinuclear antibodies (ANA): While ANA can be positive in some vasculitides, it is not specific for MPA.
• Option D. Anti- Proteinase 3(PR3) antibodies: These are cytoplasmic ANCA (c-ANCA) and are more specific for granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA).
• Educational Objective
• Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) is a small vessel vasculitis characterized by kidney inflammation, lung involvement, skin involvement, and possible involvement of other organs.Testing p-ANCA, especially against myeloperoxidase (MPO), is a key diagnostic marker for MPA, found in about 70% of patients.

subject: Medicine
question: A 4-year-old child presents with a purpuric rash on the lower extremities and buttocks, abdominal pain, and joint swelling. Which of the following diagnostic approaches would be MOST helpful in confirming a diagnosis of IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura)?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Skin biopsy with immunofluorescence
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) testing: ANA is typically negative in IgA vasculitis, and a positive result would suggest a different autoimmune condition.
• Option B. Serum IgA levels: While IgA levels may be elevated in IgA vasculitis, they are not specific for the disease and can be elevated in other conditions.
• Option D. Abdominal ultrasound: Abdominal ultrasound can help assess for gastrointestinal complications (e.g., intussusception) in IgA vasculitis, but it does not confirm the diagnosis.
• Educational Objective
• While other tests may provide supportive evidence, a skin biopsy with immunofluorescence remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old man with a history of Hepatitis C presents with fatigue, arthralgia, palpable purpura on the lower extremities, and peripheral neuropathy. Laboratory tests reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and low complement levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Type I cryoglobulinemia : Type I is associated with lymphoproliferative disorders.
• Option C. Type III cryoglobulinemia: Type III is associated with autoimmune diseases.
• Option D. Type IV cryoglobulinemia : There is no type IV cryoglobulinemia.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the association between Hepatitis C and type II cryoglobulinemia is crucial for accurate diagnosis and management of cryoglobulinemic vasculitis.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with hepatitis C-associated cryoglobulinemic vasculitis has achieved sustained virologic response (SVR) after antiviral therapy. Which of the following is the most likely outcome regarding their vasculitis?
options: Partial improvement with decreased cryoglobulin levels

explanation.short: Ans. D) Partial improvement with decreased cryoglobulin levels
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. No improvement in vasculitis activity: Achieving SVR often leads to significant improvement in symptoms and reduction of cryoglobulin levels.
• Option B. Worsening of vasculitis: While rare, there have been some cases where antiviral therapy can lead to a temporary worsening of vasculitis followed by improvement, and overall, antiviral therapy is expected to be beneficial.
• Option C. Complete resolution of vasculitis: While SVR often leads to significant improvement in vasculitis, complete resolution may not always occur.
• Educational Objective
• Antiviral therapy is essential for managing hepatitis C-associated cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. While SVR is associated with improved outcomes, patients may still require additional immunosuppressive therapy or other interventions to achieve complete remission of vasculitis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 34-year-old woman presents with recurrent, long-lasting wheals on her trunk and limbs, accompanied by joint pain and abdominal discomfort. The lesions often leave a bruise-like mark. Laboratory tests reveal normal C3 and C4 complement levels. Which of the following autoantibodies is most likely to be elevated in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA): While ANA can be positive in some cases of HUV, it is not specific and can be found in other autoimmune diseases.
• Option B. Anti- double stranded DNA (dsDNA) antibody: This antibody is more specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
• Option D. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA): ANCA is associated with other vasculitides, like granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) or microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), not urticarial vasculitis.
• Educational Objective
• Hypocomplementemic Urticarial Vasculitis (HUV):
• Characterized by urticarial lesions lasting >24 hours, leaving bruise-like marks, and systemic symptoms like joint pain and abdominal discomfort. Laboratory findings may show low complement levels, but normal C3 and C4 levels can occur. Anti-C1q antibodies are highly associated with HUV and considered a diagnostic marker.

subject: Medicine
question: A 25-year-old male presents with recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers. He also has a history of erythema nodosum. Which of the following tests is most likely to be positive and aid in the diagnosis of Behçet syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) , Option C. Rheumatoid factor (RF) , and Option D. Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) tests are not specific for Behçet's and are more commonly associated with other autoimmune diseases.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the utility of the pathergy test is essential in the diagnosis of Behçet disease. While it is not pathognomonic, a positive pathergy test, along with the characteristic clinical manifestations, can strongly support the diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 32-year-old man of Turkish descent presents with recurrent oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and uveitis. You suspect Behçet disease. Which of the following statements regarding HLA-B*51 testing in this patient is MOST accurate?
options: HLA-B51 testing is the gold standard for diagnosing Behçet disease.
A positive HLA-B51 test confirms the diagnosis of Behçet disease.
A negative HLA-B51 test rules out the diagnosis of Behçet disease.
HLA-B51 testing can be helpful, but it's not definitive for diagnosis due to its prevalence in the general population.

explanation.short: Ans. D) HLA-B*51 testing can be helpful, but it's not definitive for diagnosis due to its prevalence in the general population.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Options A. HLA-B51 testing is the gold standard for diagnosing Behçet disease and B. A positive HLA-B51 test confirms the diagnosis of Behçet disease: HLA-B*51 testing is not the gold standard for diagnosis and does not confirm the diagnosis on its own.
• Option C. A negative HLA-B51 test rules out the diagnosis of Behçet disease: A negative HLA-B51 test does not exclude the diagnosis of Behçet disease, as some patients with the disease are HLA-B51 negative.
• Educational Objective
• HLA-B*51 testing should be interpreted with caution and in the context of the overall clinical picture. A positive test can increase suspicion for Behçet disease, but it does not confirm or rule out the diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 38-year-old woman with Behçet syndrome presents with recurrent, painful oral ulcers that have not responded to topical corticosteroids. She has no significant comorbidities. Which of the following medications is FDA-approved for the treatment of oral ulcers in Behçet syndrome and would be the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Colchicine: Primarily used for acute gout flares and sometimes for erythema nodosum in Behçet's, but not specifically approved for oral ulcers.
• Option B. Azathioprine: An immunosuppressant used for various autoimmune diseases, but not the first-line treatment for oral ulcers in Behçet's.
• Option D. Cyclophosphamide: A potent immunosuppressant used for severe manifestations of Behçet's (e.g., ocular or neurological involvement), not typically the first choice for isolated oral ulcers.
• Educational Objective
• Apremilast is a relatively new treatment option for managing oral ulcers in Behçet syndrome. It offers a targeted approach with a favorable safety profile compared to more traditional immunosuppressive therapies.

subject: Medicine
question: A 25-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of photosensitivity, malar rash, and recurrent episodes of mucositis. He also reports headaches, seizures, and difficulty concentrating. Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) testing is positive, and anti-dsDNA antibodies are present. A kidney biopsy reveals signs of lupus nephritis. A mutation analysis of which of the following genes is most likely to be informative, regarding the likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. TLR7: Gain-of-function mutations in TLR7 are associated with SLE, but they typically lead to adult-onset disease with a milder phenotype, not the early-onset and severe neuropsychiatric manifestations seen in this patient.
• Option B. IRF5: Variants in IRF5 increase susceptibility to SLE, but they are not typically associated with monogenic forms or severe neuropsychiatric presentations.
• Option D. TNFSF4: Mutations in TNFSF4 are linked to an increased risk of SLE, but they do not usually cause the specific combination of early-onset, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and lupus nephritis seen in this case.
• Educational Objective
• Monogenic lupus: A small percentage of SLE cases are caused by single gene mutations, with TREX1 being one of the most well-characterized. Patients with monogenic lupus often present with early-onset disease and may have more severe manifestations, including neuropsychiatric involvement. Identifying a TREX1 mutation can confirm the diagnosis of monogenic lupus and has implications for prognosis and treatment.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with pain, swelling, and tenderness in multiple joints, particularly her wrists and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Physical exam reveals warmth and tenderness in the affected joints, but no obvious deformities. On further question, patient used to experience photosensitive rash usually occurring over the cheeks. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rheumatoid arthritis typically presents with more persistent morning stiffness and may cause joint erosions over time.
• Option B. Osteoarthritis primarily affects weight-bearing joints and is characterized by bony enlargement and crepitus.
• Option C. Psoriatic arthritis is often associated with skin psoriasis and typically involves the distal interphalangeal joints.
• Educational Objective
• SLE-associated polyarthritis is a common manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), an autoimmune disease. It is important to recognize the clinical presentation of SLE-associated polyarthritis, which can include pain, swelling, and tenderness in multiple joints, along with other systemic symptoms like fatigue, fever, and rash.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with a circular, erythematous lesion on her cheek with a raised, scaly border and a depigmented, atrophic center. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Tinea corporis (Ringworm): While ringworm can present as a circular lesion, it typically has a more active, inflamed border and central clearing, often with itching. The description of depigmentation and atrophy doesn't fit this diagnosis.
• Option B. Psoriasis: Psoriasis lesions are typically well-demarcated plaques with silvery scales, and they don't usually present with the same degree of central atrophy as seen in DLE.
• Option D. Seborrheic dermatitis: This condition presents as greasy, yellowish scales and erythema, usually affecting areas rich in sebaceous glands like the scalp, eyebrows, and nasolabial folds. The depigmentation and atrophy seen in this patient are not characteristic of seborrheic dermatitis.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the characteristic features of DLE lesions is important for differentiating it from other skin conditions and initiating appropriate treatment.

subject: Medicine
question: What is the approximate percentage of patients with DPGN who either die or develop ESRD within 10 years of diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• DPGN is a severe form of lupus nephritis with a poor prognosis. Early recognition and aggressive treatment are essential to improve outcomes.

subject: Medicine
question: A renal biopsy from a patient with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) shows diffuse mesangial hypercellularity with mesangial immune deposits on immunofluorescence. There is no evidence of glomerular crescents or endocapillary proliferation. This finding is consistent with which class of lupus nephritis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Class I LN has normal glomeruli on light microscopy.
• Option C. Class III LN involves focal segmental or global lesions.
• Option D. Class IV-S(A) involves diffuse segmental proliferative lesions.
• Educational Objective
• Classifying lupus nephritis is crucial for determining prognosis and guiding treatment decisions.

subject: Medicine
question: A 38-year-old woman with SLE presents with fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and orthopnea. Physical examination reveals an S3 gallop, jugular venous distension, and bilateral lower extremity edema. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pericardial effusion can cause similar symptoms, but the S3 gallop and bilateral edema are more suggestive of left ventricular dysfunction.
• Option B. Mitral valve regurgitation can also cause heart failure, but it's less common in SLE.
• Option C. Pulmonary hypertension can cause right heart failure, but the patient's symptoms and signs are more suggestive of left heart failure
• Educational Objective
• SLE can affect the heart in various ways, including pericarditis, myocarditis, and valvular disease. Recognizing these cardiac manifestations is essential for early intervention and preventing complications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old woman with SLE presents with fatigue, pallor, and jaundice. Laboratory tests reveal anemia, elevated reticulocyte count, and a positive direct Coombs test. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Hydroxychloroquine is used for SLE management but not as the primary treatment for AIHA.
• Option B. Rituximab is a second-line agent for refractory AIHA.
• Option D. Platelet transfusion is not indicated for AIHA unless there is severe thrombocytopenia with bleeding.
• Educational Objective
• AIHA is a common hematologic manifestation of SLE and requires prompt treatment to prevent complications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) presents with edema, fatigue, and foamy urine. Laboratory tests reveal proteinuria of 4.5 g/24 hours, hypoalbuminemia, and hyperlipidemia. What percentage of SLE patients with renal involvement typically develop nephrotic syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. 5-10%: This is the percentage of SLE patients who develop end-stage renal disease, not nephrotic syndrome.
• Option C. 30-60%: This is the percentage of SLE patients who develop renal involvement (proteinuria and cellular casts), not specifically nephrotic syndrome.
• Option D. 80-90%: This is an overestimation of the prevalence of nephrotic syndrome in SLE.
• Educational Objective
• Renal involvement is common in SLE, and a subset of these patients will develop nephrotic syndrome. Early detection and management are crucial for preventing kidney damage.

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old man with a history of SLE for 10 years presents to the emergency department complaining of sudden, painless vision loss in his left eye. He denies any recent trauma, eye pain, redness, or light sensitivity. On examination, his vision is 20/200 in the left eye and normal in the right eye. Funduscopic examination of the left eye reveals cotton wool spots, retinal hemorrhages, and a pale optic disc. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Sicca syndrome: While common in SLE, it mainly causes dryness, irritation, and blurred vision, not sudden, painless vision loss.
• Option B. Conjunctivitis: This is inflammation of the conjunctiva (the membrane lining the eyelids and covering the white part of the eye). It usually presents with redness, itching, tearing, and discharge, not sudden vision loss.
• Option C. Episcleritis: This is inflammation of the episclera (the thin layer of tissue between the conjunctiva and the sclera, the white outer coating of the eye). It typically causes mild redness and discomfort, not sudden vision loss.
• Educational Objective
• Retinal vasculitis is a serious complication of SLE and requires urgent evaluation and treatment to preserve vision.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old woman with a history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) presents to the hospital with acute confusion, disorientation, and new-onset seizures. Brain MRI reveals multiple white matter hyperintensities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Primary central nervous system ( CNS) lymphoma: While CNS lymphoma can occur in SLE patients, it is less common than NPSLE and typically presents with focal lesions rather than diffuse white matter hyperintensities.
• Option C. Multiple sclerosis: Multiple sclerosis can also present with white matter lesions on MRI, but the clinical history of SLE and other systemic symptoms makes it less likely in this case.
• Option D. Cerebral vasculitis: Cerebral vasculitis is a possible complication of SLE, but it usually presents with focal neurological deficits and less commonly with diffuse cognitive impairment. Additionally, MRI findings in cerebral vasculitis often show ischemic lesions rather than predominantly white matter hyperintensities.
• Educational Objective
• NPSLE is a common and potentially debilitating manifestation of SLE. Early recognition and treatment are essential to prevent long-term neurological damage.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 37-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of joint pain, photosensitive rash, and intermittent oral ulcers. Laboratory investigations were ordered to assess for autoimmune disorders. An Antinuclear Antibody (ANA) test was performed using indirect immunofluorescence on HEp-2 cells. The result is shown in the image below. Based on the pattern observed, which of the following conditions is most likely associated with this patient's presentation?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/07/27/screenshot-2024-07-27-162500.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA): RA may show a positive ANA with different patterns, but the homogeneous pattern is not as strongly associated with RA as it is with SLE.
• Option C. Scleroderma: Scleroderma ANA patterns often include nucleolar, centromere, or speckled patterns, not typically homogenous.
• Option D. Sjögren's Syndrome : Sjögren's Syndrome is more commonly associated with a speckled ANA pattern and anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the homogeneous pattern in ANA testing is important, as it strongly suggests the possibility of SLE. However, further testing, including specific antibody tests and clinical evaluation, is necessary for a definitive diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 36-year-old man is evaluated for symptoms of arthritis, fatigue, and an intermittent rash. Considering his clinical presentation, an Antinuclear Antibody (ANA) test is performed, and the results show a distinct immunofluorescence pattern, shown below. Which autoimmune disease is most closely associated with this ANA pattern?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/07/27/screenshot-2024-07-27-162710.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rheumatoid Arthritis is typically associated with rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies, not the 'rim' ANA pattern.
• Option C. Sjögren's Syndrome is more commonly associated with anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies, which can produce a different ANA pattern.
• Option D. Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma) often shows ANA patterns such as the nucleolar pattern, not the 'rim' pattern.
• Educational Objective
• Identifying the rim pattern in ANA testing is a valuable clue for diagnosing SLE. It signifies the presence of specific autoantibodies targeting dsDNA, a hallmark of the disease. However, it's important to remember that not all SLE patients will exhibit this pattern, and further diagnostic workup is necessary.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion for defining Lupus Low Disease Activity State (LLDAS)?
options: No new lupus disease activity compared to the previous visit

explanation.short: Ans. D) Anti-double-stranded DNA antibody titer < 1:80
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• The other options (A, B, and C) are all essential criteria for LLDAS.
• Educational Objective
• LLDAS is a state of low disease activity in SLE, defined by specific criteria including a SLEDAI-2K score ≤ 4, no new disease activity compared to the previous visit, and a physician's global assessment ≤ 1 (scale 0-3). Understanding these criteria is essential for monitoring disease activity and adjusting treatment plans in SLE patients.

subject: Medicine
question: A 25-year-old woman with severe lupus nephritis is scheduled to begin treatment with high-dose cyclophosphamide. She is concerned about the potential side effects of this medication, particularly the risk of ovarian failure. Which of the following medications can be used to reduce this risk?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The other option s do not protect against cyclophosphamide-induced ovarian failure.
• Educational Objective
• Cyclophosphamide is a potent immunosuppressive agent used in severe lupus nephritis, but it can cause premature ovarian failure. Co-administration of leuprolide can mitigate this risk and preserve fertility in young women.

subject: Medicine
question: A young woman with SLE presents with new confusion, seizures, and shortness of breath. Exam shows fever, rash, and fluid overload. Labs reveal worsening kidney function and blood counts. Urine analysis suggests nephrotic range proteinuria. Biopsy confirms class IV lupus nephritis. What is the typical initial dose of glucocorticoids?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• High-dose glucocorticoids are the cornerstone of initial therapy for severe SLE manifestations due to their rapid anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. However, their use should be balanced with the risk of side effects and tapered as soon as possible.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with SLE has a renal biopsy showing diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis (DPGN) classified as ISN/RPS Class IV. Which of the following is the most appropriate induction therapy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Hydroxychloroquine alone: Hydroxychloroquine is used for milder SLE manifestations.
• Option B. Low-dose prednisone alone: Low-dose prednisone is insufficient for severe nephritis.
• Option C. Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) alone: MMF can be used but is not the standard of care for induction in Class IV LN.
• Educational Objective
• Class IV lupus nephritis is the most severe form of lupus nephritis and requires aggressive induction therapy. The combination of glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide is the standard of care for induction, followed by maintenance therapy with mycophenolate mofetil or azathioprine.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old Caucasian man presents with fatigue, arthralgia, myalgia, and a low-grade fever. He has been taking hydralazine for hypertension for the past six months. Blood investigations showed Positive ANA. Given the likely diagnosis, which of the following antibodies will be present in this patient, in high titres?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Anti-dsDNA antibodies: These are more specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and are less likely to be elevated in DILE.
• Option B. Anti-Smith antibodies: While present in SLE, anti-Smith antibodies are not usually elevated in DILE.
• Option C. Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies: These antibodies are associated with both SLE and Sjögren's syndrome but are not a hallmark of DILE.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the association between anti-histone antibodies and DILE is crucial for accurate diagnosis. This is especially important when a patient presents with lupus-like symptoms while taking a medication known to trigger DILE.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with DILE induced by hydralazine has been successfully treated. Which of the following statements regarding prognosis is most accurate?
options: The patient has a high risk of developing systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
The patient is likely to have permanent organ damage.
The patient is expected to have complete resolution of symptoms upon drug withdrawal.
The patient will require lifelong immunosuppressive therapy.

explanation.short: Ans. C) The patient is expected to have complete resolution of symptoms upon drug withdrawal.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The risk of developing SLE is not increased after DILE. Permanent organ damage and lifelong immunosuppression are not usually necessary for DILE.
• Educational Objective
• The prognosis of DILE is generally good, especially with prompt recognition and discontinuation of the offending drug. Patients should be reassured that their symptoms are likely to resolve and long-term complications are rare. However, monitoring for recurrence of symptoms is important if the drug is reintroduced.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old man with a 5-year history of Raynaud's phenomenon presents with progressive skin thickening that is limited to his fingers, hands, and face. He also reports occasional episodes of facial and hand swelling. He offers no other complaints. Physical examination reveals sclerodactyly, telangiectasias on the face, and puffy fingers. Pulmonary function tests and echocardiogram are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (dcSSc) : This subtype involves skin thickening that extends proximal to the wrists and affects the trunk. Internal organ involvement is more common and occurs earlier in dcSSc compared to lcSSc.
• Option C.  Scleroderma sine scleroderma: This rare variant of SSc is characterized by Raynaud's phenomenon and internal organ involvement without significant skin thickening.
• Option D. Overlap syndrome: This refers to a combination of SSc with features of other connective tissue diseases like polymyositis, dermatomyositis, or systemic lupus erythematosus. There is no evidence of overlap in this case.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the distinct clinical features of lcSSc is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following antibodies is considered an SSc-specific autoantibody?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Anti-nuclear antibody ( ANA) : ANA is not specific to SSc and can be found in other autoimmune diseases.
• Option B. Anti-double stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) antibody : Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE.
• Option D. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody: Anti-CCP is specific for rheumatoid arthritis
• Educational Objective
• Identifying SSc-specific autoantibodies like anti-Scl-70 can aid in diagnosis, subtype classification, and prognostication in patients with suspected SSc.

subject: Medicine
question: A 42-year-old woman presents with Raynaud's phenomenon and progressive skin thickening of her hands. Which of the following haplotypes is most strongly associated with an increased risk of systemic sclerosis (SSc)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. HLA-DRB104:01 is associated with rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option B. HLA-DRB107:01 and Option D. HLA-DRB115:01 have not been consistently linked to SSc.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the genetic predisposition to SSc can help identify individuals at higher risk and potentially contribute to the development of targeted preventive strategies.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with suspected systemic sclerosis presents with a speckled pattern on antinuclear antibody (ANA) testing. Which autoantibody is most likely associated with this pattern and carries an increased risk of scleroderma renal crisis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Anti-RNA polymerase III antibody
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option ACA: Centromere pattern, limited SSc, lower renal crisis risk.
• Option Scl-70: Nucleolar/speckled nucleolar pattern, diffuse SSc, lower renal crisis risk.
• Option Anti-U3-RNP: Nucleolar pattern, overlap syndromes, less common in SSc, lower renal crisis risk
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the association between the speckled ANA pattern, anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies, and scleroderma renal crisis is crucial for early detection and prompt intervention to prevent renal failure.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of Raynaud's phenomenon and new onset skin tightening of the fingers. Which of the following investigations is MOST helpful in differentiating between primary Raynaud's disease and limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (lcSSc)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) test: ANA testing can be positive in both conditions but is not specific for differentiation.
• Option B. Pulmonary function tests ( PFTs) and Option D. Echocardiogram assess for internal organ involvement, which is more common in later stages of SSc and not helpful in early differentiation.
• Educational Objective
• Nailfold capillaroscopy is a valuable tool in the early diagnosis of SSc, particularly in patients presenting with Raynaud's phenomenon. It can detect microvascular abnormalities before the onset of significant skin changes or internal organ involvement.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following medications has shown promising results in recent clinical trials for slowing the progression of SSc-associated interstitial lung disease (SSc-ILD)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. D-penicillamine has not shown consistent benefits in recent controlled trials for SSc-ILD.
• Option B. Mycophenolate mofetil is often used in SSc, but its primary role is in treating skin and other organ involvement, not specifically ILD.
• Option D. Cyclophosphamide is a potent immunosuppressant used for severe SSc manifestations, but it's not the first-line treatment for ILD.
• Educational Objective
• Nintedanib is an emerging treatment option for SSc-ILD, offering potential hope for patients with this debilitating complication of SSc.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements about pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) in systemic sclerosis (SSc) is TRUE?
options: PAH is a rare complication of SSc.
PAH is typically symptomatic early in its course.
PAH accounts ~30% of deaths in SSc.
The prognosis of PAH in SSc is generally favorable.

explanation.short: Ans. C) PAH accounts ~30% of deaths in SSc.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. PAH is a rare complication of SSc: PAH is not rare in SSc; it occurs in a significant minority of patients.
• Option B. PAH is typically symptomatic early in its course: PAH is often asymptomatic until advanced stages, making early detection crucial.
• Option D. The prognosis of PAH in SSc is generally favorable: The prognosis of PAH in SSc is poor without treatment.
• Educational Objective
• PAH is a common and serious complication of SSc. Recognizing the symptoms and initiating appropriate treatment can improve outcomes and prolong survival.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with scleroderma renal crisis (SRC) is found to have activation of the complement pathway on renal biopsy. Which of the following medications might be considered as an adjunct to ACE inhibitor therapy?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• The other medications are not specifically targeted towards complement inhibition.
• Educational Objective
• Eculizumab is a novel therapeutic option for SRC that targets the underlying pathophysiology of the disease. Further research is needed to determine its long-term efficacy and safety in this setting.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old woman presents with complaints of dry mouth, dry eyes, and fatigue. On examination, you note enlarged parotid glands and dental caries. Given the likely diagnosis, which of the following is the characteristic histopathological findings?
options: Deposition of immune complexes in blood vessels

explanation.short: Ans. C) Lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Granulomatous inflammation: Granulomatous inflammation is seen in sarcoidosis.
• Option B. Neutrophilic infiltration of exocrine glands: Neutrophilic infiltration is characteristic of acute infections.
• Option D. Deposition of immune complexes in blood vessels: Immune complex deposition is seen in vasculitis .
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the characteristic histopathological findings in Sjögren's syndrome is crucial for confirming the diagnosis. A lip biopsy is commonly performed to assess the minor salivary glands for lymphocytic infiltration.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old female is diagnosed with primary Sjögren Syndrome after presenting with dry mouth, difficulty swallowing, and bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Which of the following tests is most specific for diagnosing primary Sjögren Syndrome?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Anti-Ro/SSA and Anti-La/SSB antibodies
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rheumatoid factor (RF) can be positive in Sjögren Syndrome but is not specific as it can be positive in other autoimmune diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis.
• Option B. Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) can be positive in a wide range of autoimmune diseases and is not specific to Sjögren Syndrome.
• Option D. Anti-smooth muscle antibody (ASMA) is associated with autoimmune hepatitis and not specific for Sjögren Syndrome.
• Educational Objective
• While other tests like RF and ANA can be positive in Sjögren's syndrome, they are not as specific as anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. These antibodies, in combination with clinical features, are crucial for establishing the diagnosis of primary Sjögren's syndrome.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is the MOST common extraglandular manifestation in primary Sjögren's syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fatigue (25%),
• Option C. Raynaud's phenomenon (37%),
• Option D. lung involvemen t (14%) are less common.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the wide range of extraglandular manifestations in Sjögren's syndrome is important for comprehensive patient care. While the primary symptoms involve dryness of the eyes and mouth, other organs can be affected, with arthralgias/arthritis being the most prevalent.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with Sjögren's syndrome presents with palpable purpura on the lower extremities, arthralgia, and peripheral neuropathy. Which of the following is the most likely underlying pathophysiology?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. T-cell mediated cytotoxicity: T-cell mediated cytotoxicity is involved in other autoimmune diseases but not the primary mechanism in this scenario.
• Option C. Anti-Ro/SSA antibody-mediated damage : Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies are associated with Sjögren's but don't directly cause vasculitis.
• Option D. Granulomatous inflammation: Granulomatous inflammation is more characteristic of conditions like sarcoidosis.
• Educational Objective
• Cryoglobulinemia-associated vasculitis is a serious complication of Sjögren's syndrome. Recognizing the associated symptoms and understanding the underlying pathophysiology is crucial for timely diagnosis and appropriate management.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following autoantibodies is associated with an increased risk of multiple myeloma in Sjögren's syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• While the exact mechanism linking anti-Ro52 antibodies to multiple myeloma is not fully understood, this association highlights the importance of monitoring Sjögren's patients with these antibodies for potential hematological malignancies.

subject: Medicine
question: A 60-year-old man with a history of well-controlled hypertension on medication presents with worsening Raynaud's phenomenon over the past few months. He recently started taking a new medication for migraine prophylaxis. Which medication is most likely to be contributing to his worsening Raynaud's?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Calcium channel blockers: These are often used to treat Raynaud's phenomenon because they promote vasodilation and improve blood flow to the extremities.
• Option B. Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors: These medications can also help improve blood flow, but they are not as commonly used for Raynaud's as calcium channel blockers.
• Option D. Statins: Statins are used to lower cholesterol and are not known to worsen Raynaud's phenomenon.
• Educational Objective
• It is crucial to consider the potential impact of medications on Raynaud's phenomenon when managing patients with this condition. Beta blockers should be used with caution or avoided in individuals with Raynaud's, and alternative medications may need to be considered.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 35-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of episodic color changes in her fingers when exposed to cold temperatures. The episodes start with blanching, followed by cyanosis, and then redness as her fingers rewarm. Physical examination reveals the changes shown in the picture. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in differentiating primary from secondary raynaud’s?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/08/01/screenshot-2024-08-01-170358.png]
options: Order an antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and rheumatoid factor

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Reassurance and observation: While primary Raynaud's phenomenon is generally benign, it's crucial to rule out secondary causes, especially in patients with new-onset symptoms or those with additional features suggestive of a connective tissue disease.
• Option B. Initiate treatment with nifedipine: Nifedipine, a calcium channel blocker, can be helpful in managing Raynaud's phenomenon, but it's important to establish the diagnosis first.
• Option D. Order an antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and rheumatoid factor: These tests can help identify underlying autoimmune diseases, but nailfold capillaroscopy is a more specific and sensitive test for differentiating primary and secondary Raynaud's phenomenon.
• Educational Objective
• Nailfold capillaroscopy is a valuable diagnostic tool for distinguishing primary and secondary Raynaud's phenomenon. Early differentiation is essential for identifying and managing any underlying connective tissue disease that may be causing the secondary Raynaud's.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with Raynaud's phenomenon presents with severe digital ischemia and necrosis of the fingertips. Which of the following medications is most likely to be considered for treatment in this scenario?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Nifedipine, losartan, and sildenafil are used in milder cases or as preventive measures.
• Educational Objective
• Recognize that severe Raynaud's phenomenon requires aggressive treatment with potent vasodilators like iloprost to prevent tissue loss and improve outcomes.

subject: Medicine
question: A 40-year-old man with secondary Raynaud's phenomenon due to systemic sclerosis experiences severe episodes of digital ischemia, leading to fingertip ulcers. His condition has not improved with calcium channel blockers. Which of the following therapeutic options is most appropriate for managing his severe condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Thermal biofeedback: This non-pharmacological therapy can be helpful in mild to moderate Raynaud's, but it is unlikely to be sufficient for severe cases with digital ulcers.
• Option C. Topical nitroglycerin ointment: This medication is primarily used to improve local blood flow and may be beneficial in milder cases of Raynaud's, but its efficacy in severe cases with ulcers is limited.
• Option D. Cyclophosphamide: Cyclophosphamide is an immunosuppressant used for severe manifestations of systemic sclerosis, but it is not the first-line treatment for Raynaud's phenomenon.
• Educational Objective
• Understand that ERAs are effective in treating severe Raynaud's phenomenon, especially in the context of systemic sclerosis. They target the endothelin pathway, which plays a key role in vasoconstriction and vascular remodelling.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old male with a history of alcohol abuse presents with sudden onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling in his left big toe. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Rheumatoid arthritis : This is an autoimmune condition that typically affects multiple joints symmetrically (both sides of the body). It tends to develop gradually and doesn't usually cause sudden, severe pain in a single joint like gout does.
• Option C. Pseudogout: While similar to gout in terms of causing acute joint pain, pseudogout more commonly affects the knee rather than the big toe. Also, it's not as strongly associated with alcohol use as gout.
• Option D. Osteoarthritis: This is a degenerative joint disease that usually develops slowly over time. It can affect the big toe, but the onset is typically more gradual and less severe than the sudden pain seen in gout.
• Educational Objective:
• Gouty arthritis is a common inflammatory arthritis associated with alcohol use. It typically presents with sudden, severe pain, redness, and swelling in the big toe (podagra). Recognizing the classic presentation and risk factors can help guide appropriate diagnosis and treatment

subject: Medicine
question: A 30-year-old office worker complains of pain and stiffness in the neck and shoulders. She reports feeling exhausted and having trouble sleeping. Physical examination is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR): PMR typically affects older adults (over 50) and causes pain and stiffness in the shoulders and hips. It's also associated with elevated inflammatory markers, which this patient lacks.
• Option C. Rheumatoid arthritis (RA): RA is an autoimmune disease that causes joint inflammation. While it can cause pain and fatigue, it usually affects multiple joints and would likely show up on a physical exam.
• Option D. Osteoarthritis (OA): OA is a degenerative joint disease that usually affects weight-bearing joints like the knees and hips. While it can affect the neck, it's less common and usually occurs in older individuals with a history of joint wear and tear.
• Educational Objective:
• Fibromyalgia is a common chronic pain disorder affecting young to middle-aged women. It's characterized by widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and cognitive difficulties. The diagnosis is often made based on clinical presentation and the absence of other explanatory conditions.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with joint pain undergoes arthrocentesis. The synovial fluid appears clear and forms a long string when expressed from the syringe. Which of the following is the most likely interpretation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Septic arthritis: This is an infection within a joint. The synovial fluid would typically be cloudy or purulent (pus-like) due to the presence of bacteria and white blood cells. It would not form a long string due to decreased viscosity.
• Option C. Gout: Gout is caused by uric acid crystal deposition in the joints. While the synovial fluid can be inflammatory, it is not typically cloudy in the early stages. Urate crystals might be seen under a microscope, but the fluid would not necessarily lose its viscosity.
• Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis: This is an autoimmune disease causing chronic joint inflammation. Synovial fluid in rheumatoid arthritis can be cloudy and less viscous, but not always.
• Educational Objective:
• Normal synovial fluid is clear or pale yellow, with normal viscosity, and forms a string when expressed. Abnormal findings can indicate infection, inflammation, or other joint pathology.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient presents with a swollen, painful knee. Synovial fluid analysis reveals a cloudy, yellow fluid with a WBC count of 60,000/μL and a predominance of neutrophils. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Normal synovial fluid: Normal synovial fluid is clear or pale yellow, with a low white blood cell (WBC) count (<200/µL) and few neutrophils.
• Option B. Gout: While gout can cause inflammation in the joint, the synovial fluid typically contains urate crystals and a lower WBC count (usually under 50,000/µL) compared to septic arthritis.
• Option C. Osteoarthritis: Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease, and the synovial fluid is usually clear or slightly yellow with a low WBC count and few inflammatory cells.
• Educational Objective:
• Septic arthritis is a joint infection characterized by cloudy, yellow synovial fluid with a high WBC count and predominantly neutrophils. Prompt diagnosis and treatment are crucial to prevent joint damage and complications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 38-year-old woman presents with pain and swelling in multiple joints, including the wrists, hands (predominantly PIP/MTP joints, sparing DIP), and knees. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding her condition?
options: Her risk of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is increased if she has a history of smoking.
Her risk of RA is decreased if she has a family history of autoimmune disease.
RA affects men and women equally.
RA is primarily a disease of the elderly.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Her risk of rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is increased if she has a history of smoking.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Her risk of RA is decreased if she has a family history of autoimmune disease: A family history of autoimmune disease actually increases the risk of developing RA.
• Option C. RA affects men and women equally : RA is more common in women than men, with a ratio of approximately 3:1.
• Option D. RA is primarily a disease of the elderly : While RA can occur at any age, it most commonly begins between the ages of 30 and 60.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the risk factors for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is crucial for identifying individuals at higher risk and potentially implementing preventive measures or early interventions.

subject: Medicine
question: Which HLA-DRB1 allele is associated with the HIGHEST risk of developing rheumatoid arthritis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. *0101 : This is a moderate risk allele, not the highest.
• Option B. *0404 : This is also a moderate risk allele.
• Option D. *1001 : Another moderate risk allele
• Educational Objective:
• HLA-DRB1 alleles, particularly *0401, are important genetic risk factors for rheumatoid arthritis. Understanding the role of these alleles in RA development can help with risk assessment and potentially inform personalized treatment approaches in the future.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) presents with pain and swelling in the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints of their fingers. Which of the following statements is MOST likely true?
options: DIP joint involvement is a common initial manifestation of RA.
DIP joint involvement is rare in RA and should prompt consideration of other diagnoses.
DIP joint involvement in RA usually indicates coexisting osteoarthritis.
DIP joint involvement in RA typically responds poorly to standard RA treatment.

explanation.short: Ans. C) DIP joint involvement in RA usually indicates coexisting osteoarthritis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
