• Option A. DIP joint involvement is a common initial manifestation of RA: DIP involvement is less common than PIP/MCP in early RA.
• Option B. DIP joint involvement is rare in RA and should prompt consideration of other diagnoses: DIP involvement in RA can occur, but suggests possible co-existing osteoarthritis.
• Option D. DIP joint involvement in RA typically responds poorly to standard RA treatment: RA treatment should help DIP involvement if due to RA.
• Educational Objective
• DIP joint involvement in RA is less common than PIP and MCP joint involvement. It often suggests coexisting osteoarthritis.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is the most common flexor tendon to be affected by rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Options (A, B, C): While other flexor tendons (flexor digitorum superficialis, flexor digitorum profundus, flexor carpi radialis) can be involved in RA, the FPL is the most frequently affected .
• Educational Objective
• The flexor pollicis longus tendon is the most commonly affected flexor tendon in RA.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements about wrist involvement in RA is FALSE?
options: It often occurs early in the disease course.
It typically involves the radiocarpal and intercarpal joints.
It can lead to carpal tunnel syndrome.

explanation.short: Ans. C) It is always symmetrical.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It often occurs early in the disease course : It often occurs early: True.
• Option B. It typically involves the radiocarpal and intercarpal joints : It typically involves the radiocarpal and intercarpal joints: True.
• Option D. It can lead to carpal tunnel syndrome: It can lead to carpal tunnel syndrome: True due to synovial inflammation.
• Educational Objective
• While wrist involvement is common in RA, it is not always symmetrical, particularly in the early stages of the disease.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is false regarding extra-articular manifestation of RA?
options: Extra-articular manifestations may develop in 40% of patients.
Extra-articular manifestations will always occur after the onset of arthritis.
Patients most likely to develop extraarticular disease have a history of cigarette smoking.
~10% of patients with RA have secondary Sjögren’s syndrome

explanation.short: Ans. B) Extra-articular manifestations will always occur after the onset of arthritis.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Extra-articular manifestations may develop in 40% of patients: This is true.
• Option C. Patients most likely to develop extraarticular disease have a history of cigarette smoking: This is generally true, as smoking is a known risk factor for both RA and its extra-articular manifestations.
• Option D. ~10% of patients with RA have secondary Sjögren’s syndrome: This is also true. Secondary Sjögren's syndrome is a common extra-articular manifestation of RA, affecting approximately 10% of patients.
• Educational Objective
• EAMs are common in RA and can affect various organs. Recognizing them is crucial for early diagnosis and appropriate management to prevent complications.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is FALSE regarding Felty's syndrome?
options: It is a triad of neutropenia, splenomegaly, and nodular RA.
It typically occurs in the late stages of severe RA.
Its incidence is increasing with more aggressive RA treatment.
It is more common in whites than other racial groups.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Its incidence is increasing with more aggressive RA treatment.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It is a triad of neutropenia, splenomegaly, and nodular RA: This is the classic definition of Felty's syndrome. Neutropenia is a low count of neutrophils (a type of white blood cell), splenomegaly is an enlarged spleen, and nodular RA refers to the presence of rheumatoid nodules.
• Option B. It typically occurs in the late stages of severe RA: Felty's syndrome is usually seen in patients who have had RA for a long time and whose disease is not well controlled.
• Option D. It is more common in whites than other racial groups: While the exact reason is unknown, studies have shown that Felty's syndrome is more prevalent in white populations.
• Educational Objective
• Felty's syndrome is a rare but severe complication of RA. Recognizing its classic triad (neutropenia, splenomegaly, nodular RA) is crucial for timely intervention.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the treatment of Caplan's syndrome?
options: It primarily involves treating the underlying pneumoconiosis.
It requires aggressive immunosuppression to control the lung nodules.
It focuses on managing the rheumatoid arthritis with disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs).
There is no specific treatment for Caplan's syndrome.

explanation.short: Ans. C) It focuses on managing the rheumatoid arthritis with disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs (DMARDs).
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It primarily involves treating the underlying pneumoconiosis: Treating pneumoconiosis alone won't address the RA component.
• Option B. It requires aggressive immunosuppression to control the lung nodules : Aggressive immunosuppression is not typically required.
• Option D. There is no specific treatment for Caplan's syndrome : There is a specific treatment approach, focused on managing RA.
• Educational Objective
• Caplan's syndrome is a rare lung disease occurring in individuals with RA and exposure to dust. Treatment focuses on managing RA to improve both joint and lung outcomes.

subject: Medicine
question: What is the most common cause of death in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Infectious diseases: While RA patients may be at a slightly increased risk of infections due to immunosuppressive treatments, it is not the most common cause of death.
• Option B. Respiratory diseases: Respiratory complications can occur in RA, such as interstitial lung disease, but CVD is still a more significant risk factor.
• Option D. Cancer: Some studies suggest a slightly elevated cancer risk in RA, but it is not the primary cause of death.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the increased risk of CVD in RA is essential for implementing preventive strategies like lifestyle modifications and appropriate medication to reduce cardiovascular mortality.

subject: Medicine
question:
A patient's hand is shown in the picture below. Which of the following is the MOST LIKELY associated underlying condition?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/20/picture4_3vVtYeA.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Osteoarthritis: Can cause joint deformities, but they typically present as bony enlargements (Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes) rather than the specific swan neck configuration.
• Option C. Psoriatic arthritis: Can involve various joints, it doesn't typically cause the swan neck deformity.
• Option D. Gout: Usually affects the big toe and other joints, causing acute inflammatory attacks, but not the swan neck deformity.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the swan neck deformity is essential for identifying potential RA in patients presenting with hand deformities. It highlights the importance of a thorough assessment, including history, physical examination, and potentially laboratory and imaging studies, to confirm the diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question:
A clinical photograph shows a patient's hand with a visible deformity of the ring finger. There is no sign of trauma, and the patient has a known history of chronic synovitis. Which of the following conditions does this finger deformity represent?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/20/picture5_xQGW74n.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A . Swan neck deformity: Swan neck deformity is characterized by hyperextension of the PIP joint and flexion of the DIP joint, which is the opposite of the observed deformity.
• Option C . Trigger finger: Trigger finger involves the locking of a finger in a bent position, typically at the metacarpophalangeal joint, not the PIP joint.
• Option D . Jersey finger: Jersey finger is an injury to the flexor tendon of the finger, which usually presents with an inability to flex the DIP joint, contrasting with the hyperextension seen in this patient.
• Educational Objective:
• Boutonniere deformity is a common complication of chronic synovitis, often seen in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis. Recognizing this deformity can aid in diagnosing the underlying condition and guide appropriate treatment to prevent further joint damage and functional impairment.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding rheumatoid factor (RF) in rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
options: All RA patients have a positive RF test.
A positive RF test is diagnostic for RA.
A negative RF test rules out RA.
RF can be present in both healthy individuals and those with RA.

explanation.short: Ans. D) RF can be present in both healthy individuals and those with RA.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. All RA patients have a positive RF test: Not all RA patients have a positive RF test. Some patients have seronegative RA, meaning they test negative for both RF and anti-CCP antibodies.
• Option B. A positive RF test is diagnostic for RA: While a positive RF increases the risk of RA, it's not diagnostic on its own. Other conditions can cause elevated RF, and some RA patients are seronegative.
• Option C. A negative RF test rules out RA: A negative RF test doesn't necessarily rule out RA. Some patients have seronegative RA.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the limitations of RF testing is crucial for accurate RA diagnosis. A positive RF test should be interpreted in conjunction with clinical presentation, other lab tests (like anti-CCP antibodies), and imaging findings.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient presents with early morning stiffness and swelling in multiple small joints of the hands. Which of the following imaging modalities is MOST sensitive for detecting early synovitis in suspected rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Plain radiography (X-ray): X-rays are insensitive for detecting synovitis, as they primarily image bone and can miss soft tissue inflammation.
• Option B. Ultrasound : While ultrasound is a good option for visualizing synovitis, it may not be as sensitive as MRI for detecting very early inflammatory changes, especially in deeper joints.
• Option C. Computed tomography (CT) scan: CT scans are not routinely used for diagnosing RA due to higher radiation exposure compared to X-ray and MRI. They also may not be as sensitive for detecting synovitis as MRI.
• Educational Objective
• MRI is the preferred imaging modality for early detection of synovitis in RA. Early diagnosis and intervention are crucial for preventing joint damage and improving long-term outcomes in RA.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient newly diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is prescribed methotrexate. The patient asks when they can expect to see improvement in their symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Within a few days and Option B. Within 1-2 weeks: These time frames are too short for the onset of DMARDs' action.
• Option D. Within 6-12 months: This time frames is too long; improvement should be evident sooner
• Educational Objective
• Managing patient expectations regarding the onset of action of DMARDs is important for adherence and successful treatment. Patients should be counselled about the delayed onset of action and encouraged to continue therapy even if they don't experience immediate relief.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements regarding chronic low-dose prednisone therapy in rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is TRUE?
options: It should be the first-line treatment for all RA patients.
It is not associated with any long-term complications.
It should be avoided whenever possible due to the risk of osteoporosis and other side effects.
It is safe to abruptly discontinue prednisone without tapering.

explanation.short: Ans. C) It should be avoided whenever possible due to the risk of osteoporosis and other side effects.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It should be the first-line treatment for all RA patients : Prednisone is not a first-line treatment, as other DMARDs are preferred initially.
• Option B. It is not associated with any long-term complications : It is associated with numerous long-term complications.
• Option D. It is safe to abruptly discontinue prednisone without tapering : Abrupt discontinuation can lead to adrenal insufficiency.
• Educational Objective
• While low-dose prednisone can provide rapid symptomatic relief in RA, its long-term use should be minimized due to potential complications. It is often used as a bridge therapy while waiting for DMARDs to take effect or as an adjunctive treatment for flares.

subject: Medicine
question: A 28-year-old male with early ankylosing spondylitis (AS) and evidence of sacroiliitis on MRI is considering treatment options. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding the potential benefits of early anti-TNF therapy in this patient?
options: Anti-TNF therapy is contraindicated in early AS due to the risk of exacerbating spinal fusion.
Early initiation of anti-TNF therapy may decrease the risk of spinal fusion in the long term.
Anti-TNF therapy should only be considered after spinal fusion has occurred.
Anti-TNF therapy has no impact on the progression of spinal fusion in AS.

explanation.short: Ans. B) Early initiation of anti-TNF therapy may decrease the risk of spinal fusion in the long term.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Anti-TNF therapy is contraindicated in early AS due to the risk of exacerbating spinal fusion : Anti-TNF therapy is not contraindicated; it might actually be beneficial in preventing fusion.
• Option C. Anti-TNF therapy should only be considered after spinal fusion has occurred: Early intervention is preferred to prevent irreversible damage.
• Option D. Anti-TNF therapy has no impact on the progression of spinal fusion in AS: Anti-TNF therapy does have an impact on slowing down structural damage.
• Educational Objective
• Early intervention with anti-TNF therapy is crucial in AS to prevent irreversible structural damage and maintain spinal flexibility.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old male presents with stiffness and mild pain in his thoracic spine. Radiographs reveal flowing ossification along the anterior vertebral bodies, with preserved intervertebral disc spaces. Sacroiliac and facet joints appear normal. Which of the following is the MOST likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Ankylosing spondylitis : Typically involves sacroiliac joints and can lead to fusion of the spine.
• Option B. Osteoarthritis : Affects the joints primarily, not the ligaments.
• Option D. Rheumatoid arthritis : An inflammatory arthritis that can affect the spine but not in this characteristic pattern.
• Educational Objective
• DISH is a common condition in older adults characterized by bony outgrowths in the spine. Recognizing its typical radiographic features is essential for accurate diagnosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with AS planned for etanercept therapy, should be screened for which of the following conditions BEFORE initiating treatment?

explanation.short: Ans. D) All of the above
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Anti-TNF therapy can reactivate latent infections like TB and worsen hepatitis or HIV, so screening is crucial.
• Educational Objective
• Screening for infections before initiating anti-TNF therapy is crucial for patient safety and preventing complications.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding the relationship between psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis (PsA)?
options: All patients with psoriasis will eventually develop PsA.
PsA always develops before the onset of psoriasis.
Up to 30% of patients with psoriasis may develop PsA.
Psoriasis and PsA are caused by different underlying mechanisms.

explanation.short: Ans. C) Up to 30% of patients with psoriasis may develop PsA.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. All patients with psoriasis will eventually develop PsA: Not all psoriasis patients develop PsA.
• Option B. PsA always develops before the onset of psoriasis: PsA can develop before, after, or concurrently with psoriasis.
• Option D. Psoriasis and PsA are caused by different underlying mechanisms : Both conditions share similar underlying inflammatory pathways.
• Educational Objective
• Psoriasis and PsA are linked, but not everyone with psoriasis will develop PsA. Recognizing the potential for PsA is crucial for early diagnosis and intervention to prevent joint damage.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with psoriatic arthritis (PsA) is found to have elevated serum levels of RANKL. Which of the following is the most likely consequence of this finding?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Decreased bone resorption : RANKL promotes, not decreases, bone resorption.
• Option B. Increased bone formation : RANKL primarily affects osteoclasts, not osteoblasts.
• Option D. Decreased inflammation : RANKL is pro-inflammatory and can exacerbate the inflammatory process.
• Educational Objective
• RANKL plays a crucial role in bone remodeling and is implicated in the pathogenesis of PsA. Understanding its role can lead to the development of targeted therapies aimed at inhibiting RANKL to prevent joint damage.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding arthritis mutilans in psoriatic arthritis (PsA)?
options: It is a common finding in most PsA patients.
It typically occurs early in the disease course.
It is associated with a good prognosis.
It can lead to significant functional impairment.

explanation.short: Ans. D) It can lead to significant functional impairment.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. It is a common finding in most PsA patients: It is a rare and severe form of PsA.
• Option B. It typically occurs early in the disease course: It usually occurs in long-standing, poorly controlled PsA.
• Option C. It is associated with a good prognosis: It has a poor prognosis due to its destructive nature.
• Educational Objective
• Arthritis mutilans is a rare but devastating complication of PsA. Early recognition and aggressive treatment are essential to preserve joint function and quality of life.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following best describes the structure of infliximab?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Fully human monoclonal antibody : These are entirely derived from human sources.
• Option B. Fully mouse monoclonal antibody : These are entirely derived from mouse sources and can trigger immune reactions in humans.
• Option D. Humanized monoclonal antibody : These have a higher proportion of human components than chimeric antibodies.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the structure of monoclonal antibodies is crucial for comprehending their mechanism of action and potential for adverse effects. Chimeric antibodies offer a balance between efficacy and reduced immunogenicity compared to fully mouse antibodies

subject: Medicine
question: In a direct comparison study for the treatment of PsA, the efficacy of tofacitinib was found to be:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Options A. Superior to adalimumab & B. Inferior to adalimumab suggest a difference in efficacy, which is not supported by evidence.
• Option D . Not comparable to adalimumab suggests no comparison was made, which is incorrect.
• Educational Objective
• Both tofacitinib (a Janus kinase inhibitor) and adalimumab (a TNF inhibitor) are effective treatment options for PsA. The choice between them may depend on individual patient factors, comorbidities, and preferences.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old male presents with acute onset asymmetric oligoarthritis following a gastrointestinal infection. He is HLA-B27 positive. Synovial fluid analysis is most likely to reveal:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Monosodium urate crystals are characteristic of gout,
• Option B. Calcium pyrophosphate crystals of pseudogout, and
• Option D. Septic arthritis involves infection of the joint.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the typical synovial fluid findings in reactive arthritis helps differentiate it from septic arthritis (where bacteria are present in the joint) and crystal-induced arthritis (where crystals are found in the fluid).

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following skin manifestations is most specific for reactive arthritis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Psoriasis: While psoriasis can occur in patients with ReA, it is not specific to this condition and has a distinct pathophysiology unrelated to ReA.
• Option C. Erythema nodosum: Erythema nodosum is a panniculitis (inflammation of subcutaneous fat) that can be triggered by various infections and inflammatory conditions, including ReA. However, it is not specific to ReA.
• Option D. Erythema multiforme: Erythema multiforme is a hypersensitivity reaction often triggered by infections (e.g., herpes simplex virus) or medications. While it can occasionally occur in the context of ReA, it is not a specific manifestation of this condition.
• Educational Objective
• Keratoderma blennorrhagica is a hallmark feature of reactive arthritis and can aid in diagnosis, especially when accompanied by other characteristic symptoms like arthritis, urethritis, and conjunctivitis.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for acute reactive arthritis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Methotrexate: Methotrexate is a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) used for chronic inflammatory arthritis like rheumatoid arthritis. It is not typically used as the initial treatment for acute reactive arthritis due to its delayed onset of action and potential side effects.
• Option B. Sulfasalazine: Sulfasalazine is another DMARD with anti-inflammatory properties. While it may be considered in cases of persistent ReA that do not respond to NSAIDs, it is not the first-line treatment due to its slower onset of action and potential gastrointestinal side effects.
• Option D. Intra-articular corticosteroids: These are steroid injections directly into the affected joint. While they can be effective in reducing inflammation and pain in a specific joint, they are not the initial treatment of choice for ReA, as the condition often affects multiple joints and may resolve spontaneously or with NSAIDs.
• Educational Objective
• Prompt initiation of high-dose NSAIDs is crucial for managing acute reactive arthritis and minimizing joint damage. If NSAIDs are ineffective or contraindicated, other options like intra-articular corticosteroids or DMARDs may be considered.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with Crohn's disease presents with acute hip pain and limited range of motion. Radiographs show severe joint space narrowing and osteolysis of the femoral head. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Isolated destructive hip arthritis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Osteoarthritis (OA): While OA is a common cause of hip pain and joint space narrowing, it typically presents with gradual onset, and radiographs show osteophyte formation (bone spurs) rather than osteolysis (bone destruction). The acute presentation and destructive pattern seen in this patient make OA less likely.
• Option B. Avascular necrosis (AVN): AVN of the femoral head can also cause hip pain and radiographic changes, but it often presents with a subacute or chronic course. Furthermore, the radiographic findings in AVN usually include sclerosis (increased bone density) and subchondral collapse, which are not as prominent in this case.
• Option C. Septic arthritis: Septic arthritis is an infection of the joint that can cause acute pain and joint destruction. However, it is usually accompanied by systemic symptoms like fever and elevated inflammatory markers. Additionally, joint aspiration would reveal purulent fluid with positive cultures, which is not the case here.
• Educational Objective
• It's important to be aware of isolated destructive hip arthritis as a potential complication in Crohn's disease patients, as it requires prompt recognition and specific management strategies to prevent further joint damage and disability.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following skin manifestations is MOST characteristic of SAPHO syndrome?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The other options are not typically associated with SAPHO syndrome (syndrome of synovitis, acne, pustulosis, hyperostosis, and osteitis), although they can be seen in other inflammatory conditions.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing palmoplantar pustulosis as a key feature of SAPHO syndrome can aid in the diagnosis and guide appropriate management of this complex inflammatory disorder.

subject: Medicine
question: A 50-year-old man presents with sudden onset of severe pain and swelling in his right great toe at 3 AM. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Rheumatoid arthritis typically presents with symmetric polyarthritis and morning stiffness.
• Option B. Septic arthritis would usually be accompanied by fever and systemic signs of infection.
• Option C. Pseudogout can mimic gout but is less likely to involve the great toe.
• Educational Objective
• Acute gouty arthritis is a common inflammatory arthritis characterized by sudden onset severe pain in a single joint, often the great toe. Recognizing the classic presentation and risk factors can help guide appropriate diagnosis and treatment.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient presents with acute pain and swelling in the knee, and synovial fluid analysis reveals rhomboid-shaped crystals with positive birefringence under polarized light microscopy. Which of the following genetic mutations is most likely associated with this condition?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. PRPP synthetase mutation: This mutation is associated with increased uric acid production and is a known cause of gout, not pseudogout. Gout typically presents with needle-shaped crystals with negative birefringence under polarized light microscopy.
• Option B. HPRT mutation: This mutation leads to Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, a rare genetic disorder characterized by hyperuricemia and neurological symptoms. While Lesch-Nyhan syndrome can cause gout, it's not directly linked to pseudogout or CPPD crystal formation.
• Option D. COL2A1 mutation: This mutation is associated with various skeletal dysplasias, including some types of osteoarthritis. While it can affect joint health, it doesn't directly cause pseudogout or CPPD crystal deposition.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the genetic basis of pseudogout is crucial for accurate diagnosis and the development of targeted therapies. Identifying the ANKH mutation in patients with pseudogout can also help assess the risk of familial transmission and guide genetic counseling.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with a history of gout presents with an acute flare. He is currently taking clarithromycin for a respiratory infection. Which of the following medications should be used with caution in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The other medications are not significantly affected by CYP3A4 interactions.
• Educational Objective
• Be aware of potential drug interactions when prescribing colchicine, especially with medications that affect CYP3A4 metabolism. Dose adjustments or alternative medications may be necessary to avoid adverse effects.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with recurrent gout flares has failed to achieve target serum urate levels with allopurinol alone. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Increasing allopurinol dose might be an option, but the patient may have already reached the maximum tolerated dose. Switching to febuxostat or considering pegloticase are options for patients who do not tolerate or respond to allopurinol/probenecid combination.
• Educational Objective
• Understand the various treatment options for gout and the rationale for combining different medications to achieve target serum urate levels. While increasing the allopurinol dose may be considered, it's important to balance efficacy with potential side effects and consider the maximum tolerated dose. Switching to febuxostat or pegloticase might be considered in patients who don't tolerate or respond to allopurinol/probenecid combination.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with gout and stage 3 chronic kidney disease tests positive for the HLA-B*5801 allele. Which of the following is the most appropriate urate-lowering therapy for this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Allopurinol at a reduced dose: Allopurinol should be avoided in patients with HLA-B*5801 positivity due to the risk of SCARs.
• Option B. Probenecid: Probenecid is not recommended in patients with CKD due to the risk of kidney stones.
• Option D. Colchicine: Colchicine is not a urate-lowering therapy but is used for acute gout flare management.
• Educational Objective
• Patients with gout and HLA-B*5801 positivity should be screened before starting allopurinol due to the risk of SCARs. Febuxostat is a safe and effective alternative in these patients, even in the presence of CKD.

subject: Medicine
question: What is the pathophysiology behind paradoxical gout flares?
options: Decreased renal excretion of uric acid
Inflammatory reaction to MSU crystals shed from dissolving tophi

explanation.short: Ans. C) Inflammatory reaction to MSU crystals shed from dissolving tophi
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The other options are not the primary mechanisms behind paradoxical flares.
• Educational Objective
• Paradoxical gout flares are a common complication of ULT initiation. They are usually self-limited and can be managed with prophylactic anti-inflammatory medications. Recognizing this phenomenon can help healthcare providers explain it to patients and ensure proper management during ULT initiation.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following genetic polymorphisms is most strongly associated with an increased risk of osteoarthritis (OA)?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. HLA-B27: This gene is primarily associated with ankylosing spondylitis and other inflammatory conditions, not osteoarthritis.
• Option C. NOD2: This gene is associated with Crohn's disease, an inflammatory bowel disease, and not directly linked to osteoarthritis.
• Option D. PTPN22: This gene is associated with autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis and type 1 diabetes, but not with osteoarthritis.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the genetic factors that contribute to OA susceptibility can help identify individuals at higher risk and potentially lead to the development of personalized prevention and treatment strategies.

subject: Medicine
question: The presence of osteophytes in a joint is most indicative of which stage of osteoarthritis?
options: Osteophytes are not specific to any stage of OA

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• While osteophytes may be present in earlier stages, they are more prominent and widespread in late-stage OA.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the radiographic features of OA, such as osteophytes, can help clinicians determine the stage and severity of the disease, which can guide treatment decisions.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following interventions is most likely to result in sustained weight loss and improvement in knee OA symptoms in the long term?
options: A very low-calorie diet for rapid weight loss
A combination of diet, exercise, and behavioral therapy

explanation.short: Ans. B) A combination of diet, exercise, and behavioral therapy
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. A very low-calorie diet for rapid weight loss: Very low-calorie diets are often difficult to sustain and may not be safe for everyone.
• Option C. Over-the-counter weight loss supplements: Weight loss supplements have limited efficacy and potential side effects.
• Option D. Liposuction: Liposuction is not a sustainable weight loss solution and does not address the underlying lifestyle factors contributing to OA.
• Educational Objective
• Sustainable weight loss and lifestyle modifications are crucial for managing knee OA. A comprehensive approach involving diet, exercise, and behavioral changes is most likely to result in long-term improvement in symptoms and joint health.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is the most common form of bursitis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• The other options represent different types of bursitis that occur in other locations in the body (elbow, knee, and hip, respectively).
• Educational Objective
• Bursitis is the inflammation of a bursa, a fluid-filled sac that cushions and reduces friction between bones, tendons, and muscles near joints. Subacromial bursitis is the most common type, typically affecting the shoulder due to overuse or injury.

subject: Medicine
question:
A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive muscle weakness, difficulty swallowing, and a persistent skin rash. She reports feeling tired and experiencing muscle aches. Physical examination reveals erythematous papules and plaques over the knuckles and elbows. Additionally, she has a purplish rash on her eyelids and periorbital edema. On examination patient's hands showing erythematous papules and plaques over the knuckles (as shown in the image below): Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/07/30/screenshot-2024-07-30-120241.png]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE): While SLE can present with muscle weakness and skin rash, the pattern of skin involvement and presence of Gottron sign are more characteristic of dermatomyositis.
• Option B. Psoriasis: Psoriasis typically presents with well-demarcated, silvery plaques on extensor surfaces, not the violaceous rash seen in this patient.
• Option D. Scleroderma: Scleroderma is characterized by skin thickening and fibrosis, not the muscle weakness and specific rash seen in this case.
• Educational Objective
• Recognize the key clinical features of dermatomyositis, including Gottron's papules and heliotrope rash, as these are important for early diagnosis and prompt initiation of treatment.

subject: Medicine
question: In adult-onset dermatomyositis, the approximate risk of developing a malignancy within the first 2-3 years is:

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. 1% : This underestimates the risk of malignancy in adult-onset dermatomyositis.
• Option B. 5% : While a significant risk, this is lower than the actual estimated risk.
• Option D. 30% : This overestimates the risk of malignancy in adult-onset dermatomyositis.
• Educational Objective
• Be aware of the increased risk of malignancy in adult-onset dermatomyositis and consider appropriate screening and surveillance for these patients.

subject: Medicine
question: The predominant inflammatory cell infiltrate in the muscle tissue of dermatomyositis patients is composed of:
options: Macrophages, B cells, and plasmacytoid dendritic cells (PDCs)

explanation.short: Ans. C) Macrophages, B cells, and plasmacytoid dendritic cells (PDCs)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Neutrophils : Not the predominant cell type
• Option B. Eosinophils : Not the predominant cell type
• Option D. Lymphocytes and plasma cells : While present, not the most prominent cell types
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the immune cell composition in dermatomyositis can help elucidate the underlying pathophysiology of the disease and guide the development of targeted therapies.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following is NOT associated with a favorable prognosis in dermatomyositis?

explanation.short: Ans. C) Presence of interstitial lung disease (ILD)
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Early diagnosis and treatment: Early diagnosis and aggressive treatment can significantly improve outcomes in dermatomyositis.
• Option B. Young age: Younger patients generally have a better prognosis than older patients.
• Option D. Presence of anti-Mi-2 antibodies: The presence of anti-Mi-2 antibodies is often associated with a milder disease course and better response to treatment.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the negative impact of ILD on the prognosis of dermatomyositis highlights the importance of early detection and aggressive management of this complication.

subject: Medicine
question: Polymyositis (PM) is best characterized by which of the following clinical presentations?
options: Asymmetric distal muscle weakness with rapid progression
Symmetric proximal muscle weakness with gradual progression over weeks to months
Painless muscle atrophy with sensory loss
Focal muscle involvement with cranial nerve palsies

explanation.short: Ans. B) Symmetric proximal muscle weakness with gradual progression over weeks to months
explanation.detail:
• Explanation :
• Option A. Asymmetric distal muscle weakness with rapid progression : This is more suggestive of other neuromuscular disorders like Guillain-Barré syndrome.
• Option C. Painless muscle atrophy with sensory loss : This pattern is more indicative of neuropathic conditions.
• Option D. Focal muscle involvement with cranial nerve palsies : This might be seen in certain myopathies, but not the typical presentation of polymyositis.
• Educational Objective :
• Recognizing the classic presentation of PM is crucial for timely diagnosis and appropriate management. Distinguishing PM from other neuromuscular disorders with similar symptoms can guide further diagnostic testing and treatment decisions.

subject: Medicine
question: A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive muscle weakness, particularly in her thighs and shoulders. She has difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects. She also reports a persistent cough and shortness of breath on exertion. Additionally, she experiences joint pain and swelling in her hands and knees. Given the likely diagnosis, Which is the most common aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (ARS) antibody found in this patients?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Anti-PL-7, B. Anti-PL-12 and C. Anti-OJ are other ARS antibodies but are less common than anti-Jo-1.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the association between anti-Jo-1 antibodies and the clinical triad of myositis, ILD, and arthritis is crucial for accurate diagnosis and management of anti-synthetase syndrome, a subtype of inflammatory myopathy. This syndrome often requires aggressive immunosuppressive therapy to control both the muscle and lung disease.

subject: Medicine
question: In patients with Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM), the average time from symptom onset to requiring a wheelchair or scooter is approximately:

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the natural history and progression of IBM is important for patient counselling and planning for future care needs.

subject: Medicine
question: A 62-year-old man presents with gradual onset of difficulty gripping objects and rising from a chair. He also reports occasional choking when swallowing. Which of the following diagnoses is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS): While ALS can cause muscle weakness, it typically presents with a combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs (e.g., spasticity, hyperreflexia) and does not usually involve dysphagia early in the disease course.
• Option B. Polymyositis (PM): PM usually presents with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, rather than the asymmetric pattern of weakness often seen in IBM.
• Option D. Myasthenia Gravis: Myasthenia Gravis typically causes fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. It does not typically cause the pattern of muscle weakness seen in IBM.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the typical clinical presentation of IBM is essential for early diagnosis and appropriate management. IBM can be easily misdiagnosed as other neuromuscular disorders, so a high index of suspicion is necessary.

subject: Medicine
question: Which of the following staining techniques is most sensitive for detecting inclusions in Inclusion Body Myositis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Congo red stain: While Congo red is used to detect amyloid deposits, it is not the most sensitive method for visualizing inclusions in IBM, especially in paraffin-embedded sections. The inclusions in IBM often do not stain well with Congo red due to their specific protein composition.
• Option B. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain: H&E is a standard stain for routine histological examination. It can visualize the general architecture of the muscle tissue and some inclusions, but it lacks the specificity and sensitivity of immunostaining for p62.
• Option C. Periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) stain: PAS stain is used to detect glycogen and other carbohydrates. While it can highlight some inclusions in IBM, it is not as specific or sensitive as p62 immunostaining for detecting the beta-sheet misfolded proteins that are characteristic of these inclusions.
• Educational Objective:
• Immunostaining for p62 is a valuable diagnostic tool for confirming the presence of characteristic inclusions in IBM muscle biopsies. It is more sensitive and specific than other staining techniques like Congo red, H&E, or PAS.

subject: Medicine
question: What is the recommended approach for patients with known TPMT deficiency who require azathioprine therapy?
options: Administer the standard dose of azathioprine
Avoid azathioprine completely and use an alternative immunosuppressant
Start azathioprine at a significantly reduced dose
Begin with a standard dose and monitor closely for adverse effects

explanation.short: Ans. C) Start azathioprine at a significantly reduced dose
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective
• It is crucial to test for TPMT deficiency before initiating azathioprine therapy. If deficiency is detected, the dose must be significantly reduced to avoid serious complications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 58-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive proximal muscle weakness, worsened after statin use. Examination reveals no rash. Labs show elevated creatine kinase (CK). Muscle biopsy demonstrates necrotic muscle fibers without significant inflammation. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Polymyositis: Typically presents with symmetrical proximal weakness but would show inflammatory infiltrates on muscle biopsy.
• Option B. Dermatomyositis: Similar to polymyositis but with characteristic skin findings (absent in this case).
• Option D. Myasthenia Gravis: Presents with fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Muscle biopsy would not show necrosis.
• Educational Objective
• Necrotizing autoimmune myopathy (NAM) is a rare subtype of autoimmune myopathies characterized by progressive muscle weakness, elevated creatine kinase, and muscle biopsy showing muscle fiber necrosis with minimal inflammation. Statin use can be a trigger. Treatment involves immunosuppressive medications.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on corticosteroids for 6 months presents with new-onset proximal muscle weakness and fatigue. Physical examination confirms the muscle weakness. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE): While SLE can cause muscle weakness, it is usually accompanied by other systemic symptoms like rash, joint pain, and fever.
• Option B. Polymyalgia rheumatica: Typically affects older individuals and causes pain and stiffness in the shoulders and hips, not weakness.
• Option D. Hypothyroid myopathy: Can cause muscle weakness, but it is usually associated with other signs of hypothyroidism, such as fatigue, cold intolerance, and constipation. This patient's symptoms developed after starting corticosteroids, making steroid myopathy more likely.
• Educational Objective
• Be aware of the potential for steroid myopathy in patients taking corticosteroids. It is important to monitor for muscle weakness and consider dose reduction or alternative therapies if this side effect develops.

subject: Medicine
question: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of widespread pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping. She also reports experiencing headaches, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) symptoms, and anxiety. Physical examination is unremarkable except for multiple tender points. Given her symptoms and physical examination findings, which group of conditions is most likely to include her diagnosis?

explanation.short: Ans. B) Chronic Overlapping Pain Syndromes
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Chronic Fatigue Syndromes: While fatigue is a prominent feature in fibromyalgia, the presence of widespread pain and other symptoms differentiates it from chronic fatigue syndrome, which primarily focuses on debilitating fatigue.
• Option C. Chronic Regional Pain Syndromes: These syndromes involve pain localized to a specific region of the body, unlike the widespread pain seen in this patient.
• Option D. Chronic Inflammatory Syndromes: Although inflammation might play a role in some COPS conditions, it's not the defining characteristic. Fibromyalgia, for example, is not primarily an inflammatory disorder.
• Educational Objective
• Recognizing the concept of COPS is important for understanding the complex interplay of various chronic pain conditions and their potential shared pathophysiological mechanisms. This can help guide a more holistic approach to diagnosis and management.

subject: Medicine
question: A 55-year-old man with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents with proteinuria and peripheral edema. Renal biopsy reveals amyloid deposits. Which type of amyloidosis is most likely in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. AL Amyloidosis : Associated with plasma cell dyscrasias.
• Option C. Aβ Amyloidosis : Associated with Alzheimer's disease and deposits in the brain.
• Option D. ATTR Amyloidosis : Primarily affects the heart and nerves.
• Educational Objective :
• Recognizing the association between chronic inflammatory diseases and AA amyloidosis is crucial for early diagnosis and intervention to prevent irreversible organ damage.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with long-standing multiple myeloma presents with nephrotic syndrome and macroglossia (enlarged tongue). Which of the following types of amyloidosis is most likely?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. AA Amyloidosis: This type is associated with chronic inflammatory conditions (like rheumatoid arthritis or Crohn's disease) and typically affects the kidneys, liver, and spleen. While it can cause nephrotic syndrome, macroglossia is less common.
• Option C. Aβ Amyloidosis: This type is associated with Alzheimer's disease and predominantly affects the brain. It does not typically cause systemic manifestations like nephrotic syndrome or macroglossia.
• Option D. ATTR Amyloidosis: This type can be hereditary or wild-type (age-related) and primarily affects the heart and nerves. It can also affect the kidneys and gastrointestinal tract, but macroglossia is not a typical feature.
• Educational Objective:
• Identifying AL amyloidosis as a potential complication of multiple myeloma is essential for prompt diagnosis and treatment to prevent organ damage and improve patient outcomes.

subject: Medicine
question: Which diagnostic test is considered the gold standard for confirming the presence of amyloid deposits in tissues?
options: Congo red staining of biopsy tissue

explanation.short: Ans. B) Congo red staining of biopsy tissue
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP): SPEP can detect abnormal proteins in the blood, which may suggest amyloidosis. However, it does not directly visualize or confirm the presence of amyloid deposits in tissues.
• Option C. Bone marrow biopsy: Bone marrow biopsy is helpful in diagnosing AL amyloidosis, where amyloid deposits originate from light chains produced by plasma cells. However, it's not the gold standard for all types of amyloidosis.
• Option D. Echocardiogram: While an echocardiogram can reveal structural and functional abnormalities in the heart suggestive of amyloidosis (especially ATTR amyloidosis), it does not directly confirm the presence of amyloid deposits. A biopsy is still needed for definitive diagnosis.
• Educational Objective
• Understanding the role of Congo red staining in the diagnosis of amyloidosis is crucial for accurate identification and classification of this disease. It allows for confirmation of amyloid deposits in various tissues and helps distinguish different types of amyloidosis.

subject: Medicine
question: A 65-year-old patient with ATTR amyloid cardiomyopathy and New York Heart Association (NYHA) class III heart failure is started on tafamidis. Which of the following is the most realistic expectation of treatment?
options: Complete reversal of heart failure symptoms
Stabilization of disease progression and improved quality of life

explanation.short: Ans. C) Stabilization of disease progression and improved quality of life
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Complete reversal of heart failure symptoms: Unlikely due to existing heart damage.
• Option B. Regression of cardiac hypertrophy: Tafamidis doesn't reverse thickening.
• Educational Objective
• Tafamidis is a valuable therapeutic option for ATTR amyloid cardiomyopathy, but it's important to understand its limitations. It's not a cure but can offer significant benefits in terms of slowing disease progression and improving quality of life.
